summary: Static Application Security Testing (SAST) is a toolkit of self-contained, zero-dependency Python scanners that hunt source-code vulnerabilities across five language ecosystems - Java, PHP, Python, MERN/Node.js, and AI/LLM applications - plus a live Microsoft 365 + Entra ID posture scanner. Every scanner is a single .py file that runs on stock Python 3.8+ with no pip install, no agents, and no cloud back-end, so a security engineer can drop one file onto a build box or into a CI job and start scanning immediately.

Under the hood the toolkit ships roughly 257 regex-driven detection rules and 97 dependency-CVE signatures, all tagged with CWE identifiers and paired with concrete remediation advice. It goes beyond classic injection and crypto checks: the Java scanner unpacks WAR/JAR/EAR archives and flags Log4Shell, Spring4Shell, Struts and Shiro-550; the Python and MERN scanners cover framework misconfigurations and secrets; and a dedicated OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) scanner catches modern AI failure modes like prompt injection, excessive agency (ShellTool / PythonREPLTool), unsafe LLM-output handling, and unbounded token consumption.

Rounding out the toolkit, the Microsoft 365 + Entra ID SSPM scanner makes live Microsoft Graph API calls under an OAuth2 client-credentials flow to audit 50 real configuration checks - Security Defaults, Conditional Access, MFA, PIM/privileged access, app-registration hygiene, guest access, Exchange/SharePoint/Teams, and Identity Protection - producing the same console/JSON output as the SAST scanners plus a self-contained HTML report.

It is built for AppSec engineers, DevSecOps teams, and consultants who want a fast, auditable, dependency-free way to gate merges (exit code 1 on any CRITICAL/HIGH finding) and to bring both traditional and AI-era code under one consistent scanning model.

features.title: Five-ecosystem SAST coverage
features.description: One toolkit scans Java, PHP, Python, MERN/Node.js (JS/TS), and AI/LLM code with language-specific rule sets rather than a lowest-common-denominator regex pass.

features.title: OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) scanner
features.description: A dedicated engine maps 69 rules across all ten LLM categories - prompt injection, sensitive-info disclosure, supply chain, excessive agency, improper output handling, and unbounded consumption.

features.title: Dependency CVE / software-composition analysis
features.description: Parses pom.xml, build.gradle, package.json, requirements.txt, Pipfile, and pyproject.toml against 97 version-ranged CVE signatures including Log4Shell, Spring4Shell, Struts and Shiro-550.

features.title: Microsoft 365 + Entra ID SSPM
features.description: Makes live Microsoft Graph API calls to evaluate 50 real tenant checks across MFA, Conditional Access, privileged access, guest sharing, and Exchange/SharePoint/Teams posture.

features.title: AI / agentic-specific Python detections
features.description: Flags LangChain allow_dangerous_deserialization, ShellTool/PythonREPLTool, LLM output piped to eval/exec, and leaked sk- / cloud API keys that generic scanners miss.

features.title: Archive-aware Java scanning
features.description: Unpacks WAR/JAR/EAR archives to inspect nested JARs, pom.properties, web.xml servlet descriptors, and Spring Boot .properties/.yml misconfigurations.

features.title: Zero-dependency single-file design
features.description: Each SAST scanner is one standalone Python file using only the standard library, so it drops into any build box, container, or air-gapped host with no install step.

features.title: CI/CD exit-code gating
features.description: Returns exit code 1 whenever a CRITICAL or HIGH finding is present, letting pipelines fail a build or block a merge automatically.

features.title: CWE-tagged findings with remediation
features.description: Every finding carries a CWE ID (53 distinct weaknesses covered), file/line reference, and specific fix guidance citing NIST SP 800-131A/800-63B where relevant.

features.title: Multi-format reporting
features.description: Colour-coded ANSI console output, machine-readable JSON for pipelines, and self-contained dark-themed HTML reports (SSPM) with filterable severity tables.

features.title: Config & secret misconfiguration checks
features.description: Dedicated rules for php.ini, .env files, and Spring Boot config catch exposed debug modes, wildcard CORS, weak secrets, and embedded database credentials.

features.title: Severity filtering
features.description: A shared --severity flag on every scanner shows only findings at or above CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/INFO for triage-friendly output.

app-security-scanner.architecture_summary: Each scanner is a self-contained single Python file that follows one identical pipeline: module-level rule dictionaries feed a path walker and file dispatcher, which routes each file to a per-line regex SAST scan and a manifest version-range CVE analyzer; matches become Finding objects (memory-efficient via __slots__) that pass through a severity filter into console/JSON/HTML reporting with exit-code gating. The Microsoft 365 SSPM scanner reuses the same Finding/report layers but swaps the file-walker front-end for a live Microsoft Graph API collector authenticated with OAuth2 client credentials.

architecture_layers.name: Rule engine (module-level dicts)
architecture_layers.description: Each scanner declares rule lists such as SAST_RULES, SPRING_RULES, PHP_INI_RULES, ENV_RULES and LLM_*_RULES, where every rule is a dict of id, category, severity, regex pattern, CWE, description, and recommendation.

architecture_layers.name: File dispatcher
architecture_layers.description: scan_path walks directories (honouring SKIP_DIRS like node_modules/.git/target), and _dispatch_file routes each file by extension to the matching language scanner or manifest handler.

architecture_layers.name: SAST regex scanner
architecture_layers.description: _sast_scan iterates every line against the applicable rule list, emitting a Finding on each regex match with the offending line captured.

architecture_layers.name: Dependency / version analyzer
architecture_layers.description: _parse_ver and _version_in_range parse manifest versions into integer tuples and evaluate <, <=, >, >= and comma-separated ranges against the vulnerable-package CVE tables.

architecture_layers.name: Finding model
architecture_layers.description: A __slots__-based Finding class carries rule_id, name, category, severity, file, line, description, recommendation, cwe and cve for every detection.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting & gating layer
architecture_layers.description: print_report (ANSI severity colours), save_json (schema with metadata, findings array, summary counts), save_html (Catppuccin Mocha dark theme, SSPM), plus filter_severity and 0/1 exit codes.

architecture_layers.name: M365 Graph API collector
architecture_layers.description: The SSPM scanner authenticates via OAuth2 client credentials and queries Microsoft Graph endpoints, evaluating live tenant configuration against 50 posture checks in 12 categories.

project_structure.description: v4.0 combined engine (~3,750 lines): Java SAST plus embedded PHP/Python/MERN rule sets, Maven/Gradle CVEs, and WAR/JAR/EAR archive extraction.
Standalone PHP scanner: 36 source-code rules plus 11 php.ini runtime misconfiguration checks.
Standalone Python scanner: 56 rules including AI/agentic patterns, plus 26 PyPI dependency CVEs.
Standalone JS/TS scanner: 39 source rules + 8 .env rules + 25 npm dependency CVEs for MongoDB/Express/React/Node.
OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) scanner: 69 rules across Python/JS/env/YAML plus 27 vulnerable LLM package CVEs.
Microsoft 365 + Entra ID SSPM scanner (~2,258 lines): 50 live Graph API posture checks with HTML report support.
Eleven intentionally-vulnerable fixtures (VulnerableApp.java, vulnerable.php, vulnerable_agent.py, vulnerable_mern.js, pom.xml, php.ini, .env, etc.) that exercise the scanner rules.
Full scanner catalogue, per-language vulnerability tables, CVE lists, CLI usage, and report-format documentation.
Architecture reference: shared scanner pattern, rule-ID conventions, dispatch flow, and per-scanner rule inventory.
Project banner graphic used in the README.
MIT license.
Excludes Python bytecode, scanner JSON reports, and editor/OS noise.

security_controls.detail: SQL injection (concatenated JDBC/MySQLi, f-string/format queries, Sequelize/Knex), NoSQL injection ($where, mass assignment), command injection (Runtime.exec, ProcessBuilder, os.system, child_process.exec, shell_exec), RCE (eval/exec, preg_replace /e), and SSTI (Jinja2 render_template_string).
Java ObjectInputStream/XMLDecoder/XStream/SnakeYAML, Python pickle/yaml.load/marshal, PHP unserialize() on user input, and Node node-serialize.unserialize().
Detects MD5/SHA-1/DES, ECB mode, and insecure PRNGs (java.util.Random, Python random for tokens), with remediation citing NIST SP 800-131A.
OpenAI sk- keys, AWS/GCP keys, Django SECRET_KEY, JWT/session secrets, and MongoDB connection URIs embedded in source or .env files.

security_controls.area: SSRF, path traversal, XXE, open redirect
security_controls.detail: User-controlled URLs in requests/axios/fetch/curl, file reads from request parameters, entity-resolving XML parsers (DocumentBuilderFactory, lxml resolve_entities), and reflected redirect headers.

Spring Boot exposed actuators/H2 console/debug, Flask/Django DEBUG=True and ALLOWED_HOSTS=[*], wildcard CORS, missing helmet(), insecure cookies, and full php.ini hardening (allow_url_include, register_globals, cookie flags).

97 version-ranged signatures including Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228), Spring4Shell (CVE-2022-22965), Struts (CVE-2017-5638), Shiro-550, fastjson, jackson-databind, xstream, and vulnerable npm/PyPI/LLM packages.

Prompt injection (LLM01), sensitive-info disclosure (LLM02), supply chain (LLM03), data/model poisoning (LLM04), improper output handling into eval/exec (LLM05), excessive agency via ShellTool/PythonREPLTool (LLM06), system-prompt leakage (LLM07), vector/embedding weaknesses (LLM08), misinformation (LLM09), and unbounded consumption (LLM10).
Weak/hardcoded JWT secrets and algorithms:['none'] bypass in Node apps, plus M365 MFA and password-policy checks aligned to NIST SP 800-63B.

50 live checks across Security Defaults, Conditional Access (MFA, legacy auth, risky sign-in), privileged access (Global Admin count, PIM, break-glass), app-registration secret/cert hygiene, guest/external sharing, and Exchange/SharePoint/Teams/audit/Identity-Protection settings.

summary: Static analysis finds bugs in code that never runs the way production does. DAST Scanner attacks the gap: it points a real HTTP client at a live web application, crawls it like a user, injects genuine attack payloads, and reads the responses for proof of exploitation. It is a single, portable Python file with exactly one third-party dependency (requests) — the entire crawler, attack engine, WAF detector, authenticator, and four-format reporter fit in ~3,650 lines of Python 3.10+ that runs unchanged on Windows, macOS, and Linux.

Under the hood it ships 109 distinct security checks organised into 23 vulnerability categories and mapped to the OWASP Top 10 2021 and CWE. Coverage spans the full active-testing surface: error/time/boolean-blind SQL injection, reflected and DOM XSS, SSRF and open redirect, LFI/RFI, XXE, JWT algorithm and signature flaws, insecure deserialization across four runtimes, file-upload bypasses, HTTP request smuggling, web cache poisoning, OAuth and WebSocket weaknesses, plus a curated pack of 10 named CVE probes (Log4Shell, Spring4Shell, Struts, Text4Shell, MOVEit, and more).

Built for real engagements and pipelines, it detects eight major WAF vendors before it starts, enforces strict single-host scope so it never wanders off-target, throttles itself with a token-bucket rate limiter, and supports five authentication modes (none, bearer, cookie, basic, form-login) for testing behind a login. Output comes as coloured console, JSON, an interactive HTML report, and SARIF for GitHub/GitLab code scanning — and it returns exit code 1 on CRITICAL/HIGH findings so it fails a CI build cleanly.

It is aimed at penetration testers, application-security engineers, and DevSecOps teams who want a dependency-light, auditable DAST tool they can drop into a repo, route through Burp or ZAP, and read end to end. A bundled intentionally-vulnerable app (stdlib only) lets anyone validate the scanner in two terminals without touching a production target.

dast-scanner.architecture_summary: A single-file Python scanner organised as a five-stage pipeline over a shared HTTP client. The target URL flows through authentication, WAF detection, and BFS crawling to build a SiteMap (URLs, forms, API endpoints, tech), which 22 check modules consume in parallel to emit typed Finding objects that the reporter renders to console, JSON, HTML, and SARIF.

architecture_layers.name: HTTP client & rate limiter
architecture_layers.description: HTTPClient wraps requests with a token-bucket RateLimiter, request budget, timeout, optional TLS verify, proxy routing, and allowed-hosts scope enforcement.

architecture_layers.name: Auth & WAF pre-flight
architecture_layers.description: AuthManager establishes a session across five modes and detect_waf fingerprints eight WAF vendors before any attack traffic is sent.

architecture_layers.name: Crawler & fingerprinting
architecture_layers.description: WebCrawler runs a BFS crawl seeded by robots.txt/sitemap.xml, using _LinkFormParser (stdlib html.parser) to extract links and forms and detect server/framework/CMS/language into a SiteMap.

architecture_layers.name: Parallel check engine
architecture_layers.description: The CHECK_MODULES registry of 22 generator-based modules runs on a 4-worker ThreadPoolExecutor, yielding Finding dataclasses with rule_id, severity, evidence, CWE, and OWASP mapping.

architecture_layers.name: Multi-format reporter
architecture_layers.description: The report layer serialises findings via save_json, save_html, and save_sarif plus coloured console output, and drives the CRITICAL/HIGH exit-code gate.

Project banner rendered at the top of the README.

Full documentation: rule catalogue, OWASP mapping, CLI reference, pipeline, and CI examples.
MIT License.
Python gitignore.

security_controls.detail: Error-based, time-based blind, and boolean-blind SQL injection via URL params and forms, plus OS command injection and NoSQL injection.
Reflected XSS via URL params, forms, and HTTP headers; DOM sink detection; and reflected content in error pages.
Server-side request forgery via URL parameter and form input, plus open-redirect detection.
Local/Remote File Inclusion and backup-file exposure, plus XXE external-entity file disclosure and XXE via SOAP endpoints.
Missing CSRF tokens, HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite cookie flags, default-credential acceptance, username enumeration, and session fixation.
Exposed .env, .git, wp-config.php, DB connection strings, API keys, stack traces, phpinfo, internal IPs, and server-version leakage.
Missing CSP, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, HSTS, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, plus wildcard and reflected CORS origins.
JWT alg=none / unverified signature / weak secret; Java/.NET/PHP/Python deserialization; and unrestricted upload with extension/content-type bypass.
HTTP request smuggling (CL.TE/TE.CL), web cache poisoning/deception, OAuth flow flaws, WebSocket origin/transport issues, and host-header injection.
Probes for Log4Shell, Spring4Shell, Struts, Text4Shell, MOVEit and more; WordPress/Joomla/Drupal weaknesses; and expired/weak-TLS/mixed-content checks.

summary: APIs are where modern breaches happen — and traditional web scanners largely miss the class of flaws that live at the API layer: broken object-level authorization, mass assignment, unrestricted resource consumption, and shadow endpoints. The API Security Scanner closes that gap with a static-analysis engine that discovers, audits, and reports API security vulnerabilities and misconfigurations across REST, GraphQL, gRPC, OpenAPI/Swagger specs, and API gateway configurations, with every finding mapped to the OWASP API Security Top 10 (2023).

It ships as a single, zero-dependency Python file that runs on pure 3.10+ standard library across Windows, macOS, and Linux — no pip installs, no services, no cloud. Point it at a file or a whole project tree and it fingerprints your API stack (frameworks, protocols, auth methods, gateways, databases), runs 112 regex-backed detection rules across 22 categories, and emits coloured console output, machine-parseable JSON, and an interactive HTML report. A CI-friendly exit code (1 on any CRITICAL/HIGH) makes it a drop-in pull-request gate.

The scanner is deliberately broad in surface: it reads Python, JavaScript/TypeScript, Java, Go, Ruby, and PHP source; GraphQL schemas and Protobuf service definitions; OpenAPI specs, Nginx/Kong/Envoy gateway configs, Dockerfiles, Kubernetes manifests, and .env files — applying the right rule packs per file type. Each finding carries a severity, CWE reference, actionable remediation, and mappings to PCI-DSS v4.0, GDPR, HIPAA, and DORA alongside OWASP.

It's built for AppSec engineers, API platform teams, and security-minded developers who want fast, offline, dependency-free API risk visibility they can wire into a pipeline in minutes rather than standing up a scanning platform.

features.title: Severity filtering
features.description: A --severity threshold trims noise so teams can focus on CRITICAL/HIGH first or surface everything down to LOW/INFO.

api-security-scanner.architecture_summary: The scanner is a single self-contained Python module organized as a bank of module-level rule dictionaries feeding a regex SAST engine. A file dispatcher routes each file by type to the appropriate scanner method, which applies the relevant rule packs line-by-line; matches become Finding dataclasses that are aggregated, severity-sorted, and rendered to console, JSON, or HTML.

architecture_layers.name: Rule definition layer
architecture_layers.description: 22 module-level *_RULES lists (BOLA_RULES, AUTH_RULES, INJECTION_RULES, GRAPHQL_RULES, K8S_API_RULES, etc.) — each rule a dict of id, category, severity, regex pattern, description, CWE, recommendation, and compliance tags, plus a COMPLIANCE_MAP.

architecture_layers.name: Finding model
architecture_layers.description: A Finding dataclass capturing rule_id, name, category, severity, file path, line number, matched code, description, remediation, CWE/CVE, and compliance list — the single normalized record used across all reports.

architecture_layers.name: File dispatcher
architecture_layers.description: APISecurityScanner.scan_path / _scan_directory walk the tree (skipping node_modules, venv, build dirs) and _dispatch_file routes by extension/name to _scan_source, _scan_proto, _scan_graphql, _scan_docker, _scan_gateway, _scan_env, or _scan_config.

architecture_layers.name: SAST regex engine
architecture_layers.description: _sast_scan iterates file lines, skips comments/blank lines, and case-insensitively matches each applicable rule's pattern, emitting a Finding via _add on every hit.

architecture_layers.name: Inventory detection
architecture_layers.description: _detect_inventory keyword-matches file content against framework, protocol, auth, gateway, and database dictionaries to build a discovered API stack profile alongside the vulnerability findings.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting layer
architecture_layers.description: print_report (coloured console), save_json (structured export), and save_html (dark-theme interactive report with severity cards and JS filtering), driven by summary() counts and severity ordering.

architecture_layers.name: CLI and exit-code gate
architecture_layers.description: An argparse front end (target, --json, --html, --severity, --verbose, --version) that filters by severity and exits 1 on any CRITICAL/HIGH for CI/CD.

project_structure.description: The entire scanner — ~1,163 lines: 112 rules, dispatcher, SAST engine, inventory detection, and console/JSON/HTML reporters in one zero-dependency file.
Full documentation — rule category table, compliance mappings, file-type matrix, inventory discovery, CI/CD snippets, and quickstart.
Project instructions and architecture notes: rule schema, category counts, conventions, and contribution guidelines.
Nine intentionally vulnerable fixtures used to exercise and regression-test the rule set.
Insecure Flask API fixture covering BOLA, auth, SSRF, injection, and secrets.
Insecure Express.js API fixture for the JavaScript/TypeScript rule path.
Insecure OpenAPI 3.x spec triggering the OpenAPI spec rule pack.
Insecure GraphQL schema exercising introspection, depth, and mutation-auth checks.
Insecure Protobuf/gRPC definition for Protobuf and gRPC rules.
Project banner graphic used in the README.
MIT License.
Python/IDE ignore rules.

security_controls.detail: 6 rules for direct object-ID access without ownership checks, unguarded path/pk params across Flask/Express/Django/Spring, and sequential/predictable ID enumeration (CWE-639, CWE-330).

10 rules covering missing auth decorators, hardcoded JWT secrets, 'none'/weak algorithms, disabled signature/expiry verification, API keys in query strings, unhashed passwords, and OAuth implicit flow (CWE-798, CWE-306, CWE-327, CWE-345).
5 rules for mass assignment, request body unpacked into models, sensitive fields returned in responses, and GraphQL field over-exposure.
6 rules for missing rate limiting, absent pagination, unbounded uploads, missing timeouts, and unlimited GraphQL query depth/complexity.
Admin endpoints without RBAC, privilege escalation, method override, unprotected DELETE, plus missing CAPTCHA on login and unprotected payment/reset flows.

User-controlled URLs in server requests, open redirects, webhook/file-import SSRF, plus CORS wildcards, debug mode, verbose errors, missing security headers, HTTP serving, and disabled TLS verification (10 misconfig rules).
7 injection rules (SQL, NoSQL, command, XSS, LDAP, XXE, path traversal) and 6 secrets rules (hardcoded API keys, passwords, private keys, AWS creds, DB connection strings, bearer tokens).

GraphQL introspection, missing depth limits, batching attacks, unauthenticated mutations, missing field auth; gRPC insecure channels, reflection enabled, missing auth interceptors, and unlimited message size.

Nginx/Kong/Envoy/AWS gateways without rate limiting or auth; Dockerfile root user, embedded secrets, unpinned base images; K8s Ingress-without-TLS, open LoadBalancers, missing NetworkPolicy/limits, and secrets mounted as env vars.

TLS rules (HTTP endpoints, weak versions/ciphers, missing HSTS), logging rules (sensitive data in logs, disabled logging, full-body logging), OpenAPI spec rules (missing security schemes, API key in query, HTTP server URLs), and .env exposure checks.

summary: Generative-AI features ship faster than security teams can review them, and the classic SAST tools that guard web apps have no concept of a prompt, an agent tool, a vector store, or a model file. The OWASP LLM Top 10 Scanner closes that gap. It is a single, self-contained Python file (owasp_llm_scanner.py, ~2,000 lines, zero third-party dependencies) that statically analyzes AI/LLM application source, config, and dependency manifests against the OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications (2025) — from prompt injection and system-prompt leakage to excessive agency, insecure output handling, and unbounded consumption.

Unlike a generic linter, every rule is written for the way LLM code actually looks: f-strings and template literals that splice user input into prompts, LangChain agents invoked on raw request bodies, torch.load() and from_pretrained(trust_remote_code=True) that turn a model file into remote code execution, ShellTool / PythonREPLTool wired into an autonomous agent, LLM output piped into eval()/exec()/subprocess/innerHTML, and ChatOpenAI clients instantiated with no max_tokens. It reads Python, JavaScript/TypeScript, .env, and YAML for pattern findings, and parses requirements.txt, Pipfile, pyproject.toml, and package.json to flag known-vulnerable LLM libraries (LangChain, transformers, torch, gradio, mlflow, litellm, llama-index, ollama, and more) by CVE and version range.

It is built for AppSec engineers, ML/platform teams, and CI/CD owners who need a fast, dependency-free gate they can drop into any pipeline. Findings carry a severity, CWE mapping, the offending line, a plain-English risk description, and concrete remediation. Output is human-readable color console or machine-readable JSON, and the process exits non-zero on any CRITICAL/HIGH finding so a build can fail the moment risky AI code is introduced.

It is notable for being a purpose-built LLM SAST scanner rather than a bolt-on ruleset: all ten OWASP LLM categories are represented, the checks map to the concrete APIs of the modern AI stack, and the whole thing runs anywhere Python 3.8+ runs with nothing to install.

features.title: Full OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) coverage
features.description: Ships rules for every category LLM01 through LLM10, so a single scan reports against the entire framework rather than a subset.

features.title: LLM-native prompt-injection detection
features.description: Flags user input spliced into prompts via f-strings, .format(), string concatenation, template literals, and PromptTemplate/agent invocations across Python and JS/TS.

features.title: Insecure model-loading and RCE checks
features.description: Catches torch.load() without weights_only, trust_remote_code=True in from_pretrained/load_dataset, allow_dangerous_deserialization, and pickle-based chain loading.

features.title: Improper output-handling sinks
features.description: Detects LLM output flowing into eval/exec, SQL execute, subprocess/os.system, Jinja2 |safe, innerHTML, document.write, and React dangerouslySetInnerHTML.

features.title: Excessive-agency guardrail checks
features.description: Warns when ShellTool, PythonREPLTool, unrestricted tools, unbounded AgentExecutor loops, or human_input_mode=NEVER hand too much power to an agent.

features.title: Dependency CVE scanning for the AI stack
features.description: Parses requirements.txt, Pipfile, pyproject.toml, and package.json to match LangChain, transformers, torch, gradio, mlflow, litellm and others against known CVEs by version range.

features.title: Secret and system-prompt leakage detection
features.description: Finds hardcoded OpenAI/Anthropic/HuggingFace keys, keys and system prompts in .env/YAML/client-side code, and prompts leaked via verbose mode, logs, or API responses.

features.title: Unbounded-consumption and cost controls
features.description: Flags LLM clients without max_tokens, API calls without timeouts, and generate/invoke calls inside while-True/setInterval loops that can run away on cost.

features.title: Vector and embedding weakness checks
features.description: Detects unauthenticated ChromaDB clients, unsanitized user input written to vector stores, and similarity_search results fed straight into prompts (indirect injection).

features.title: Multi-format input support
features.description: One scanner reads .py/.pyw, .js/.jsx/.ts/.tsx/.mjs/.cjs, .env, .yaml/.yml, and Python/Node dependency manifests from a file or a whole directory tree.

features.title: CI/CD-ready reporting
features.description: Color-coded console output plus a structured JSON report, severity filtering, and an exit code of 1 on CRITICAL/HIGH findings to gate builds.

features.title: Zero-install, single-file deployment
features.description: The entire scanner is one standard-library-only Python file, so it drops into any environment or pipeline with nothing to pip install.

owasp-llm-scanner.architecture_summary: The scanner is a single Python module organized as data-driven rule sets fed through a shared regex engine. A central LLMScanner class walks the target, dispatches each file to a handler by extension, and either line-matches SAST/config rules or parses a dependency manifest against a CVE database; every hit becomes a Finding that flows into console and JSON reporters. There is no external SAST framework — pattern rules, the CVE table, the version-range matcher, and the reporters are all self-contained.

architecture_layers.name: Rule sets (data layer)
architecture_layers.description: Module-level lists LLM_PYTHON_SAST_RULES (45), LLM_JS_SAST_RULES (16), LLM_ENV_RULES (4) and LLM_YAML_RULES (4), each a dict of id/category/severity/regex/description/cwe/recommendation.

architecture_layers.name: CVE knowledge base
architecture_layers.description: LLM_VULNERABLE_PACKAGES (14 Python packages) and LLM_NPM_VULNERABLE_PACKAGES (3 npm packages) map library names to affected version ranges, CVE ids, severity, and fix versions.

architecture_layers.name: LLMScanner orchestrator
architecture_layers.description: scan_path -> _scan_directory (os.walk with a SKIP_DIRS denylist) -> _dispatch_file routes each path to the right handler by suffix/name.

architecture_layers.name: File-type handlers
architecture_layers.description: _scan_python/_scan_js/_scan_env/_scan_yaml run the SAST engine, while _scan_requirements/_scan_pipfile/_scan_pyproject_toml/_scan_package_json parse manifests and add an unpinned-package heuristic (LLM03-REQ-001).

architecture_layers.name: SAST regex engine
architecture_layers.description: _sast_scan compiles each rule's pattern once, iterates file lines, skips pure comment lines, and emits a Finding on any match.

architecture_layers.name: Version-range CVE matcher
architecture_layers.description: _parse_ver and _version_in_range normalize versions to int tuples and evaluate constraints like '<0.0.247' or '>=2.0,<2.15.0' to decide if a dependency is vulnerable.

architecture_layers.name: Finding model and reporters
architecture_layers.description: A __slots__-style Finding object carries rule/severity/file/line/CWE/CVE; summary, filter_severity, print_report (ANSI color) and save_json render results and drive the exit code.

project_structure.description: The scanner itself — rule sets, CVE database, LLMScanner engine, reporters, and CLI in one ~1,998-line file.

Sibling Java/Maven/Gradle/WAR SAST scanner.

Project banner asset; LICENSE holds the MIT license.

security_controls.detail: Detects user input interpolated into prompts via f-strings, concatenation, .format(), PromptTemplate.from_template, ChatPromptTemplate, and agent run/invoke on raw request data (Python + JS/TS).
Flags hardcoded OpenAI/Anthropic/HuggingFace keys, PII field names passed to LLM calls, and LLM responses logged or printed; .env rules catch API keys and DB creds that can reach model context.

torch.load without weights_only, trust_remote_code=True, unpinned HuggingFace revisions, HTTP model downloads without integrity checks, plus a version-range CVE database of 14 Python + 3 npm LLM libraries.
Detects trust_remote_code in load_dataset, unvalidated training-data paths from argv, unsanitized user feedback written to training corpora, and remote datasets loaded without integrity verification.
Catches LLM output flowing into eval/exec, cursor.execute (SQLi), subprocess/os.system (command injection), Jinja2 |safe, innerHTML, document.write, Function(), and React dangerouslySetInnerHTML.

Flags ShellTool/BashTool and PythonREPLTool/CodeInterpreter exposed to agents, AgentExecutor without max_iterations, allow_dangerous_tools=True, write-capable FileManagementTool, and human_input_mode=NEVER.
Detects verbose=True agents, system prompts returned in API responses, logged, hardcoded in client-side JS, or stored in .env/YAML config.
Flags ChromaDB clients created without authentication, unsanitized user input stored in vector DBs, and similarity_search results used directly in prompts (indirect injection).

security_controls.area: LLM09 & LLM10 Misinformation and Unbounded Consumption
security_controls.detail: Flags temperature>0.9 and unvalidated streaming, plus LLM clients without max_tokens, API calls without timeouts, and generate/invoke inside while-True/setInterval loops.
Every rule carries a CWE (e.g., CWE-74, CWE-502, CWE-94, CWE-78, CWE-89, CWE-79, CWE-400, CWE-312, CWE-1104) and a concrete remediation, with severity from CRITICAL to LOW.

summary: SAP ABAP Vulnerability Analyzer is a dual-mode security scanner purpose-built for the SAP stack that generic SAST tools consistently miss. It performs passive static analysis of ABAP, CDS, UI5/JavaScript and SAP BTP configuration files, and — when handed XSUAA credentials — actively audits a live SAP BTP sub-account over OAuth 2.0. The entire tool is a single self-contained Python file: SAST mode runs on pure standard library with zero pip installs, and only the live BTP API mode needs the requests package.

Inspired by SAP's in-system Code Vulnerability Analyzer (CVA), it understands ABAP-specific attack surface that off-the-shelf scanners can't parse: dynamic Open SQL (SELECT ... WHERE (variable)), runtime code generation (INSERT REPORT, GENERATE SUBROUTINE POOL), missing AUTHORITY-CHECK in RFC-enabled function modules, RFC lateral-movement via dynamic DESTINATION, and insider-backdoor patterns such as hardcoded SY-UNAME checks or direct writes to USR02/AGR_* authorization tables. It correctly handles ABAP comment syntax, case-insensitive keywords and statement terminators so it doesn't drown teams in parse-noise.

Across the two modes it ships 134 security checks — 104 SAST regex rules (89 ABAP/CDS, 8 BTP config, 7 UI5/JS) plus 30 live BTP API checks — every one mapped to a CWE and an OWASP Top 10 (2021) category. An optional intra-procedural data-flow (taint) engine confirms findings fed by real external input, suppresses literal/sanitized false positives, and prints the source-to-sink path (also emitted as SARIF codeFlows for GitHub code scanning).

It is built for SAP security teams, ABAP developers and compliance auditors who need CI-gateable results: a weighted A-F security grade, scan profiles, inline #NOSEC and baseline suppression, exit code 1 on CRITICAL/HIGH, and four output formats (console, JSON, HTML, SARIF 2.1.0). A 90-test pytest suite runs on a Python 3.10-3.13 CI matrix with no SAP system or network required.

features.title: Dual-mode scanning
features.description: Runs passive SAST over source and config files, active OAuth 2.0 API auditing of a live BTP sub-account, or both simultaneously into one unified report.

features.title: ABAP-aware parsing
features.description: Strips ABAP comment syntax (* full-line and " inline), handles case-insensitive keywords and . terminators, and does block-aware analysis of whole FUNCTION/METHOD blocks so a comment-only AUTHORITY-CHECK never fools it.

features.title: Data-flow (taint) analysis
features.description: Optional --data-flow engine tracks tainted / sanitized / literal variables per procedure to confirm real injection findings, drop false positives, and print the source-to-sink path.

features.title: Insider-backdoor detection
features.description: A dedicated malicious-code category flags hardcoded SY-UNAME/SY-MANDT logic bombs, direct DML writes to USR02/AGR_* tables, and programmatic SAP_ALL/SAP_NEW grants.

features.title: SAP dependency CVE checking
features.description: Cross-references package versions against known SAP CVEs including RECON (CVE-2020-6287), HTTP request smuggling (CVE-2022-22536) and CommonCryptoLib privilege escalation.

features.title: BTP configuration analysis
features.description: Deeply inspects xs-security.json, xs-app.json, mta.yaml and CDS annotations for wildcard scope grants, unauthenticated routes, disabled token validation and CORS wildcards.

features.title: Zero-dependency single file
features.description: The whole scanner is one ~3,400-line Python file that runs SAST mode on pure stdlib — clone or curl one file and go, no install step.

features.title: SARIF 2.1.0 + GitHub code scanning
features.description: Exports SARIF with per-rule security-severity scores, CWE helpUris, an OWASP taxonomies block and codeFlows so findings render inline on pull requests.

features.title: OWASP + CWE mapping
features.description: Every rule maps to a CWE identifier and an OWASP Top 10 (2021) category, with per-OWASP breakdowns in console, JSON and SARIF.

features.title: Security grade (A-F)
features.description: A weighted score (CRITICAL 10 / HIGH 5 / MEDIUM 2 / LOW 1) summarises posture at a glance in the console header, JSON and an HTML grade card.

features.title: Scan profiles & rule selection
features.description: --profile full|critical-high|quick plus --disable / --enable-only by rule ID or category prefix, and --list-rules to preview the active catalogue for fast PR gates vs deep audits.

features.title: False-positive management
features.description: Inline #NOSEC suppression comments and a line-number-independent baseline file let teams onboard the scanner onto existing code and fail only on NEW findings.

sap-abap-scanner.architecture_summary: Everything lives in one self-contained Python file, abap_scanner.py. Categorised module-level rule dictionaries feed two scanner engines that share a common reporting mixin. The passive AbapSastScanner walks a target tree, dispatches each file by extension, runs a line-by-line regex pass and a state-carrying block pass, then optionally refines results through a taint analyzer; the active AbapBtpScanner authenticates to SAP BTP via XSUAA OAuth 2.0 and evaluates live API responses. Both funnel Finding objects through a RuleSelector (profiles/toggles) and _ReportMixin, which renders unified console, JSON, HTML and SARIF output.

architecture_layers.name: Rule catalogue (module-level dicts)
architecture_layers.description: Categorised ABAP_*_RULES lists of {id, category, severity, pattern, cwe, recommendation} plus BTP_API_CHECKS — 104 SAST rules and 30 BTP checks defined as data, not code.

architecture_layers.name: AbapSastScanner (passive engine)
architecture_layers.description: Two-pass SAST over ABAP/CDS/UI5/config files: _sast_scan runs pre-compiled case-insensitive regex line-by-line and _block_scan carries state to flag whole FUNCTION/METHOD blocks missing a required statement like AUTHORITY-CHECK.

architecture_layers.name: TaintAnalyzer (data-flow refinement)
architecture_layers.description: Intra-procedural taint tracker (opt-in --data-flow) that classifies variables as tainted/sanitized/clean, confirms or suppresses sink findings, and reconstructs the source-to-sink provenance trace.

architecture_layers.name: AbapBtpScanner (active engine)
architecture_layers.description: XSUAA OAuth 2.0 client-credentials client with automatic token refresh that queries SCIM, role collections, destinations and audit-log APIs and evaluates them via pure eval_* functions.

architecture_layers.name: RuleSelector (profiles & selection)
architecture_layers.description: Applies scan profiles and --disable/--enable-only toggles at a single _add funnel so selection is enforced uniformly across SAST, block, CVE and BTP findings.

architecture_layers.name: _ReportMixin (reporting & post-processing)

security_controls.detail: 24 rules for dynamic Open SQL WHERE/ORDER BY/FROM (variable), ADBC execute_query, and ABAP runtime code generation via INSERT REPORT, GENERATE SUBROUTINE POOL, CALL TRANSFORMATION and RFC_ABAP_INSTALL_AND_RUN.
9 rules including block-aware detection of function modules lacking AUTHORITY-CHECK, DUMMY/unchecked SY-SUBRC bypasses, unguarded DML and CALL TRANSACTION without S_TCODE.
11 rules for kernel CALL 'SYSTEM', SXPG_COMMAND_EXECUTE, unvalidated OPEN/READ/DELETE DATASET without FILE_VALIDATE_NAME, and ../ concatenation.
7 rules targeting insider implants: hardcoded SY-UNAME/SY-MANDT/SY-SYSID checks, direct writes to USR02/AGR_* auth tables, BAPI_USER_* admin, and programmatic SAP_ALL/SAP_NEW grants.
5 rules for trusted RFC without validation, dynamic DESTINATION variables, RFC callback, registered server programs and async STARTING NEW TASK without error handling.
12 rules for literal passwords/API keys/tokens, RFC destinations with embedded passwords, MD5/SHA-1/DES-3DES usage, hardcoded keys/IVs and non-cryptographic RNG.
13 rules across ABAP HTTP/BSP response handling and UI5/Fiori JS — set_cdata without escaping, cl_abap_browser show_html, eval, innerHTML, document.write and jQuery .html().

8 config rules for xs-security.json scope grants, xs-app.json unauthenticated routes, mta.yaml secrets and disabled XSUAA validation, plus version checks against 6 known SAP CVEs (RECON CVE-2020-6287, CVE-2022-22536, CVE-2023-40309/44487/49583, CVE-2021-21491).

Password policy, inactive/admin/service accounts and MFA, wildcard role scopes, stale service bindings, trust config, audit logging, HTTP/basic-auth destinations and session/token lifetimes across 9 check groups.
9 rules for ssl_id ANONYM, http:// URLs, disabled CSRF, ssl_verify=0, wildcard ACTVT='*', open redirects and hardcoded IPs.

summary: Mobile Application Security Testing (MAST) Scanner is a single-file, zero-dependency Python tool that performs static analysis of shipped Android APK and iOS IPA binaries — no source code, emulator, or SDK required. Point it at a build artifact and it unzips, parses the compiled formats (Android binary XML, DEX string pools, Mach-O binaries, Info.plist), and runs 24 check modules covering manifest misconfiguration, hardcoded secrets, weak cryptography, insecure transport, unsafe storage, WebView and IPC exposure, biometric and session flaws, missing binary/resilience protections, and privacy/tracking concerns.

Every finding is mapped to the OWASP Mobile Top 10 2024, MASVS v2 (22 of 24 controls spanning all eight groups), and CWE — so results speak the language a mobile security program already reports against. A bundled dependency-CVE engine matches version strings pulled from DEX, Mach-O, and native libraries against a curated database of 39 CVEs across 29 libraries (OkHttp, Retrofit, Jackson, Log4j, Bouncy Castle, libwebp, OpenSSL, AFNetworking, Alamofire, Firebase, and more), flagging Log4Shell, the libwebp heap overflow, cert-pinning bypasses, and insecure-deserialization RCEs hiding inside third-party code.

It is built for AppSec engineers, pentesters, and DevSecOps teams who need fast, offline triage of mobile builds without standing up a heavyweight platform. Four output formats — coloured console, JSON, a dark-themed HTML report with MASVS coverage, and SARIF v2.1.0 — make it equally at home on an analyst's laptop and in a CI pipeline, where a non-zero exit on CRITICAL/HIGH findings can gate a release.

Because the whole scanner is one ~2,800-line Python file that runs on the 3.8+ standard library alone, it drops into air-gapped labs, ephemeral CI runners, and bug-bounty workflows with nothing to install and no network calls.

mast-scanner.architecture_summary: A single-file pipeline: the MASTScanner orchestrator auto-detects the platform, hands the artifact to an APKAnalyzer or IPAAnalyzer that unzips it and invokes custom binary parsers, then fans the extracted manifest, strings, and metadata across 24 check modules plus a dependency-CVE engine, each returning typed Finding objects that are deduplicated, severity-sorted, and rendered into console, JSON, HTML, and SARIF reports.

architecture_layers.name: CLI & Orchestrator (main / MASTScanner)
architecture_layers.description: Parses arguments, auto-detects Android vs iOS, runs the platform's check set, deduplicates and severity-sorts findings, applies the severity filter, and sets the CI exit code.

architecture_layers.name: Artifact Analyzers (APKAnalyzer / IPAAnalyzer)
architecture_layers.description: Open the ZIP container and extract the manifest/Info.plist, DEX and Mach-O string pools, native .so libraries, entitlements, and file listings into a normalized dict.

architecture_layers.name: Binary Parsers
architecture_layers.description: parse_android_binary_xml decodes compiled AndroidManifest.xml chunk streams, extract_dex_strings reads DEX string tables, and extract_macho_strings pulls printable strings from Mach-O and native binaries.

architecture_layers.name: SAST Check Modules (24)
architecture_layers.description: Per-domain functions (manifest, secrets, crypto, network, storage, webview, components, auth, resilience, code, privacy, plus iOS and common variants) each return List[Finding] tagged with severity, CWE, OWASP, and MASVS.

architecture_layers.name: Dependency CVE Engine
architecture_layers.description: ANDROID_LIB_CVES, IOS_LIB_CVES, and NATIVE_LIB_CVES databases matched via regex version patterns and _parse_ver / _version_in_range range comparison to flag known-vulnerable library versions.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting Layer
architecture_layers.description: print_report, save_json, save_html, and save_sarif render the finding set as coloured console output, JSON, a dark-themed MASVS-coverage HTML report, and SARIF v2.1.0.

security_controls.detail: 12 rules for debuggable/backup flags, exported activities/services/receivers/providers without permission, cleartext traffic, low min SDK, dangerous permissions, SYSTEM_ALERT_WINDOW, missing network security config, and task affinity.
Android (8) + common (6) rules for AWS access/secret keys, Google/Stripe/Twilio/SendGrid API keys, Firebase URLs, hardcoded passwords, private keys, OAuth client secrets, and bearer tokens.
Android crypto (5) + common crypto (5) rules for MD5/SHA1/DES/ECB, insecure random, hardcoded keys and IVs, and Base64 pseudo-encryption.

Android network (7) + iOS transport (4) rules for TrustManager and hostname-verifier bypass, cleartext HTTP, missing cert pinning, weak TLS/SSL versions, custom SSLSocketFactory, and iOS ATS exception weaknesses.
8 storage rules for world-accessible files, external storage, unencrypted SQLite, clipboard leakage, missing screenshot prevention, and keyboard cache exposure.
WebView (5) and component (5) rules for JavaScript/file access and debugging, PendingIntent mutability, dynamic code loading, URI permission grants, and dynamic loadUrl input.

Android auth (5) + iOS auth (4) rules for BiometricPrompt without CryptoObject, deprecated FingerprintManager, auth state in SharedPreferences/UserDefaults, missing session timeout, and Keychain items without access control.

Android resilience (6) + iOS resilience (5) + iOS binary (4) rules for missing obfuscation, root/jailbreak/emulator/debugger detection, tamper/integrity verification, hooking-framework references, and PIE/ARC/stack-canary flags.

Android privacy (5) + iOS privacy (4) rules for advertising IDs/IDFA, tracking and analytics SDKs, device fingerprinting, background location, and missing PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy / App Tracking Transparency.

39 CVEs across 29 Android, iOS, and native libraries — including Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228), the libwebp/ExoPlayer heap overflow (CVE-2023-4863), OkHttp cert-pinning bypass, and Facebook SDK deserialization RCE — matched by embedded version strings.

summary: Traditional SAST tools were built for web apps and miss the entire class of risks that AI/ML systems introduce. AI Security Posture Management (AI-SPM) closes that gap: a single-file, zero-dependency Python scanner that statically analyzes AI/ML codebases end-to-end — from data ingestion and model training through deployment and live inference — and flags the security misconfigurations, unsafe patterns, and compliance gaps that generic scanners walk straight past.

Point it at a repo and it does three things at once. It runs 145 AI-specific SAST rules across 26 categories (unsafe pickle/torch.load model loading, prompt injection, data poisoning, PII leakage to LLM APIs, missing guardrails, over-autonomous agents, RAG and MCP exposure, shadow AI, and more). It builds an AI/ML inventory — auto-discovering the frameworks, models, APIs, vector databases, and experiment trackers actually present in the code. And it checks the supply chain, matching pinned dependency versions against a curated database of 33 real AI/ML CVEs in packages like TensorFlow, PyTorch, LangChain, mlflow, Ray (ShadowRay), Ollama, and ChromaDB.

Every finding is mapped to four AI-specific governance frameworks — NIST AI RMF, the EU AI Act, OWASP ML Top 10, and MITRE ATLAS — so a security finding doubles as a compliance signal. It scans Python, JS/TS, Jupyter notebooks, .env, YAML/TOML, Terraform, Dockerfiles, and dependency manifests, and emits colored console output, machine-parseable JSON, and an interactive HTML report.

It is built for AppSec engineers, ML platform teams, and AI governance leads who need to shift AI risk left. Because the whole scanner is one portable Python 3.10+ stdlib file with no pip installs, it drops into any CI pipeline (GitHub Actions, GitLab, Azure DevOps, Jenkins) and gates builds via an exit code of 1 on CRITICAL or HIGH findings — no agents, no cloud, no dependency management.

ai-spm-scanner.architecture_summary: The entire scanner is one self-contained Python file (ai_spm_scanner.py, ~2,230 LOC, stdlib only). Rules live as module-level lists of dicts; an AISPMScanner class walks the target, dispatches each file by extension to a language-specific scanner, runs the regex SAST engine line-by-line, and simultaneously builds an AI/ML inventory and checks dependency manifests against a CVE database. Findings (a Finding dataclass carrying CWE, CVE, and compliance tags) are then rendered to console, JSON, or HTML.

architecture_layers.name: Rule & CVE Knowledge Base
architecture_layers.description: 23 module-level *_RULES lists (145 pattern rules), the COMPLIANCE_MAP of 24 framework controls, and AI_VULNERABLE_PACKAGES / AI_NPM_VULNERABLE_PACKAGES CVE databases.

architecture_layers.name: Traversal & Dispatch
architecture_layers.description: scan_path → _scan_directory walks the tree (skipping node_modules, __pycache__, etc.); _dispatch_file routes each file to the right scanner by extension.

architecture_layers.name: Language Scanners
architecture_layers.description: Dedicated handlers _scan_python, _scan_js, _scan_env, _scan_config, _scan_terraform, _scan_docker, _scan_requirements, _scan_pyproject, _scan_package_json, and _scan_notebook.

architecture_layers.name: SAST Regex Engine
architecture_layers.description: The shared _sast_scan applies compiled regex rules line-by-line, emitting a Finding with rule metadata, CWE, and compliance mappings.

architecture_layers.name: AI Inventory & Supply-Chain Analysis
architecture_layers.description: In parallel, detects AI/ML frameworks, models, APIs, vector DBs, and trackers, and version-compares dependencies against the curated CVE database.

architecture_layers.name: Compliance & Model Card Layer
architecture_layers.description: Tags findings with NIST AI RMF / EU AI Act / OWASP ML / MITRE ATLAS controls and runs the directory-level _check_model_card EU AI Act documentation check.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting
architecture_layers.description: print_report renders color-coded console output, JSON, and a self-contained interactive HTML report, then sets a CI-friendly exit code.

summary: AWS Security Scanner is a two-in-one AWS security toolkit that covers both sides of the cloud lifecycle from a single repository: catch misconfigurations in infrastructure code before it ships, and audit what is actually running in a live account afterwards. Both tools are pure Python with a deliberately small footprint, so they drop into a laptop, a CI runner, or a compliance workstation with almost no setup.

The IaC Security Scanner (aws_offline_scanner.py) performs pure static analysis of Terraform (.tf) and CloudFormation (YAML/JSON) files with no AWS credentials and no network access. It combines 59 Terraform regex SAST rules with 42 CloudFormation resource-type structural checks across 25+ AWS services, emitting console, JSON, or interactive HTML reports and a non-zero exit code on CRITICAL/HIGH findings so it can gate a pipeline. Because it never touches AWS, security teams can review a vendor's or a developer's templates before anything is provisioned.

The Live Audit Scanner (aws_live_scanner.py) connects to a running account through boto3 using only read-only describe/get/list calls, executing 98 distinct checks across 25 service sections. Every finding is auto-tagged with severity, mapped controls from five compliance frameworks (CIS AWS v3.0, PCI DSS v4.0, HIPAA, SOC 2, NIST 800-53 Rev 5), and a copy-paste AWS CLI remediation command. It rolls results into a 0-100 posture score with an A-F letter grade and saves CSV/JSON evidence artefacts for auditors.

Together the two scanners give a security leader a shift-left gate and a runtime assurance check that speak the same language of AWS services, severities, and compliance controls. The live scanner ships with 28 mock-boto3 unit tests so its check logic can be validated without ever hitting a real AWS account.

features.title: Dual-mode coverage (pre-deploy and runtime)
features.description: One repo pairs a credential-free IaC static analyzer with a live boto3 account auditor so misconfigurations are caught both before and after deployment.

features.title: Terraform SAST engine
features.description: 59 regex-based rules (TF_SAST_RULES) scan .tf files line-by-line, skipping comments, to flag insecure resource arguments across 25+ services.

features.title: CloudFormation structural analysis
features.description: A CF_DISPATCH table routes 42 AWS:: resource types to dedicated _cf_* check methods that inspect template structure rather than raw text.

features.title: Custom CloudFormation YAML loader
features.description: A purpose-built loader (_CF_LOADER) parses CloudFormation intrinsic tags like !Ref, !Sub, and !GetAtt that would otherwise break a standard YAML parser.

features.title: Read-only live account audit
features.description: The live scanner uses only describe/get/list boto3 calls and never modifies AWS resources, so it is safe to run against production.

features.title: Five-framework compliance mapping
features.description: Each finding is tagged via COMPLIANCE_MAP with the applicable CIS, PCI DSS, HIPAA, SOC 2, and NIST 800-53 controls.

features.title: Posture scoring with letter grade
features.description: compute_risk_score turns findings into a severity-weighted 0-100 score and score_to_grade converts it to an A-F grade for executive reporting.

features.title: Actionable AWS CLI remediation
features.description: REMEDIATION_MAP attaches a ready-to-run AWS CLI command to every failed check so engineers can fix issues without hunting through docs.

features.title: Evidence artefact collection
features.description: save_evidence writes per-check CSV/JSON artefacts to a timestamped output directory to support audit and compliance workflows.

features.title: Three output formats per scanner
features.description: Both tools produce colour-coded console output, machine-readable JSON, and a self-contained dark-themed interactive HTML report with severity and search filtering.

features.title: Section-scoped and severity-filtered scans
features.description: The live scanner can run only named sections (e.g. IAM S3 VPC) and the IaC scanner can suppress findings below a chosen severity threshold.

features.title: CI/CD-friendly exit codes
features.description: The IaC scanner returns exit code 1 when any CRITICAL or HIGH finding is present, making it a drop-in pipeline gate for infrastructure code.

aws-scanner.architecture_summary: Two independent single-file Python scanners share a common design language of Findings/Results, severities, and compliance tags. The IaC scanner runs a Terraform regex-SAST pass and a CloudFormation structural pass over local files; the live scanner drives lazy-cached boto3 clients through 25 section methods, then enriches every result with severity, compliance, and remediation before scoring and reporting.

architecture_layers.name: IaC input dispatch
architecture_layers.description: AWSIaCScanner.scan_path walks a file or directory and _dispatch_file routes .tf to the Terraform engine and .yaml/.yml/.json to the CloudFormation engine, skipping package/tsconfig JSON.

architecture_layers.name: Terraform SAST engine
architecture_layers.description: _sast_scan applies the 59 TF_SAST_RULES regex patterns line-by-line, producing Finding dataclasses (rule_id, severity, file/line, description, recommendation, CWE).

architecture_layers.name: CloudFormation structural engine
architecture_layers.description: A custom _CF_LOADER parses intrinsic tags, then CF_DISPATCH maps 42 AWS:: resource types via getattr to 38 dedicated _cf_* methods that evaluate resource properties.

architecture_layers.name: Live boto3 audit engine
architecture_layers.description: AWSLiveScanner._client lazily caches boto3 clients and a CHECK_MAP of 25 sections invokes read-only check methods that call self._add to record Results.

architecture_layers.name: Enrichment and scoring
architecture_layers.description: self._add auto-populates severity (CHECK_SEVERITY), compliance controls (COMPLIANCE_MAP), and remediation (REMEDIATION_MAP); compute_risk_score and score_to_grade yield the 0-100 posture score and A-F grade.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting and evidence
architecture_layers.description: Both scanners emit colour console, JSON, and interactive HTML reports; the live scanner additionally writes CSV/JSON evidence artefacts to an output directory.

summary: Azure Security Scanner is a pair of read-only Bash audit scripts that interrogate a live Microsoft Azure subscription and grade its security posture against the CIS Microsoft Azure Foundations Benchmark v4.0.0. Instead of shipping a heavyweight agent or requiring API keys, it rides the operator's existing `az login` session, runs entirely with Azure CLI + Python 3, and never mutates a single resource. Every check emits a color-coded PASS / FAIL / WARN / INFO verdict with the exact CIS control reference, so a security engineer can go from `az login` to a prioritized findings list in one command.

The project ships two tiers. The base script (`azure_security_audit_scripts.sh`) covers 8 sections and ~36 checks spanning IAM, Storage, Networking, Logging, Key Vault, VMs, App Service and Databases. The enhanced script (`azure_security_audit_enhanced.sh`) is a superset: 16 sections and 70+ effective checks that add Microsoft Defender for Cloud plan coverage, Conditional Access / MFA policy inspection via Microsoft Graph, resource locks, NSG flow logs, Azure Bastion, Container Registry, AKS hardening and orphaned-resource hygiene. Sections 1-8 are byte-identical across both scripts so shared logic stays in sync.

Under the hood it's a deliberately simple, dependency-free design: a Bash driver with `log/pass/fail/warn` helper functions and ANSI counters, hybrid Bash-plus-inline-Python (33 `python3` heredocs) for the multi-resource checks, and Entra ID coverage delivered through `az rest` calls to the Microsoft Graph API. Each run creates a timestamped evidence directory holding per-check JSON/CSV artifacts plus an `AUDIT_MANIFEST.txt` catalog — turning an audit into a reproducible, auditor-friendly evidence package.

It's built for cloud security engineers, Azure admins, compliance teams and consultants who need a fast, transparent, offline-inspectable posture check they can read line-by-line before running it against production. Because it's read-only by design, needs only Reader/Global Reader permissions, and hardcodes no secrets, it's safe to drop into a hardening review, a client engagement, or a pre-audit dry run.

azure-scanner.architecture_summary: A single-file Bash driver per tier orchestrates the audit: it resolves Azure context from the active `az login` session, sets up a timestamped evidence directory, then runs sequential section blocks. Simple checks call Azure CLI directly and score via `pass/fail/warn` helpers; complex multi-resource and Entra ID checks execute inline Python heredocs that shell out through `subprocess`, parse JSON, print colored verdicts, and persist evidence files. A summary stage tallies results and writes an evidence manifest.

architecture_layers.name: Context & setup layer
architecture_layers.description: `set -euo pipefail`, `--subscription` arg parsing, `az account show` resolution of subscription/tenant, and creation of the timestamped `OUTPUT_DIR` evidence folder.

architecture_layers.name: Helper / scoring layer
architecture_layers.description: `log()`, `pass()`, `fail()`, `warn()` functions emit ANSI-colored output and auto-increment PASS/FAIL/WARN counters used in the final tally.

architecture_layers.name: Sectioned check engine
architecture_layers.description: 16 labeled `SECTION N: SERVICE` blocks (8 in the base script), each grouping domain checks with consistent headers and CIS control references.

architecture_layers.name: Hybrid Bash + Python execution
architecture_layers.description: Lightweight checks run Azure CLI inline; heavier checks run 33 `python3 - <<'PYEOF'` heredocs using `subprocess` + `json` for multi-resource iteration and logic.

architecture_layers.name: Microsoft Graph query layer
architecture_layers.description: Identity and Conditional Access sections issue `az rest --method GET` calls to the Microsoft Graph API to audit Entra ID policies and role membership.

architecture_layers.name: Evidence & reporting layer
architecture_layers.description: Per-check JSON/CSV files (e.g. `storage_accounts.json`, `defender_pricing.json`) plus a final PASS/FAIL/WARN summary and `AUDIT_MANIFEST.txt` written to OUTPUT_DIR.

summary: GCP CNAPP Security Scanner is a single, offline tool that mimics a modern Cloud-Native Application Protection Platform for Google Cloud, collapsing five distinct security disciplines - CSPM, CIEM, CWPP, KSPM, and IaC scanning - into one Python scanner. Instead of connecting to a live project, it reads standard JSON configuration exports produced by the gcloud CLI, gsutil, bq, and Terraform, then audits them against the CIS Google Cloud Foundation Benchmark v2.0, the CIS GKE Benchmark, and Google Cloud best practices.

The scanner ships 67 concrete checks organized into 15 audit modules spanning IAM and org policy, VPC and firewall exposure, Compute Engine, Cloud Storage, Cloud SQL, logging and monitoring, BigQuery, service-account hygiene, entitlement/least-privilege analysis, GKE hardening, serverless and container security, KMS encryption, and Terraform state misconfiguration. Each finding carries a severity, category, CIS mapping, affected resource list, and specific remediation, and the run rolls up into a self-contained interactive HTML dashboard with a weighted risk score and severity/category breakdowns.

It is built for cloud security engineers, GCP platform teams, auditors, and consultants who need a fast, air-gapped posture review without deploying an agent, paying for a SaaS CNAPP, or granting a third party live access to their environment. Because it runs purely on exported JSON, it is safe for authorized assessments in restricted or regulated environments.

What makes it notable: zero external dependencies (pure Python 3.8+ standard library), a clean data-loader that tolerates alternate export filenames, and honest CNAPP-pillar coverage - the same conceptual surface (misconfiguration, identity risk, workload hardening, Kubernetes posture, and shift-left IaC) that commercial platforms charge for, delivered as ~1,800 lines of readable, auditable code.

features.title: Unified five-pillar CNAPP coverage
features.description: One scanner spans CSPM, CIEM, CWPP, KSPM, and IaC security so posture, identity, workload, Kubernetes, and shift-left checks run from a single command.

features.title: 67 grounded security checks across 15 modules
features.description: Every check has a stable ID (IAM-001, NET-002, GKE-004, KMS-002...), severity, category, and remediation, making findings traceable and repeatable.

features.title: Fully offline, agentless analysis

gcp-scanner.architecture_summary: The scanner is a linear pipeline: a DataLoader ingests exported GCP JSON, 15 auditor engines (each subclassing a common BaseAuditor) evaluate their domain and emit structured findings through a single finding() contract, and a ReportGenerator renders the aggregated findings into an interactive HTML dashboard. All auditors share the same finding schema (id, severity, category, CIS mapping, affected items, remediation), so the CLI can run any subset, filter by severity, and roll everything into one weighted risk report.

architecture_layers.name: Configuration input
architecture_layers.description: Standard JSON exports from gcloud CLI, gsutil, bq, and Terraform state/plan - the offline data the scanner consumes instead of a live API connection.

architecture_layers.name: DataLoader (modules/base.py)
architecture_layers.description: Maps ~32 logical datasets to expected filenames (with alternate-name fallbacks), loads them as UTF-8 JSON, and reports how many config files were found.

architecture_layers.name: BaseAuditor contract
architecture_layers.description: Common base class providing severity constants, baseline access, and a single finding() method that normalizes every result into a consistent schema.

architecture_layers.name: 15 auditor engines
architecture_layers.description: Domain modules split across cspm_core.py (IAM, Network, Compute, Storage), cspm_ciem.py (Database, Logging, BigQuery, CIEM, SA), and cwpp_iac.py (GKE, Serverless, Container, IaC, CIS, KMS).

architecture_layers.name: CLI orchestrator (gcp_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: Argument parsing, MODULE_MAP dispatch, module/severity selection, aggregation, and console summary of CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW counts.

architecture_layers.name: ReportGenerator (modules/report_generator.py)
architecture_layers.description: Computes a weighted risk score and severity/category breakdowns and emits a self-contained interactive HTML dashboard, with JSON fallback if unavailable.

project_structure.description: Main CLI entry point: argument parsing, MODULE_MAP of 15 auditors, orchestration, and summary output.

CWPP/KSPM and shift-left auditors: GKE, Serverless, Container, Terraform IaC, CIS summary, and KMS/Encryption.

security_controls.detail: Service accounts with Owner/Admin roles, primitive basic roles, separation-of-duties conflicts, KMS admin/crypto overlap, missing domain-restricted sharing, and absent Essential Contacts (CIS 1.x).
Default/legacy VPCs, SSH (22) and RDP (3389) open to 0.0.0.0/0, allow-all firewall rules, subnets without Flow Logs or Private Google Access, and load balancers without SSL policy (CIS 3.x).
Default SA with cloud-platform scope, public IP exposure, serial-port access, Shielded VM disabled, project-wide OS Login off, and disks without CMEK (CIS 4.x).

Public GCS buckets, non-uniform bucket-level access, missing logging/retention/CMEK; Cloud SQL public IPs, no SSL enforcement, 0.0.0.0/0 authorized networks, missing backups and security flags; BigQuery broad access and no CMEK (CIS 5.x/6.x/7.x).
Missing audit-log config, insufficient Data Access coverage, no export sinks, no log-based metrics for IAM/firewall/route changes, and no alerting policies (CIS 2.x).

security_controls.area: CIEM entitlements & least privilege (2 modules, 8 checks)
security_controls.detail: Users with excessive roles, custom-role review, external @gmail identities, project-level allUsers/allAuthenticatedUsers, SA keys >90 days, user-managed keys, dead disabled SAs, and excessive impersonation grants.

Legacy ABAC, missing Network Policy, public (non-private) nodes, no Workload Identity, disabled Shielded Nodes, no Binary Authorization, basic-auth API server, and disabled node auto-upgrade (CIS GKE Benchmark).
Publicly invocable Cloud Functions, ALLOW_ALL ingress, default App Engine SA usage, Container Analysis API disabled, and images stuck in legacy gcr.io.
Scans Terraform state for 0.0.0.0/0, allUsers, cloud-platform scope, legacy ABAC, and disabled SSL, and flags destructive resource deletions in plans - shifting detection left before deploy.
Keys without rotation periods, keys with public (allUsers) access, and KMS admin/crypto role overlap (CIS 1.9-1.11).

summary: The MultiCloud Security Audit Tool is a ScoutSuite-inspired, Python-native auditing engine that connects to live AWS, Azure, and GCP accounts through their provider SDKs, evaluates real configuration state against a library of JSON rule files, and renders a single self-contained HTML report with posture scoring, compliance mapping, and remediation playbooks. It replaces per-cloud console clicking and spreadsheet triage with one repeatable command that speaks all three clouds.

The heart of the tool is a declarative rule engine borrowed from the ScoutSuite design philosophy: every security check is a small JSON file that names a data path (with `*` wildcards for iterating resources) and a conditions expression built from 32 leaf operators plus and/or/not logic. Because checks are data, not code, security teams can extend coverage, tune severities, or ship a custom ruleset without touching Python. The collector layer is strictly read-only, so scans never mutate cloud resources.

Findings are turned into decisions rather than noise. Each scan produces a 0-100 posture score with an A-F letter grade, an automatic compliance scorecard across CIS, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOC 2, NIST CSF, DORA, and AWS Well-Architected, and per-finding CLI fix commands that can be exported as a runnable remediation shell script. A YAML exceptions file suppresses false positives auditably (suppressed findings are greyed-out, not hidden).

Built for cloud security engineers, platform teams, and auditors, it is CI/CD-native out of the box: SARIF 2.1.0 feeds GitHub Code Scanning, JUnit XML turns CRITICAL/HIGH findings into pipeline gate failures, and a bundled GitHub Actions workflow scans all three clouds in parallel on every push. A zero-credential demo mode makes the whole pipeline explorable in one command.

features.title: Unified tri-cloud auditing
features.description: One CLI (scout.py) audits live AWS, Azure, and GCP accounts against 98 rules spanning 37 services, using each provider's native SDK/API.

features.title: Declarative JSON rule engine
features.description: Every check is a JSON file with a data path and conditions expression, so coverage can be extended or tuned without writing Python.

features.title: 30+ condition operators with path wildcards
features.description: The condition engine offers 32 leaf operators (null/empty, boolean, equality, string, regex, list, dict, length, network CIDR, date-age) plus and/or/not logic, evaluated over dot-notation paths with `*` resource iteration.

features.title: 0-100 posture score with A-F grade
features.description: A weighted severity model (CRITICAL 40, HIGH 15, MEDIUM 5, LOW 1) produces an at-a-glance posture score and letter grade per scan.

features.title: Automatic compliance scorecard
features.description: Findings are mapped and aggregated to CIS Benchmarks, PCI-DSS, HIPAA, SOC 2, NIST CSF, DORA, and AWS Well-Architected.

features.title: Remediation playbooks
features.description: Per-finding CLI fix commands (56 templates across the three clouds) are embedded in the report and exportable as a runnable bash remediation script.

features.title: Auditable exception management
features.description: A YAML suppressions file silences false positives by rule/resource with a reason and expiry, and suppressed findings appear greyed-out in the report rather than being hidden.

features.title: SARIF 2.1.0 export
features.description: Findings export to SARIF for GitHub Code Scanning, GitLab, or VS Code, with CRITICAL/HIGH mapped to error severity.

features.title: JUnit XML CI gate
features.description: Findings become JUnit test results grouped by provider.service, where CRITICAL/HIGH become failures to break a pipeline.

features.title: Self-contained HTML report
features.description: A single dark-themed HTML file with an SVG posture gauge, compliance cards, service matrix, and collapsible fix-command panels — no server or assets required.

features.title: Zero-credential demo mode
features.description: `scout.py demo` renders a full sample report with built-in findings so the entire pipeline can be evaluated without any cloud access.

features.title: CI/CD-native GitHub Actions workflow
features.description: The bundled scout-scan.yml runs parallel AWS/Azure/GCP scans on push, PR, and schedule, uploading SARIF, JUnit, and HTML artifacts and posting a PR summary.

multicloud-scanner.architecture_summary: The tool is a three-stage pipeline: provider collectors make read-only API calls and build a nested data dict, a ProcessingEngine walks each rule's path (expanding `*` wildcards) and evaluates its conditions expression to emit Findings, and an output layer post-processes findings into scores, compliance rollups, and multiple report formats. Rules are declarative JSON decoupled from collector code, and all three clouds share the same core engine and output modules.

architecture_layers.name: Provider collectors (providers/)
architecture_layers.description: Per-cloud facades (AWSFacade via boto3, AzureFacade via Azure SDK + Microsoft Graph, GCPFacade via the Discovery API) and service fetchers make read-only calls and return a nested data dict shaped as {service: {resource_type: {name: flags}}}.

architecture_layers.name: Ruleset loader (core/rule.py, core/ruleset.py)
architecture_layers.description: Ruleset parses the ruleset JSON, instantiates enabled Rule objects from individual JSON rule files, and resolves _ARG_N_ token substitution and _INCLUDE_ references.

architecture_layers.name: Condition engine (core/conditions.py)
architecture_layers.description: pass_conditions() evaluates a rule's conditions against each item using 32 leaf operators plus and/or/not logic; returns True when an item is flagged.

architecture_layers.name: Processing engine (core/engine.py)
architecture_layers.description: ProcessingEngine.run() walks each rule.path through the data dict (with `*` wildcard expansion and nested indexing), applies conditions per item, and produces a sorted list of Finding objects.

architecture_layers.name: Analytics (core/scoring.py, core/compliance.py, core/exceptions.py)
architecture_layers.description: Computes the 0-100 posture score and A-F grade, aggregates per-framework compliance counts, and applies auditable YAML-based suppressions.

architecture_layers.name: Output layer (output/)
architecture_layers.description: Serializes findings into a self-contained HTML report, SARIF 2.1.0, JUnit XML, JSON, and a bash remediation script from CLI fix-command templates.

architecture_layers.name: CLI orchestrator (scout.py)
architecture_layers.description: Argparse subcommands (demo/aws/azure/gcp) wire provider selection, engine run, exception application, scoring, and all output writers, returning exit codes for CI gating.

project_structure.description: CLI entrypoint with argparse subcommands (demo/aws/azure/gcp) that orchestrates the full scan-to-report pipeline.
Provider-agnostic engine: conditions, rule/ruleset loaders, processing engine, scoring, compliance aggregation, exceptions, and the Finding dataclass.
The condition-evaluation engine implementing the 32 leaf operators plus and/or/not logic and dot-notation field resolution.
Report writers: HTML report generator, SARIF 2.1.0 writer, JUnit XML writer, JSON encoder, and CLI remediation-command generator.
Abstract base classes (BaseProvider, BaseService, Resources) shared by all three cloud providers.
AWS provider — boto3 facade, 16 service fetchers, and 37 JSON rules mapped to CIS AWS Foundations v3.0.
Azure provider — Azure SDK + Microsoft Graph facade, 12 service fetchers, and 33 JSON rules mapped to CIS Azure Foundations v2.0.
GCP provider — Discovery API facade, 9 service fetchers, and 28 JSON rules mapped to CIS GCP Foundations v2.0.
GitHub Actions CI/CD workflow that runs parallel tri-cloud scans and uploads SARIF, JUnit, and HTML artifacts.
Template for the YAML suppressions file used to auditably silence false positives.
Python dependencies grouped by provider (boto3, azure-*, google-* SDKs, pyyaml); demo mode is stdlib-only.
Committed sample HTML report demonstrating the posture gauge, compliance cards, and fix-command panels.

security_controls.detail: IAM (root MFA/access keys, user MFA, weak/no-expiry password policy, stale keys), S3 (public access block, encryption, versioning, logging), EC2 (open SSH/RDP SGs, IMDSv2, unencrypted EBS), RDS (public, unencrypted, no backup/deletion protection), CloudTrail (not logging, not multi-region, no log validation), KMS rotation, GuardDuty, Config, VPC flow logs, Lambda, EKS, ECR, Secrets Manager, and OpenSearch.

Entra ID (MFA gaps, legacy auth, guest users, security defaults), Storage (HTTPS-only, min TLS, public blob, soft delete), Key Vault (purge protection, soft delete, public network, diagnostics), Network (open SSH/RDP), SQL (auditing, threat detection, Entra admin), Defender for Cloud, Monitor log retention, App Service, AKS (RBAC/AAD/network policy), Container Registry, and Cosmos DB.

IAM (primitive roles, service-account admin roles, stale SA keys), Cloud Storage (public access, uniform access, versioning, logging), Compute (public IP, OS Login, serial port, default network/SA, full-API SA scope, open SSH/RDP firewall), Cloud SQL (public IP, SSL required, backups), Logging sinks/audit logs, KMS rotation, GKE, BigQuery, and Cloud Functions.
The 98 rules are graded 16 CRITICAL, 35 HIGH, 41 MEDIUM, and 6 LOW, driving both the posture-score penalties and the CI gate exit codes.
Network operators (ispubliccidr, insubnets/notinsubnets) evaluate CIDR reachability to flag publicly-open security groups, firewall rules, public IPs, and public storage/registry/database endpoints.
Checks enforce encryption at rest (EBS, RDS, S3, OpenSearch, BigQuery CMEK, EKS secrets) and automatic KMS key rotation across AWS, Azure, and GCP.
MFA enforcement, root/privileged account controls, weak password policy, and stale-credential detection via date-age operators (olderthandays) for access keys and service-account keys.
Verifies CloudTrail/Config, Azure Monitor/diagnostics, and GCP audit logging + sinks are enabled and retained so detective controls are actually in place.
YAML suppressions match by rule/resource/service with a reason and expiry; expired or non-matching suppressions never silence a finding, and suppressed items remain visible in reports and SARIF.
All provider fetchers make only read/list/describe API calls and never modify cloud resources, making scans safe to run against production accounts.
