summary: Cloud Detection & Response (CDR) Scanner is a zero-dependency, single-file Python engine that analyses exported cloud audit logs -- AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity/Sign-in, and GCP Cloud Audit -- to surface active threats, backdoors, and misconfigurations mapped to the MITRE ATT&CK Cloud Matrix. Traditional SIEM rulepacks systematically under-cover cloud-native attacks: root console logins, IAM backdoor users, CloudTrail/GuardDuty tampering, public S3/GCS buckets, and cross-account role abuse. CDR closes that gap with a curated catalogue of provider-specific detections that read the raw audit event and reason about it, not just grep for an event name.

Under the hood the scanner auto-detects the cloud provider from each log file's JSON shape, unwraps the many envelope formats each vendor emits (CloudTrail `Records`, Azure `value`/`records`, GCP `entries`), extracts a normalised field set, and evaluates every matching detection rule. Rules go beyond pattern matching by attaching Python lambda condition functions -- so 'ConsoleLogin' only fires a finding when the caller is Root, when MFA was absent, or when the login failed. Each finding carries its MITRE technique IDs, mapped compliance controls, actor, source IP, region, and a concrete remediation recommendation, and is rendered to a coloured console report, machine-parseable JSON, or an interactive dark-themed HTML dashboard.

The whole tool is one portable Python file requiring only the 3.10+ standard library -- no pip installs, no cloud SDKs, no network calls -- so it runs identically on an analyst laptop, an air-gapped forensics box, or a CI runner. A non-zero exit code on CRITICAL/HIGH findings makes it a natural deployment gate.

It is built for cloud security engineers, incident responders, and detection engineers who need a fast, offline, transparent way to triage a bundle of cloud logs after an alert, and for platform teams who want a lightweight guardrail wired into their pipeline.

features.title: Unified multi-cloud detection
features.description: One engine detects threats across AWS CloudTrail, Azure Activity/Sign-in, and GCP Cloud Audit logs, each with its own dedicated rule set and field extractor.

features.title: 60 curated detection rules
features.description: 36 AWS, 14 Azure, and 10 GCP rules spanning nine ATT&CK tactics from Initial Access through destructive Impact.

features.title: Automatic provider detection
features.description: `_detect_cloud` fingerprints each file from its JSON structure (eventSource, operationName, protoPayload) so no provider flag or configuration is ever needed.

features.title: Contextual lambda conditions
features.description: 18 helper functions (e.g. `_ct_mfa_used`, `_ct_cross_account`, `_ct_sg_open`, `_az_global_admin`, `_gcp_public_bucket`) reason about event context instead of matching event names alone, cutting false positives.

features.title: MITRE ATT&CK Cloud Matrix mapping
features.description: Every finding is tagged with ATT&CK technique IDs resolved against a 40-technique reference map, and reports include a per-tactic detection summary.

features.title: Compliance context on every finding
features.description: Findings are annotated with the CIS AWS/Azure/GCP, NIST 800-53, SOC 2, and PCI-DSS controls they violate for audit-ready output.

features.title: Three report formats
features.description: ANSI-coloured console output, indented JSON for SIEM/SOAR ingestion, and a self-contained interactive HTML dashboard -- generated in a single run.

features.title: Interactive HTML dashboard
features.description: Dark-themed report with severity summary cards, a MITRE tactic breakdown, and live client-side severity checkboxes to filter the findings table.

features.title: CI/CD-friendly exit codes
features.description: Returns exit code 1 when any CRITICAL or HIGH finding is present and 0 otherwise, so it can gate a pull request or pipeline stage.

features.title: Zero-dependency single file
features.description: The entire scanner is one ~1,100-line Python file using only the 3.10+ standard library -- portable, auditable, and installable by copying one file.

features.title: Severity model and filtering
features.description: Five-level CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW/INFO scale with a `--severity` floor to focus a scan on only the findings that matter.

features.title: Robust envelope parsing
features.description: Dedicated parsers accept single events, vendor batch envelopes, or raw arrays, and the scanner silently skips malformed files and events so one bad log never aborts a scan.

cdr-scanner.architecture_summary: CDR is a single-file, staged pipeline. Each log file flows through provider auto-detection, an envelope parser, a field extractor, and the rule engine, which evaluates every matching detection's lambda condition to emit Finding dataclasses; those findings are then serialised by three interchangeable reporters. All rules, engine logic, and report templates live in cdr_scanner.py with no external dependencies.

architecture_layers.name: Detection rule catalogue
architecture_layers.description: Module-level `AWS_RULES` (36), `AZURE_RULES` (14), and `GCP_RULES` (10) dicts plus the `MITRE_MAP` (40 techniques) and `COMPLIANCE_MAP` (7 frameworks) lookup tables that drive every finding's metadata.

architecture_layers.name: Condition helpers
architecture_layers.description: Cloud-scoped lambda functions prefixed `_ct_` (AWS), `_az_` (Azure), and `_gcp_` (GCP) that perform contextual analysis -- MFA checks, cross-account role math, 0.0.0.0/0 exposure, public-ACL detection, GPU-instance cryptomining heuristics.

architecture_layers.name: Parsers & auto-detection
architecture_layers.description: `_parse_cloudtrail`, `_parse_azure_log`, `_parse_gcp_log` unwrap vendor JSON envelopes, and `_detect_cloud` fingerprints the provider from log structure before routing.

architecture_layers.name: Field extractors
architecture_layers.description: `_extract_aws_fields`, `_extract_azure_fields`, `_extract_gcp_fields` normalise each provider's schema into a common event_time / event_name / actor / source_ip / region shape consumed by the rule engine.

architecture_layers.name: CDRScanner engine
architecture_layers.description: Orchestrates `scan_path` -> `_scan_directory` -> `_scan_file` -> `_analyse_events` -> `_add_finding`, matching event names, running conditions, and accumulating findings with cloud and tactic tallies.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting layer
architecture_layers.description: `print_report` (coloured console with tactic summary), `save_json` (structured export), and `save_html` (interactive dark dashboard) render the same Finding list three ways.

architecture_layers.name: CLI & exit gate
architecture_layers.description: An `argparse` front end exposes target/--json/--html/--severity/--verbose/--version and converts CRITICAL/HIGH findings into a non-zero exit status.

project_structure.description: The entire scanner -- rules, condition helpers, parsers, extractors, CDRScanner engine, three reporters, and CLI in one ~1,100-line stdlib file.
Full documentation: rule tables per cloud, MITRE and compliance mappings, quickstart, CLI reference, supported log formats, and CI examples.

security_controls.detail: Detects AWS root console logins, console logins without MFA, brute-force login failures, and API calls from unusual regions; Azure risky sign-ins, failed sign-in bursts, and MFA-absent logins.

Flags new IAM users, access keys, login profiles, Lambda functions and EC2 key pairs on AWS; new/guest users, app credentials and OAuth consent grants on Azure; and service-account and SA-key creation on GCP.

Catches AdministratorAccess and wildcard (Action:*/Resource:*) IAM policy attachments, inline policies, and cross-account role assumption on AWS; Global Administrator and privileged directory-role assignments on Azure; roles/owner and roles/editor and custom-role grants on GCP.

Detects CloudTrail stopped or deleted, GuardDuty disabled, Config recorder stopped, and VPC flow logs deleted on AWS; Conditional Access and Defender disabled and diagnostic settings deleted on Azure; audit sink and firewall-rule deletion on GCP.
Surfaces Secrets Manager retrieval, SSM parameter access, and password-policy weakening on AWS, mapped to T1552 Unsecured Credentials and T1556 Modify Authentication Process.
Identifies enumeration and infrastructure-discovery API patterns and S3 bucket listing, mapped to T1580 Cloud Infrastructure Discovery and T1619 Cloud Storage Object Discovery.
Detects S3 buckets and GCS buckets made public (public-read / allUsers / allAuthenticatedUsers), EBS snapshots shared with all, and RDS snapshots shared externally (T1530, T1537).
Flags security groups and firewall rules opened to 0.0.0.0/0 or ::/0, deleted SG rules, VPC modification on AWS, NSG any-source rules on Azure, and open firewalls on GCP.

Catches EC2 termination, RDS/database deletion, S3/bucket and resource-group deletion, KMS key deletion or destruction, and Key Vault secret deletion across all three clouds (T1485 Data Destruction, T1578.003).
`_ct_crypto_instance` flags launches of GPU/accelerator and oversized instance families (p3/p4/p5/g4dn/g5/inf/trn and 24xlarge/32xlarge) as likely resource hijacking (T1496).

summary: Microsoft 365 is the identity and collaboration backbone of most enterprises, yet its security posture is spread across a dozen admin portals — Entra ID, Conditional Access, Exchange, SharePoint, Teams, Intune, Defender, and Purview — each with its own settings that quietly drift out of policy. SSPM-O365 is an open-source, read-only SaaS Security Posture Management scanner that connects directly to a tenant through the Microsoft Graph API and evaluates 88 security rules across 24 check groups in a single pass, turning that scattered configuration surface into one prioritized, compliance-mapped findings report.

Unlike a checklist or a screenshot audit, the scanner performs a live assessment: it authenticates with OAuth 2.0 client-credentials (app-only, no user interaction), pages through Graph v1.0 and beta endpoints, and inspects the real state of MFA enforcement, Conditional Access, privileged roles, OAuth app consent, external sharing, guest access, audit logging, Identity Protection risk, Secure Score, and more. Every rule carries a severity, a CWE weakness class, the exact Graph endpoint it checked, a plain-English issue statement, and a concrete remediation step.

It is deliberately lightweight and safe to run against production. The entire tool is a single ~3,770-line Python file with one dependency (requests), makes only GET requests, and degrades gracefully when a permission or endpoint is unavailable. Findings are emitted to the terminal, JSON, or a self-contained filterable HTML report, and the process exits non-zero on CRITICAL/HIGH findings so it drops straight into a CI/CD gate.

The audience is M365 administrators, security engineers, and GRC teams who need continuous, evidence-backed assurance rather than a point-in-time consultant review. Because every one of the 88 rules is pre-mapped to CIS Microsoft 365 Foundations v3.1.0, NIST SP 800-53 Rev 5, ISO/IEC 27001:2022, and SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria, a single scan doubles as a compliance evidence artifact across four frameworks at once.

features.title: Live Graph API scanning
features.description: Authenticates via OAuth 2.0 client credentials and queries Microsoft Graph v1.0 and beta endpoints to assess the tenant's real, current configuration rather than a static export.

features.title: 88 rules across 24 check groups
features.description: Covers the full M365 stack — Entra ID, Conditional Access, MFA, privileged access, apps, guests, Exchange, SharePoint, OneDrive, Teams, audit, Intune, DLP, Defender, and governance — in one run.

features.title: Four-framework compliance mapping
features.description: Every finding is auto-enriched from a COMPLIANCE_MAP with its CIS M365 v3.1.0, NIST 800-53, ISO 27001:2022, and SOC 2 control IDs, so a scan is also an audit-evidence artifact.

features.title: Read-only and production-safe
features.description: Requires only Graph read permissions and issues exclusively GET requests, so it can never modify tenant state or lock users out.

features.title: Severity-ranked, CWE-tagged findings
features.description: Each result carries CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW severity plus a CWE weakness class, a specific issue statement, and an actionable fix.

features.title: Multi-format reporting
features.description: Emits colorized terminal output, machine-readable JSON, and a self-contained dark-themed HTML report with severity counters, category/severity filters, and free-text search.

features.title: CI/CD-friendly exit codes
features.description: Returns exit code 1 when any CRITICAL or HIGH finding exists, making it a drop-in pipeline gate with a documented GitHub Actions example.

features.title: Graceful permission degradation
features.description: When a Graph endpoint returns 403/404 the scanner logs a verbose skip and continues, so a partial permission set still yields useful results instead of a crash.

features.title: Zero-install footprint
features.description: A single Python file with requests as its only dependency — no agents, no cloud services, no packaging — for trivial deployment and review.

features.title: 'Verify:' findings for PowerShell-only settings
features.description: Where Graph cannot directly read a setting, the tool emits a Verify finding embedding the exact Exchange/PowerShell command to confirm the control manually.

features.title: Offline demo mode
features.description: run_demo.py injects synthetic findings across all categories to render sample terminal/JSON/HTML reports with no tenant or credentials required.

m365-scanner.architecture_summary: SSPM-O365 is a single self-contained Python script organized as a linear scan pipeline: authenticate, run 24 check-group methods that each call paginated Graph helpers, collect Finding objects that auto-enrich themselves with compliance metadata, then render to three output formats. There are no modules or packages by design — the single-file layout is an intentional deployment feature.

architecture_layers.name: Reference data & compliance map
architecture_layers.description: Module-level dicts — PRIVILEGED_ROLE_IDS (Entra role GUIDs), HIGH_RISK_PERMISSION_IDS (dangerous OAuth GUIDs), and COMPLIANCE_MAP (88 rule IDs to CIS/NIST/ISO/SOC2 controls) — that drive detection and enrichment.

architecture_layers.name: Authentication layer
architecture_layers.description: _authenticate() runs the OAuth 2.0 client-credentials flow against login.microsoftonline.com, caches the bearer token with expiry, and resolves the tenant display name.

architecture_layers.name: Graph API access layer
architecture_layers.description: _graph_get() and _graph_get_single() wrap Microsoft Graph v1.0 and beta endpoints with pagination, headers, and 403/404 graceful-skip error handling.

architecture_layers.name: Check-group engine
architecture_layers.description: 24 self-contained _check_*() methods (security defaults, CA, MFA, privileged access, apps, guests, Exchange, SharePoint, OneDrive, Teams, audit, identity protection, secure score, Intune, DLP, Defender, sessions, cross-tenant, consent, auth strengths, governance, named locations, stale users) invoked in order by scan().

architecture_layers.name: Finding model
architecture_layers.description: The Finding data class captures rule_id, severity, the Graph endpoint checked, setting/value context, CWE, description, and recommendation, and auto-populates its compliance dict from COMPLIANCE_MAP on init.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting layer
architecture_layers.description: print_report(), save_json(), and save_html() render the collected findings to terminal, JSON, and a filterable self-contained HTML report, all including per-finding compliance data.

architecture_layers.name: CLI entry point
architecture_layers.description: main() parses arguments (tenant/client/secret, severity filter, json/html output, verbose) with argparse, supports M365_* env-var fallback, and sets the CRITICAL/HIGH exit code.

security_controls.detail: M365-CA (9 rules) and M365-MFA (4 rules) verify an enforced all-users MFA policy, legacy-auth blocking, risky sign-in and device-compliance policies, location policies, admin MFA, SMS-only MFA, and SSPR/MFA mismatch.
M365-PRIV (5 rules) flags excessive Global Admins, guest and service-principal admins, missing PIM, and break-glass account gaps using the PRIVILEGED_ROLE_IDS role map.

M365-APP (5) and M365-CONSENT (3) detect high-risk Graph permissions via HIGH_RISK_PERMISSION_IDS, expired/expiring secrets, secret-vs-certificate use, app sprawl, unrestricted user consent, and unverified-publisher apps.
M365-AUTH (3 rules) audits phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/CBA) enforcement, authentication-strength binding in CA policies, and detection of weak SMS/Voice methods.
M365-IDP (5 rules) surfaces high/medium-risk users, missing sign-in and user-risk policies, and risky service principals via the Identity Protection API.

M365-EXO (4), M365-SPO (4), M365-OD (3), and M365-TEAMS (3) check external mail forwarding, SMTP AUTH and legacy protocols, anonymous/external sharing links, default link type, OneDrive sync restrictions, and Teams anonymous meetings and external federation.

M365-INTUNE (4), M365-DLP (3), and M365-MDO (4) evaluate compliance-policy platform coverage and stale/non-compliant devices, sensitivity-label and DLP coverage, and Defender for Office 365 anti-phishing, Safe Links, Safe Attachments, and ZAP.

M365-AUDIT (3), M365-SESSION (3), and M365-XTA (3) confirm Unified Audit Log and mailbox auditing, sign-in frequency and persistent-browser token policies, and cross-tenant MFA/device-trust and outbound B2B settings.

M365-GOV (3), M365-LOC (3), and M365-STALE (3) check Access Reviews and Entitlement Management, missing or overly broad named locations, and inactive 90/180-day, never-signed-in, and disabled-but-privileged accounts.

M365-SEC (2), M365-PWD (3), M365-GUEST (4), and M365-SCORE (2) assess Security Defaults/CA coexistence, password-expiry policy, guest invitation and staleness controls, and Microsoft Secure Score with unresolved high-impact improvement actions.

summary: ServiceNow is the connective tissue of enterprise IT: it holds identities, roles, workflows, integration credentials, and the change records that run the business. A single misconfigured system property or an over-scoped OAuth client can quietly expose all of it. SSPM-ServiceNow is an open-source SaaS Security Posture Management scanner that audits a live ServiceNow instance for exactly these misconfigurations, without installing an agent, plugin, or scoped app.

The tool talks to the standard ServiceNow Table REST API using read-only Basic Auth credentials, batch-fetches the security-relevant system properties in a single call, then runs 47 distinct checks across 13 posture domains: XSS, CSRF, session management, authentication (lockout, password, Basic Auth, SSO, MFA), password policy, file-upload safety, transport security, server-side script sandboxing, audit logging, user/privileged-access hygiene, OAuth client hygiene, ACL exposure, and email security. Findings are severity-ranked (CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW), each mapped to a CWE, and rendered to the console, machine-readable JSON, and a self-contained interactive HTML report.

It is deliberately lightweight and safe: one Python file, one external dependency (requests), and read-only queries mean a security engineer can point it at a dev, test, or production instance in minutes and get a prioritized remediation list. Non-zero exit codes on CRITICAL/HIGH findings make it drop-in for CI/CD posture gates.

The check set is grounded in Palo Alto Networks' SSPM for ServiceNow, CIS ServiceNow best practices, and OWASP guidance, making it a practical, auditable posture baseline for SecOps teams, ServiceNow platform admins, and GRC functions who need continuous assurance rather than a once-a-year manual review.

features.title: Agentless live REST scanning
features.description: Audits a real ServiceNow instance through the standard Table API with read-only Basic Auth credentials, so no plugin, MID Server, or scoped app has to be installed.

features.title: Declarative system-property rule engine
features.description: 28 sys_properties checks are expressed as data with reusable operators (eq_true/eq_false/lte_int/gte_int/not_empty) and a missing_is_fail flag that models ServiceNow's safe defaults.

features.title: 13-domain posture coverage
features.description: Spans XSS, CSRF, sessions, authentication, passwords, file uploads, transport, script sandboxing, audit logging, users, OAuth, ACLs, and email in a single pass.

features.title: Identity and privileged-access checks
features.description: Flags an active default admin account, stale accounts idle 90+ days, maint-role holders, admins without MFA, service accounts holding admin, and security_admin sprawl.

features.title: OAuth integration hygiene
features.description: Inspects registered oauth_entity clients for wildcard/admin scope, access tokens living beyond one hour, never-used-but-active clients, and overall client sprawl.

features.title: ACL exposure detection
features.description: Queries sys_acl for role-less read/write rules on sys_user, core sys_ tables, and log tables that would let any session read or modify sensitive data.

features.title: CWE-mapped findings
features.description: Every finding carries a CWE reference (e.g. CWE-79, CWE-352, CWE-613, CWE-434, CWE-269) to slot cleanly into existing vulnerability and risk workflows.

features.title: Multi-format reporting
features.description: Emits a color-coded console report, indented JSON for pipelines, and a dark-themed self-contained HTML report with live severity/category/text filtering.

features.title: CI/CD posture gating
features.description: Returns exit code 1 whenever any CRITICAL or HIGH finding remains after severity filtering, so it can fail a build or scheduled posture check.

features.title: Secret-friendly configuration
features.description: Accepts instance, username, and password via CLI flags or SNOW_INSTANCE / SNOW_USERNAME / SNOW_PASSWORD environment variables to keep credentials out of shell history.

features.title: Resilient API client
features.description: Auto-paginates at 500 records per page and degrades gracefully on 401/403/404/timeout so missing plugins or tables are skipped rather than crashing the scan.

features.title: Offline demo mode
features.description: run_demo.py injects synthetic findings to produce sample JSON and HTML reports without any live connection, ideal for demos and report-format previews.

servicenow-scanner.architecture_summary: A single-file Python scanner organized as a pipeline: a CLI/config layer resolves credentials, a resilient Table-API client batch-fetches the relevant sys_properties and per-table records, a declarative rule engine plus a set of API-driven check modules evaluate them into normalized Finding objects, and a reporting layer renders console, JSON, and interactive HTML output.

architecture_layers.name: CLI & configuration
architecture_layers.description: argparse front-end (main) that resolves --instance/--username/--password or SNOW_* env vars, validates required credentials, normalizes short instance names to full service-now.com URLs, and sets severity/output options.

architecture_layers.name: REST API client
architecture_layers.description: _api_get performs authenticated GETs against /api/now/table/{table} with automatic 500-record pagination and graceful 401/403/404/timeout handling; _fetch_properties batch-loads all rule properties via a single nameIN query.

architecture_layers.name: Declarative property rule engine
architecture_layers.description: PROPERTY_RULES (28 rules) plus _check_system_properties / _evaluate_property apply operator + threshold + missing_is_fail logic against cached sys_properties values.

architecture_layers.name: API-driven check modules
architecture_layers.description: Dedicated methods scan live records: _check_users, _check_oauth_apps, _check_acls, _check_audit_logging, _check_email_security, and _check_file_attachments (blocklist diffing against DANGEROUS_EXTENSIONS).

architecture_layers.name: Finding model
architecture_layers.description: A single Finding class provides a normalized schema (id, name, category, severity, table, context, description, recommendation, CWE) shared with the author's other scanners for consistent tooling.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting layer
architecture_layers.description: summary/filter_severity/print_report/save_json/save_html produce a color console report, indented JSON, and a self-contained dark-themed HTML report with client-side filtering.

security_controls.detail: Verifies glide.ui.escape_text, escape_html_text_area, AntiSamy HTML sanitization, and an active Content Security Policy so user content cannot be rendered as executable HTML (CWE-79/CWE-1021).

Checks glide.security.use_csrf_token, HTTPS redirect enforcement (glide.ui.https_redirect), and the SameSite cookie attribute to close cross-site request forgery and cleartext-session vectors (CWE-352/CWE-319/CWE-1275).
Enforces a <=30-minute idle timeout, bounded active/guest session lifespans, session-ID rotation after login (session fixation), and concurrent-session control (CWE-613/CWE-384).
Flags high login lockout thresholds, blank-password acceptance, Basic-Auth-for-scripted-APIs, unenforced SSO, and disabled platform MFA (CWE-307/CWE-521/CWE-287/CWE-308).
Seven checks on minimum length (NIST SP 800-63B), upper/lower/digit/special complexity, application-level lockout count, and password-history depth (CWE-521/CWE-307).

Detects an active default admin account, accounts idle 90+ days, maint-role backdoors, admins without MFA enrolled, service accounts holding admin, and security_admin sprawl (CWE-798/CWE-269/CWE-308).
Reviews oauth_entity clients for admin/wildcard scope, access-token lifetimes over one hour, never-used active clients, and total active-client sprawl (CWE-269/CWE-284/CWE-613).
Queries sys_acl for role-less read on sys_user, role-less write on core sys_ tables, and unrestricted log-table reads that enable data exposure and tampering (CWE-284/CWE-532).

Validates MIME-type checking and the attachment blocklist against a curated dangerous-extension set (exe/bat/ps1/jar/scr...), plus inbound-email antivirus scanning and address filtering (CWE-434/CWE-200).
Confirms platform audit logging, an active syslog/SIEM probe, adequate log retention, server-side script sandboxing, and restricted dynamic-form generation (CWE-778/CWE-250/CWE-20).

summary: SAP SuccessFactors is one of the world's most widely deployed HR SaaS platforms, holding a company's most sensitive data: employee PII, compensation, org structure, and payroll feeds. Yet its security posture is governed by a sprawl of RBP permission roles, OAuth clients, Integration Center jobs, SAML/MFA settings, and data-retention rules that no single admin screen presents holistically. This project is a purpose-built SaaS Security Posture Management (SSPM) toolkit that audits exactly that surface.

It ships as two complementary, standalone scanners. The Offline Scanner is a modular, zero-dependency (Python stdlib only) tool that ingests exported CSV/JSON configuration data and runs 49 checks across 7 audit modules, producing an interactive HTML dashboard with severity ratings and remediation. The Online Scanner is a single-file tool that connects live to a running instance over the standard SAP SuccessFactors OData v2 REST API using read-only credentials, running 35 posture checks across 8 categories with no agent or plugin to install.

The dual-mode design maps directly to how real assessments happen: use the Offline Scanner for air-gapped reviews, deep RBP/permission analysis from exports, or when you only have data dumps; use the Online Scanner when you hold read-only API credentials and want a real-time posture snapshot of password policy, users, SSO, audit logging, privacy, and integrations. Both are strictly non-destructive, both emit severity-ranked findings with remediation, and both exit non-zero on CRITICAL/HIGH findings so they drop cleanly into CI or assessment pipelines.

It is built for HR-security and GRC teams, SAP administrators, and offensive/defensive security consultants who need SuccessFactors-specific coverage that generic SaaS scanners lack. Findings are grounded in concrete controls (RBP over-privilege, SoD conflicts, terminated-employee access, OAuth scope sprawl, GDPR purge jobs, audit-log gaps) and annotated with CWE identifiers and framework alignment rather than vague scores.

features.title: Dual-mode scanning (offline + online)
features.description: One toolkit covers both air-gapped config-export review and live OData API scanning, so you can audit whether or not you have instance credentials.

features.title: Zero-dependency offline auditor
features.description: The offline scanner runs on Python 3.8+ stdlib alone, making it trivial to drop into a locked-down or air-gapped assessment environment.

features.title: Agentless live OData v2 scanning

features.title: Deep RBP and Segregation-of-Duties analysis

features.title: Terminated and dormant account detection
features.description: Flags terminated employees who still hold active accounts, dormant users inactive 90+ days, and service accounts with hygiene issues before they become an attack path.

features.title: Integration and OAuth security checks

features.title: Data privacy and GDPR coverage

features.title: Interactive HTML dashboards

features.title: CWE-annotated, framework-aligned findings

successfactors-scanner.architecture_summary: The toolkit is two independent scanners sharing a common finding/severity model. The offline scanner is a thin CLI orchestrator that loads exported CSV/JSON via a DataLoader, then dispatches to seven BaseAuditor subclasses (one per domain) that each emit structured finding dicts, which a ReportGenerator renders into an interactive HTML dashboard. The online scanner is a single-file OData v2 client that authenticates with read-only Basic Auth (username@companyId), pages through nine SuccessFactors entities, and runs eight check groups (including 10 data-driven password-policy rules) that produce Finding objects rendered to console, JSON, and HTML.

architecture_layers.name: CLI / orchestration layer
architecture_layers.description: successfactors_offlinescanner.py and successfactors_onlinescanner.py parse args/env vars, select modules and severity thresholds, run checks, and set CI exit codes.

architecture_layers.name: Data ingestion layer
architecture_layers.description: Offline: modules/data_loader.py loads exported CSV/JSON from --data-dir. Online: the _api_get OData v2 helper fetches entities with $format=json and $top/$skip pagination, handling 401/403/404 gracefully.

architecture_layers.name: Audit engine (offline)
architecture_layers.description: Seven BaseAuditor subclasses (rbp_permissions, auth_provisioning, sso_auth, api_integration, data_privacy, instance_security, audit_logging) implement run_all_checks and emit severity-rated finding dicts.

architecture_layers.name: Check engine (online)
architecture_layers.description: SuccessFactorsScanner runs eight _check_* groups over nine OData entities; password policy is driven by a PASSWORD_POLICY_RULES table with typed operators (gte_int, eq_true, non_zero, lte_days, etc.).

architecture_layers.name: Finding model
architecture_layers.description: A shared finding structure (offline dict via BaseAuditor.finding, online Finding class) carries id, severity, category, description, remediation, CWE, and affected items.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting layer
architecture_layers.description: modules/report_generator.py builds the offline HTML dashboard (severity + category charts); the online scanner renders coloured console, JSON, and self-contained HTML.

project_structure.description: Offline scanner CLI entry point; orchestrates DataLoader, the 7 auditor modules, severity filtering, and report generation.
Single-file live OData v2 API scanner (~1,599 lines); auth, entity fetch, 8 check groups, and console/JSON/HTML output.
BaseAuditor class defining the severity constants and the finding() helper shared by all offline modules.
Loads exported CSV/JSON configuration files from the data directory; every input file is optional.
RBP-* checks: over-privileged roles, admin sprawl, SoD conflicts, sensitive-data and wildcard-population access (7 checks).
SSO-* checks: SAML config weaknesses, MFA enforcement, IP restrictions, session timeout, certificate health, brute-force patterns (9 checks).
API-* checks: OAuth scope/grant/idle, sensitive OData entity exposure, Integration Center auth/TLS, broad API access (8 checks).
PRIV-* checks: field masking, data retention/purge, MDF PII classification, picklist and bulk-export controls (8 checks).
748-line HTML dashboard generator with severity/category breakdowns and per-finding remediation.
22 demo config files (CSV/JSON) plus a sample_report.html so the offline scanner runs out of the box.
Project instructions documenting both scanners, rule-ID formats, and architecture.
Full usage, module/check catalogues, data-source reference, and DC host map.

security_controls.detail: 7 checks (RBP-001..007): roles with >50 permissions, excessive admins, manage/full access to comp/PII/payroll, wildcard all-employee populations, SoD conflicts (role-admin+user-admin, export+PII), manage access to critical platform objects, and unused roles.

7 checks (AUTH-*): missing/weak password policy, dormant accounts (90+ days), users with no roles, SCIM/auto-deprovision gaps, service-account hygiene, and CRITICAL terminated-employees-with-active-accounts.

9 offline checks (SSO-*): no SAML, unsigned/IDP-initiated assertion weaknesses, missing/unenforced MFA, SMS/bypass allowed, absent or overly permissive IP restrictions, long session timeouts, expiring/expired certificates, and brute-force login patterns.

8 checks (API-*): broad OAuth scopes, deprecated password/implicit grants, OAuth idle 180+ days, sensitive OData write/manage exposure, unauthenticated Integration Center jobs, sensitive-HR-data export, non-TLS jobs, and roles with >30-entity API access.

8 checks (PRIV-*): missing field masking, uncovered/explicitly-unmasked PII, no retention/purge policy, excessive retention, unclassified custom MDF PII, unrestricted sensitive picklists, and unrestricted bulk export.

4 instance checks (INST-*: HSTS/CSP/X-Frame-Options, provisioning access, debug/preview-sync risk) plus 6 audit-logging checks (LOG-*: logging disabled, event-coverage gaps, <365-day retention, no SIEM export, no alert rules).

Data-driven PASSWORD_POLICY_RULES (SF-PWD-000..010): minimum length, upper/lower/number/symbol complexity, expiry, history, lockout threshold, and temp-password TTL evaluated against the live PasswordPolicy entity, each mapped to CWE-521.

SF-USER-001..005 and SF-ROLE-001..002: super-admin count, never-logged-in admins, stale accounts, service accounts, broad-scoped roles, and excessive role count queried live from the User and PermissionRole entities.

SF-LOG/PRIV/INT groups: audit enablement and retention, GDPR PersonalDataPurgeJob and ConsentManagementConfig status, sensitive-field masking, OAuth client sprawl, admin-scope clients, token lifetime, and non-HTTPS integration flows.

Online findings are tagged with 14 distinct CWEs including CWE-521 (weak password), CWE-269 (improper privilege management), CWE-613 (insufficient session expiration), CWE-778 (insufficient logging), CWE-319 (cleartext transmission), and CWE-287/284/307/308/312/359/640.

summary: Tableau Cloud SSPM is a read-only SaaS Security Posture Management scanner purpose-built for Tableau Cloud. It connects to a live site over the Tableau REST API using a scoped Personal Access Token, harvests the full configuration surface (users, groups, projects, data sources, workbooks, flows, schedules, and site settings), and evaluates 45 security controls spanning five domains. The result is a severity-weighted posture score from 0 to 100, plus machine-readable JSON and a dark-themed HTML report with per-finding evidence and concrete remediation steps.

BI platforms are a blind spot for most security teams: Tableau Cloud holds curated views of the organization's most sensitive data (finance, HR, PII), yet its configuration drifts as admins, creators, and embedded credentials accumulate. This scanner turns that opaque surface into an auditable posture. It catches the failures that actually lead to exposure -- users bypassing IdP federation, embedded database credentials, unencrypted extracts, over-privileged 'All Users' grants, stale and service accounts, and unlocked default project permissions -- and grades them on a weighted scale so the highest-impact gaps float to the top.

It is designed for cloud security engineers, GRC teams, and Tableau site administrators who need repeatable, evidence-backed access reviews rather than one-off manual audits. Because it is strictly read-only and driven entirely by a PAT, it is safe to run against production and slots naturally into a scheduled GitHub Actions pipeline with score-gating (fail the build if posture drops below a threshold).

The codebase is deliberately small and legible: a three-phase collect -> analyze -> report pipeline, a modular per-domain check architecture built on an abstract base class, 58 unit tests with mock Tableau fixtures, and a CI matrix (Python 3.10-3.12) enforcing lint, bandit, pip-audit, and an 80% coverage gate. No fabricated capabilities -- every control maps to a real REST API field the collector actually pulls.

features.title: 45 controls across 5 security domains
features.description: Evaluates Identity & Authentication, Access Control & Permissions, Data Security, API & Integrations, and Logging & Monitoring -- nine controls each, all grounded in real Tableau REST API fields.

features.title: Severity-weighted posture score (0-100)
features.description: A single defensible number derived from CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW weights (25/15/8/3), where PASS earns full weight, WARN earns 50%, and FAIL earns zero -- so noisy low-severity items can't mask a critical gap.

features.title: Strictly read-only, PAT-authenticated
features.description: Uses a Personal Access Token and only issues GET requests against nine endpoints, so it is safe to run against production without ever modifying the Tableau environment.

features.title: Evidence-rich JSON + dark-themed HTML reports
features.description: Every finding ships with details, a why-it-matters description, remediation guidance, and a capped list of the exact offending users/resources for immediate triage.

features.title: Per-domain category scoring
features.description: Breaks the overall score into per-domain percentages (Identity, Access, Data, API, Logging) so teams can see exactly which area is dragging posture down.

features.title: CI/CD score gating
features.description: The --min-score flag and CRITICAL-failure detection drive the process exit code, letting a pipeline fail automatically when posture regresses below a threshold.

features.title: Resilient REST collection
features.description: The collector wraps every API call in exponential-backoff retry with configurable connect/read timeouts, tolerating transient Tableau Cloud errors during large-site pagination.

features.title: Modular, extensible check suites
features.description: Each domain is a suite subclassing a BaseChecks ABC with an _add() helper, so adding a new control is a single method plus a test -- no changes to the orchestrator.

features.title: Service account and admin-sprawl detection
features.description: Pattern-matches service-account naming, flags admins on local auth, and grades site-role distribution against least-privilege ratios to surface privilege creep.

features.title: Containerized and automation-ready
features.description: Ships a SHA-pinned, non-root Dockerfile with HEALTHCHECK, a Makefile of dev targets, and a ready-to-use scheduled GitHub Actions workflow.

tableau-scanner.architecture_summary: The scanner is a linear three-phase pipeline -- Collect, Analyze, Score & Report -- wired together by a small CLI. Phase 1 (collector.py) authenticates via PAT and pulls the site's configuration into a single nested dict using the tableauserverclient SDK. Phase 2 (checks/ package) hands that dict to a SecurityChecks orchestrator that delegates to five per-domain suites, each subclassing a BaseChecks ABC and emitting Finding dataclasses. Phase 3 (scoring.py + report.py) computes the severity-weighted score and renders JSON plus a self-contained HTML report.

architecture_layers.name: CLI orchestrator (cli.py)
architecture_layers.description: argparse entrypoint with env-var fallback; wires the three phases, configures logging, prints the console summary, and sets the exit code from CRITICAL failures and --min-score.

architecture_layers.name: Collection layer (collector.py)
architecture_layers.description: TableauCollector signs in with a PersonalAccessTokenAuth PAT and pulls users, groups, projects, data sources (with connections), workbooks, schedules, flows, and site settings via paginated, retry-wrapped GET calls.

architecture_layers.name: Analysis layer (checks/ package)
architecture_layers.description: SecurityChecks.run_all() iterates five suites -- IdentityChecks, AccessControlChecks, DataSecurityChecks, APIChecks, LoggingChecks -- each running nine _check_* methods that record Findings through the BaseChecks._add() helper.

architecture_layers.name: Domain model (models.py)
architecture_layers.description: Shared Finding and ScanResult dataclasses plus Severity, Status, and Category enums and the SEVERITY_WEIGHT map that every downstream stage depends on.

architecture_layers.name: Scoring engine (scoring.py)
architecture_layers.description: calculate_score() folds findings into total/earned weight buckets to produce the overall 0-100 posture score and a per-category score dict.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting layer (report.py)
architecture_layers.description: Serializes the ScanResult to indented JSON and renders a dependency-free dark-themed HTML report via an embedded Jinja2 template.

project_structure.description: The five domain suites (identity, access, data, api, logging_checks) plus base.py ABC and __init__.py orchestrator -- all 45 controls live here.
Tableau REST API collector: PAT sign-in, paginated data pull across nine endpoints, exponential-backoff retry.
Severity-weighted posture score and per-category score calculation.
JSON serializer and embedded-Jinja2 dark-themed HTML report generator.
Finding/ScanResult dataclasses and Severity/Status/Category enums with the SEVERITY_WEIGHT map.
argparse CLI entrypoint with env-var fallback, console UI, and exit-code logic.
58 unit tests (test_checks.py, test_scoring.py) with conftest.py mock Tableau fixtures.
ci.yml (ruff lint, pytest matrix, bandit, pip-audit) and scan.yml (scheduled SSPM scan).
SHA-pinned, non-root image with HEALTHCHECK for containerized scans.
A committed sample JSON + HTML report pair demonstrating real output.
Offline demo that runs the full pipeline against mock data without a live Tableau connection.
Package config plus ruff/mypy/pytest/coverage tool settings and the tableau-sspm entrypoint.

security_controls.detail: IdP federation (SAML/OIDC) enforcement with break-glass tolerance, stale/never-logged-in account detection (>90 days), site-admin count and duplicate-admin sprawl, auth-method consistency, external/cross-domain users, site-role distribution health, and service-account discovery.

Over-privileged Creator accounts, insecure default project permissions, permission-lock (ManagedByOwner) enforcement, guest access, group-based access adoption, dangerous 'All Users' group scope, derived permissions, project-hierarchy depth, and single-owner content concentration.

Embedded database credential auditing (CRITICAL), extract encryption at rest, sensitive-data naming (salary/PHI/HIPAA/PII keywords), certification coverage, download surface area, stale sources, Bridge/remote-query-agent usage, multi-connection sources, and uncertified sources in sensitive projects.

Dashboard extension allowlisting, revision-history change tracking, subscribe-others and run-now controls, Prep flow security review, content sprawl, stale-flow detection, data-driven alert exposure, and Ask Data / natural-language mode.

Tableau Catalog (Data Management) enablement, user-visibility controls, commenting/mention controls, access-review readiness, orphaned-content detection, governed access-request workflow, admin-mode configuration, content-ownership distribution, and site activation state.
Each control is weighted CRITICAL=25, HIGH=15, MEDIUM=8, LOW=3, INFO=0; PASS earns full weight, WARN 50%, FAIL 0%, SKIP is excluded -- yielding both an overall and per-domain 0-100 score.
Only GET requests are issued across nine REST resources; the collector performs no writes and the PAT requires only read-level Site Administrator scope.

summary: SAP S/4HANA RISE Security Scanner is an offline, agentless security config-review tool for SAP S/4HANA RISE and BTP landscapes. Instead of connecting to the system, it ingests configuration you export from SAP as CSV/JSON (user master, AGR_1251 role content, profile parameters, RFC destinations, HANA system views, BTP Cockpit/Cloud Connector/CPI dumps, and more), runs 20 auditor modules over it, and produces a self-contained interactive HTML dashboard plus a multi-page PDF hand-over report ranked by severity with concrete remediation. Because it never opens an RFC or SQL session, it is purpose-built for RISE tenants where SAP runs the basis layer and customer RFC/DB access is restricted.

The scanner runs entirely on the Python 3.8+ standard library with zero third-party runtime dependencies — no agent, no connector, nothing to get approved through a change board. It ships 289+ checks spanning the classic ABAP stack (users, critical authorizations, SoD, security parameters, transports, custom code) and the cloud era (BTP subaccounts, Cloud Connector, destinations, IAS, CPI/Event Mesh, OAuth, S/4HANA business roles and CDS authorization). A standout is its GRC-style offline Access Risk Analysis: it resolves each user's transactions AND authorization object/field/activity across all roles from AGR_1251 + AGR_USERS, then evaluates a 25-conflict-plus-critical-access ruleset at the permission level so display-only access is not flagged as a false positive — honouring documented mitigating controls and producing a per-user risk score.

Every finding is backed by a bundled knowledge base (data/finding_details.json, 323 entries keyed by check-id) that supplies an in-depth Security Risk narrative and a numbered, step-by-step remediation naming the exact SAP transactions, reports, IMG paths, tables and parameters to change. Findings cite real SAP Notes, the SAP Security Baseline, CIS SAP and DSAG guidance, and the tool refuses to fabricate SAP identifiers — note numbers, CVEs and authorization objects are verified before shipping. The SAP Security Notes / HotNews module even diffs your SNOTE export against a curated catalog of major notes since 2020 (RECON, ICMAD, the 2025 NetWeaver VC RCE) and flags actively-exploited CVEs from CISA KEV.

It is built for SAP security architects, GRC teams, Basis administrators and external assessors who need a repeatable, evidence-based posture review without deploying tooling into a production SAP system. A crafted sample_data set (93 exports) triggers every check for demos and CI, and a pytest suite runs all 20 modules end-to-end on Python 3.8–3.12 so regressions that silence a check are caught automatically.

sap-s4hana-scanner.architecture_summary: A single CLI orchestrator (sap_scanner.py) loads exported SAP data through a normalizing DataLoader, dispatches it to 20 independent auditor modules that each subclass a common BaseAuditor and emit severity-ranked findings, enriches those findings from a JSON knowledge base, and renders them to HTML and/or PDF. The pipeline is LOAD (CSV/JSON exports) -> MODULES (20 auditors) -> CHECKS (289+ rules) -> RANK by severity -> REPORT, with every stage running purely on the Python standard library.

architecture_layers.name: CLI orchestrator (sap_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: Parses arguments (--data-dir, --modules, --severity, --config, --format), loads data once via DataLoader, runs each selected auditor in its own guarded block, applies the severity filter, and invokes the report generators.

architecture_layers.name: DataLoader (modules/data_loader.py)
architecture_layers.description: Maps ~101 logical data sources to candidate filenames, parses CSV into dicts with UPPERCASE/underscore-normalized headers and stripped values, loads JSON as objects, and returns None for missing files so checks self-skip.

architecture_layers.name: BaseAuditor + 20 auditor modules
architecture_layers.description: Each module subclasses BaseAuditor and implements run_all_checks(), producing findings via self.finding(check_id, title, severity, category, description, affected_items, remediation, references, details) with baseline-overridable thresholds from get_config().

architecture_layers.name: Findings knowledge base (modules/finding_kb.py + data/finding_details.json)
architecture_layers.description: Loads 323 detailed risk + step-by-step remediation entries keyed by check-id with family-prefix fallback, so both report formats stay in-depth and complete even when an entry is absent.

architecture_layers.name: HTML reporter (modules/report_generator.py)
architecture_layers.description: Builds a self-contained, XSS-safe (html.escape) dark dashboard with a weighted risk score, severity/category breakdowns, live filtering and collapsible per-finding detail.

architecture_layers.name: PDF reporter (modules/pdf_report.py + modules/pdf_writer.py)
architecture_layers.description: Renders a multi-page hand-over report (cover, executive summary, per-finding pages) on a dependency-free, pure-standard-library PDF engine with standard-14 fonts, text wrapping and tables.

Export guide (how to pull each file from SAP), the complete per-check reference and the README banner.

25 permission-level SoD conflicts across P2P/O2C/R2R/H2R and Basis/Security plus 2 HR critical accesses, evaluated on maintain-activity so display access is not a false positive, with mitigating controls and a per-user risk score.

Cloud Connector wildcard mappings/ACLs/certificates, destinations (stored creds, TLS-verify off), IAS conditional auth/IP/MFA, service-binding rotation, CPI iFlow credentials, Event Mesh topic ACLs, OAuth scopes and subaccount governance.

Privileged DB users, PUBLIC and direct system-privilege grants, the _SYS_BI_CP_ALL analytic bypass, database auditing (disabled/CSV-target), audit policies and HANA ini parameters (password policy, TLS enforcement, error verbosity).

Deprecated TLS/ciphers and HSTS, certificate expiry/weak-key/self-signed checks, SNC enablement and quality of protection, HANA data/log volume encryption, CommonCryptoLib version and PSE/key-management health.

Read Access Logging coverage, ILM retention/end-of-purpose, non-production PII masking, DPP toolkit, GDPR Art.6 legal basis, sensitive-field inventory, cross-border transfer safeguards and DSAR SLA compliance.

ABAP SQLi/hardcoded creds/dangerous statements, ATC findings, transport-route/approval SoD, client lock state, SAP Notes/HotNews gaps (incl. CISA KEV CVEs), and SM69/TBTCO OS-command and armed-job step-user hardening.

summary: Oracle E-Business Suite is the financial and HR backbone of thousands of enterprises, yet its sprawling FND security model, thousands of responsibilities, and 25-year-old default accounts make it one of the hardest ERP platforms to audit. This tool tackles that head-on: two single-file Python scanners that run 140 security checks across 13 domains against a live R12.x instance or against offline CSV exports, then produce console, JSON, and self-contained HTML reports.

The dual-mode design is the differentiator. Security teams with direct database access run the live scanner (`oracle_ebs_scanner.py`, ~4,000 lines, one dependency: `oracledb`). Teams behind a locked-down DBA gate hand off a single SQL export script, get back a folder of CSVs, and run the zero-dependency offline scanner (`oracle_ebs_offline_scanner.py`, stdlib only) on any workstation. Both scanners are kept byte-for-byte in sync on rule IDs, severities, and the `Finding` contract, so the same audit is reproducible whether or not the auditor ever touches production.

Coverage spans the full EBS attack surface: default and terminated-user accounts, password and sign-on policy, profile options, responsibility sprawl, 20 segregation-of-duties conflict pairs across every major financial module, dangerous concurrent programs (FNDCPASS/FNDLOAD), AuditTrail configuration, Oracle Database hardening, patch/EOL posture, workflow health, application configuration, PII/data-privacy exposure, and custom PL/SQL code security. Every finding carries a severity, an issue explanation, a concrete remediation, and a CWE mapping (25 distinct CWEs).

It is built for SOX auditors, IT risk teams, and EBS administrators who need evidence, not guesswork. Findings map to SOX, CIS Oracle Database Benchmark, NIST 800-53, PCI-DSS v4.0, HIPAA, ISO 27001, and DISA STIG, and the scanner returns a non-zero exit code on any CRITICAL/HIGH finding so it can gate a CI/CD pipeline.

oracle-ebs-scanner.architecture_summary: Two self-contained, single-file Python scanners share one audit design and stay in lockstep on rule IDs and severities. A scan() orchestrator invokes 13 check-group methods; each emits Finding objects (slotted dataclass with rule_id, name, category, severity, source, context, description, recommendation, cwe). The live scanner sources data from Oracle via oracledb queries; the offline scanner loads the same data from CSVs produced by the SQL export script. Both feed the same trio of report writers (console, JSON, HTML).

architecture_layers.name: Live DB scanner (oracle_ebs_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: ~4,000-line OracleEBSScanner connects via oracledb and runs all checks against a live R12.x instance using read-only SELECT queries.

architecture_layers.name: Offline CSV scanner (oracle_ebs_offline_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: ~3,500-line stdlib-only OracleEBSOfflineScanner that loads ~45 CSVs via _load_csv/load_data and runs the identical 140-check set with no dependencies.

architecture_layers.name: SQL export bridge (export_ebs_audit_data.sql)
architecture_layers.description: ~726-line script of export queries a DBA runs to emit the CSV set the offline scanner consumes, decoupling data collection from analysis.

architecture_layers.name: Check-group engine
architecture_layers.description: A scan() orchestrator dispatches 13 domain methods (_check_users, _check_sod, _check_database, _check_data_privacy, _check_custom_code, etc.), each producing Finding records.

architecture_layers.name: Finding model
architecture_layers.description: A __slots__ dataclass carrying rule_id, severity, source, context, description, recommendation, and CWE, serialized uniformly across all outputs.

architecture_layers.name: Report writers
architecture_layers.description: print_report (color console), save_json (machine-readable), and save_html (filterable, searchable, self-contained Catppuccin-themed report).

security_controls.detail: Flags active default/seeded accounts, terminated employees with live logins, orphan/shared/generic accounts, weak password hashes, direct APPS-schema login, and SysAdmins holding financial responsibilities.
Verifies failed-login limit, minimum length >=8, hard-to-guess complexity, password history/no-reuse, and password age against SIGNON_* profile options.
Detects toxic responsibility combinations across AP, AR, GL, PO, INV, HR, Payroll, Cash Management, Fixed Assets, and System Administrator, each with a described business risk.

Checks locked default accounts, PUBLIC EXECUTE on UTL_FILE/DBMS_*, DBA grants to non-system schemas, UTL_FILE_DIR, remote OS auth, O7_DICTIONARY_ACCESSIBILITY, SQLNET encryption, Database Vault, and FGA policies.

Confirms EBS AuditTrail activation, audit coverage on 13 critical tables (FND_USER, AP/GL/PO transaction tables), DB audit_trail parameter, unified audit policies, and change auditing on profiles/responsibilities.
Finds dangerous programs (FNDCPASS, FNDLOAD, WFLOAD, CONCSUB) in non-admin request groups, host/OS-execution programs, and overly broad ALL-program request groups.
Audits ICX_SESSION_TIMEOUT, non-HTTPS APPS_SERVLET_AGENT/APPS_FRAMEWORK_AGENT, FND_DIAGNOSTICS, guest password defaults, and sign-on audit level.

Surfaces non-standard schemas with SELECT on HR (PER_ALL_PEOPLE_F) and customer (HZ_PARTIES) PII, unencrypted credit-card data, SSN access, non-production masking gaps, and PII data-extract interfaces.
Reviews custom PL/SQL for dynamic-SQL injection, hardcoded credentials, insecure FND_USER_PKG usage, triggers on security tables, security-config FNDLOAD scripts, and AUTHID CURRENT_USER packages.
Validates document sequencing (SOX), approval limits, GL period control, Multi-Org security, EBS/DB EOL and patch recency, and workflow mailer/background-engine health.

summary: The Fortinet FortiGate Security Scanner is a Python security-posture assessment tool that grades FortiGate next-gen firewalls against best-practice hardening, five compliance frameworks, real FortiOS CVEs, and MITRE ATT&CK resilience. It ships as two scanners that share the exact same rule engine: a live scanner that talks to the FortiOS REST API, and an offline scanner that parses an exported .conf backup with zero network access and zero third-party dependencies — purpose-built for OT, ICS, and air-gapped networks where you can't reach the firewall's management plane.

Under the hood it runs 260+ checks across 18 audit domains — admin access, firewall policy hygiene, SSL/IPsec VPN crypto, security profiles, logging, HA, certificates, network hardening, ZTNA/SD-WAN, wireless, backup/DR, and advanced FIPS-grade hardening. Beyond static config review, it does two things most config auditors don't: it matches the parsed firmware version against 66 known FortiOS CVEs (2019-2026, sourced from FortiGuard PSIRT) using train-based version logic across six release trains, and it runs 31 MITRE ATT&CK technique tests across 11 tactics to produce a 0-100% attacker-resilience score.

Every finding auto-resolves to CIS FortiGate, PCI-DSS 4.0, NIST 800-53 Rev 5, SOC 2, and HIPAA controls, and to a ready-to-paste FortiOS CLI remediation block — so the same scan feeds an auditor's evidence CSV and an engineer's fix script. It's a zero-agent, single-file design with a non-zero exit code on CRITICAL/HIGH for CI/CD gating and JSON inventory-driven fleet scanning across many firewalls at once.

It's built for firewall and network-security engineers, red/blue teams, OT/ICS security staff, and GRC auditors who need a repeatable, evidence-grade FortiGate review that works whether the box is online or locked behind an air gap.

fortinet-scanner.architecture_summary: A single-file live engine (fortinet_scanner.py) holds all data (CVE list, compliance map, remediation commands) and logic (18 check methods, report mixin, multi-device orchestrator). A thin offline adapter (fortinet_offline_scanner.py) subclasses the engine and overrides only the data-access seam (_api_get), so a parsed .conf feeds the identical checks. Data flows Connect/Parse -> Collect -> Audit -> CVE match -> MITRE score -> Compliance resolve -> Report.

architecture_layers.name: Data layer (FORTIOS_CVES / COMPLIANCE_MAP / REMEDIATION_COMMANDS)
architecture_layers.description: Module-level structures: 66 CVE definitions with train/fixed versions, 76 rule-to-framework mappings, and 42 CLI remediation blocks, all keyed by rule ID.

architecture_layers.name: Finding model
architecture_layers.description: A __slots__ dataclass that on init auto-resolves each finding's compliance references and remediation command from the shared maps.

architecture_layers.name: _ReportMixin
architecture_layers.description: Rendering layer providing print_report, save_json, save_html (Catppuccin-Mocha dark theme), save_remediation, save_compliance_csv, summary, and severity filtering.

architecture_layers.name: FortinetScanner engine
architecture_layers.description: The core class (subclassing _ReportMixin) with 18 _check_* methods plus _check_cves and _check_mitre_attack_resilience, driving 260+ possible findings.

architecture_layers.name: _api_get data seam
architecture_layers.description: The single swap point abstracting data retrieval — HTTP for live mode, a pre-parsed dict for offline mode — so every check runs unchanged across both.

architecture_layers.name: Offline parser + adapter
architecture_layers.description: FortiGateConfParser does recursive-descent parsing of config/edit/set/end blocks into API-shaped dicts, and OfflineFortinetScanner overrides _api_get to serve them.

architecture_layers.name: MultiDeviceScanner
architecture_layers.description: Fleet orchestrator that sequentially scans firewalls from a JSON inventory, aggregates results, prints a unified summary, and gates the exit code.

architecture_layers.name: CLI (main)
architecture_layers.description: argparse front end handling single-host and inventory modes, output flags, severity filtering, and CRITICAL/HIGH exit-code gating.

security_controls.detail: 24 admin checks (FORTIOS-ADMIN): HTTP admin exposure, idle timeout, password policy, MFA coverage, trusted hosts, API users, default admin — plus WAN management-interface exposure.
16 policy checks flagging any/any/any rules, missing logging, absent security profiles, and weak egress filtering, backed by lateral-movement ATT&CK tests.
SSL VPN (14 checks: TLS version, ciphers, client cert, split tunneling, session limits) and IPsec VPN (12 checks: Phase 1/2 crypto, DH groups, DPD, PFS, key lifetime, IKE version).
Verifies AV, IPS, WebFilter, Application Control, DLP, DNS filter, and SSL/SSH deep inspection are deployed on policies — the controls the MITRE tests probe.
Train-based firmware matching (six trains 6.2-7.6) against 66 FortiGuard PSIRT CVEs: 16 CRITICAL, 27 HIGH, 23 MEDIUM, including KEV-listed SSL VPN/fgfmd RCEs, FortiJump, and xortigate.
31 technique tests across 11 tactics (Initial Access through Impact and Reconnaissance) verify FortiGate controls mitigate real attacks, yielding a 0-100% resilience score.
18 logging checks (FortiAnalyzer/syslog/encryption/alerts), 8 HA checks (heartbeat auth/encryption, session pickup, firmware sync), and 11 cert checks (default/expiry/SHA-1/self-signed/CRL-OCSP).
18 network checks (DoS, SNMP, routing auth, NTP, IPv6, anti-spoofing, DNS encryption) plus ~15 advanced checks (FIPS 140-2, TCP timers, SSH grace, SCP).
Wireless (9: SSID security, WIDS, 802.11w/r, CAPWAP), ZTNA/SD-WAN (6: access proxy, client certs, SLA), backup/DR (5), and authentication (6: LDAP/RADIUS/SAML/MFA server verification).
Each finding auto-resolves to CIS/PCI-DSS/NIST/SOC2/HIPAA controls (76 mappings) and to one of 42 exportable FortiOS CLI fix blocks.

summary: Network Security Scanner (NSS) is a zero-dependency Python tool that audits Cisco device configurations offline. Instead of reaching into live gear over SSH, SNMP, or an API, it ingests exported running-configs (.cfg/.txt/.conf) and evaluates them against CIS Benchmarks, Cisco and NSA hardening guides, and the published Cisco PSIRT advisory database. The output is an interactive dark-theme HTML dashboard with severity-ranked findings, matched evidence lines, and copy-paste remediation commands.

The scanner auto-detects six Cisco platforms from the config text alone — IOS, IOS-XE (Catalyst), NX-OS (Nexus), ASA, Firepower Threat Defense, and WLC (AireOS / Catalyst 9800) — then runs the appropriate subset of 11 audit modules. Because it never touches the network, it is safe to run against configs pulled from an air-gapped environment, a change-management ticket, or a backup vault, which makes it well-suited to consultants, audit teams, and network engineers who receive config dumps rather than device access.

Beyond misconfiguration hardening, a dedicated CVE-detection engine parses the device software train from embedded 'show version' output and matches it against 40 curated Cisco advisories (2018–2026), covering headline threats like the ArcaneDoor ASA/FTD trio, the BadCandy IOS-XE web UI chain, NX-OS Velvet Ant CLI injection, and WLC AP-image RCE. KEV-listed and actively-exploited CVEs are tagged in the finding title so operators triage the real emergencies first.

It is deliberately conservative and transparent: CVE matching flags any device on an affected major.minor train and links the canonical PSIRT advisory rather than guessing patch-level windows, and platform guards emit explicit INFO 'skipped' notices so an absent finding is never misread as a clean bill of health.

cisco-scanner.architecture_summary: A single-file CLI orchestrator loads device configs, a shared parser auto-detects each device type, and a registry of eleven BaseAuditor subclasses runs its platform-guarded checks; findings are tagged with device context, severity-filtered, and rendered into an HTML dashboard. A distinct CVE engine parses the software train and matches it against a curated advisory database rather than grepping for misconfigurations.

architecture_layers.description: detect_version() extracts the running train per platform, matches it against the 40-entry CISCO_CVES database, flags KEV/exploited items, and emits meta-findings for coverage gaps.

architecture_layers.description: Aggregates all findings into a self-contained dark-theme dashboard colour-coded by severity.

Assesses SSID encryption, WPA2/WPA3 enforcement, rogue-AP detection, Management Frame Protection, and WLC management hardening.
Confirms syslog destinations, buffered logging, SNMP traps, NetFlow export, timestamps, and config archive/rollback.
Disables unused small/legacy services, hardens SNMP communities and views, and enforces TCP keepalives and log timestamps.

summary: Palo Alto Network Security Scanner is an offline configuration auditor for Palo Alto Networks NGFW (PAN-OS) and Prisma SASE deployments. Instead of touching production firewalls over an API, it ingests the config exports teams already have — running-config XML from a PA-Series, VM-Series, CN-Series, or Panorama box, plus JSON/CSV exports from Strata Cloud Manager and Prisma Access — and grades them against the CIS Palo Alto Benchmarks, PAN-OS best practices, and IronSkillet baselines. Nothing leaves the analyst's machine and no device connectivity is required, which makes it safe to run in change-control windows, air-gapped assessment environments, and third-party audits.

What sets it apart is dual coverage in one tool. Most PAN-OS hardening scripts stop at the on-prem firewall; this one carries the same policy-as-data discipline into the SASE world, with a dedicated module for ZTNA 2.0 enforcement, Secure Web Gateway, CASB, DLP, Prisma SD-WAN, and cloud-identity MFA. The result is a single scan that spans the classic security stack (App-ID policy hygiene, threat prevention, SSL decryption, GlobalProtect, HA) and the cloud-delivered edge.

The engine is deliberately boring in the best way: pure Python 3.8+ standard library, zero third-party dependencies, and a small BaseAuditor framework that every module extends. Each of the 68 checks emits a structured finding with severity, affected items, GUI remediation path, and a CIS reference, and the run produces an interactive HTML dashboard with a weighted risk score and severity/category breakdowns. A separate, clearly-fenced red-team module ships an authorized-use validator for the 2024 PAN-OS management-interface auth-bypass chain (CVE-2024-0012 / CVE-2024-9474).

It is aimed at firewall engineers, cloud-security teams, and consultants who need a repeatable, evidence-producing review of Palo Alto estates — the kind of artifact that drops straight into a hardening ticket or an audit workpaper.

features.title: Actionable remediation
features.description: Every finding carries the exact PAN-OS GUI navigation path and a benchmark citation so fixes can be applied without guesswork.

paloalto-scanner.architecture_summary: A thin CLI orchestrator (pan_scanner.py) loads all config exports from a directory into a single PanConfig object, then dispatches it through ten domain auditors that each extend a shared BaseAuditor. Every auditor emits normalized finding dicts, which are aggregated, severity-filtered, and rendered by a standalone ReportGenerator into an interactive HTML dashboard. A separate redteam package holds an active-scan CVE validator that is intentionally decoupled from the offline pipeline.

architecture_layers.name: CLI orchestrator (pan_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: Parses arguments, maps module keys to auditor classes, runs selected modules, applies severity filtering, tallies findings, and triggers report generation.

architecture_layers.name: Config loader / PanConfig parser (base.py)
architecture_layers.description: load_all_configs walks a directory, ingests XML/JSON/CSV via a FILE_MAP, detects device type (ngfw/panorama/prisma_access), and exposes XPath, regex, and JSON/CSV query helpers.

architecture_layers.name: BaseAuditor framework
architecture_layers.description: Defines the severity constants and the finding() factory that stamps each result with check_id, category, affected items, remediation, references, and timestamp.

architecture_layers.name: Ten domain auditor modules
architecture_layers.description: device_mgmt, security_policy, threat_prevention, network_security, decryption, globalprotect, logging_fwd, ha_resilience, sase_security, and url_data_filter each implement run_all_checks over the parsed config.

architecture_layers.name: Findings model
architecture_layers.description: A uniform list of structured finding dicts flows from every module, decorated with device hostname and type before aggregation.

architecture_layers.name: ReportGenerator (report_generator.py)
architecture_layers.description: Computes a weighted risk score and severity/category rollups, then renders a self-contained interactive HTML dashboard with filtering.

architecture_layers.name: Red-team validation module (redteam/)
architecture_layers.description: A standalone urllib/ssl-based validator for CVE-2024-0012 / CVE-2024-9474 with connectivity, version, auth-bypass, and session-creation probes and JSON reporting.

project_structure.description: Main CLI entry point; module registry, run loop, severity filtering, and summary output.
PanConfig parser (XML/JSON/CSV) and BaseAuditor framework with the finding() factory.
Device & Management auditor (10 checks): password policy, lockout, permitted IPs, SNMP, TLS, timeouts, admin roles.
Security Policy auditor: any/any rules, App-ID usage, logging, attached profiles, rule hygiene.
Threat Prevention auditor: AV, anti-spyware, vulnerability, WildFire, file blocking, DNS Security, content updates.
SASE / Prisma Access auditor (9 checks): ZTNA 2.0, SWG, CASB, DLP, SD-WAN, cloud identity, remote networks, ADEM.
Builds the interactive HTML dashboard with risk score and severity/category breakdowns.
Authorized-use active validator for CVE-2024-0012 / CVE-2024-9474 (~661 LOC, stdlib urllib/ssl).
Bundled demo NGFW XML and Prisma/SASE JSON exports plus a sample HTML report for quick smoke runs.
Project banner asset referenced by the README.
Contribution guidance and MIT license.

security_controls.detail: Password complexity, admin lockout thresholds, management permitted-IP restrictions, idle timeout, login banner, SNMPv2c detection, minimum TLS version, superadmin sprawl, default admin account, and API key lifetime.
Flags any/any and overly permissive source rules, missing App-ID, unlogged rules, rules without security profiles, absent intrazone-deny, disabled rules, and undocumented rules.
Verifies antivirus, anti-spyware, and vulnerability-protection profiles with blocking actions, WildFire forwarding, file blocking, DNS Security, and scheduled dynamic content updates.

Audits Prisma Access status, ZTNA 2.0 continuous trust and per-app access, Secure Web Gateway, CASB and shadow-IT detection, DLP data patterns/actions, SD-WAN encryption, cloud-identity MFA, remote-network IPsec, and ADEM.
SSL decryption policy coverage, forward-proxy configuration, decryption profiles, TLS protocol settings, and certificate expiry.
Gateway/portal authentication, HIP enforcement, split-tunnel exposure, certificate validation, and IPsec crypto strength.
Zone protection profiles, zone coverage, interface management profiles, DoS protection, and packet-buffer protection.

External/syslog forwarding, log-forwarding profiles, forwarded log types, log encryption, and config-change logging; HA configuration, passive link state, preemption, link/path monitoring, and HA2 encryption.
URL filtering profile presence, high-risk category handling, credential-phishing prevention, safe search enforcement, and data filtering.

Non-destructive detection of CVE-2024-0012 (CVSS 9.8 auth bypass via X-PAN-AUTHCHECK) and CVE-2024-9474 (CVSS 6.9 privilege escalation) via version fingerprinting and header/session probes, gated behind an explicit --confirm authorization flag.
