summary: Kubernetes Security Posture Management (KSPM) Scanner is an agentless, single-file Python tool that connects to a live cluster through your existing kubeconfig or in-cluster service account and runs a comprehensive security posture assessment against the Kubernetes API. There are no agents, sidecars, or DaemonSets to deploy — it reads the cluster read-only and needs only the `kubernetes` Python client, with every other dependency degrading gracefully when absent.

Under the hood it ships roughly 146 discrete rules organized into 19 check groups spanning RBAC, workload/pod hardening, images, network policy, namespaces, secrets, service accounts, control-plane configuration, storage, admission control, nodes, availability (PDB/HPA), service mesh, deprecated APIs, runtime/ephemeral containers, advanced graph-based RBAC, supply chain, and Kyverno policy health. A v2.0.0 policy engine layers a custom YAML policy DSL, OPA/Rego execution (via the `opa` binary), and Kyverno validation on top of the built-in checks, so teams can codify their own guardrails without forking the scanner.

It is built for platform and security engineers who need CI/CD-friendly, benchmark-aligned cluster assessments. Every finding is mapped across six compliance frameworks — CIS Kubernetes Benchmark, NSA/CISA Hardening Guide, MITRE ATT&CK for Containers, SOC 2, PCI-DSS v4.0, and NIST SP 800-190 — and results export to five formats including SARIF v2.1.0 for the GitHub Security tab and PDF executive summaries. Baseline profiles (dev/staging/production), an exception allow-list, RBAC baseline drift tracking, scan-to-scan diffing, multi-cluster runs, and Slack/Teams webhooks make it practical to wire directly into pipeline gates, where a non-zero exit code on CRITICAL/HIGH findings fails the build.

What makes it notable is the depth beyond a static config linter: graph-based RBAC escalation-path analysis, supply-chain checks that drive Trivy/Grype CVE scans plus cosign signature verification and SBOM generation, and managed-cluster awareness that degrades control-plane checks to informational findings on EKS/GKE/AKS where the API server is not a visible pod.

features.title: Agentless live-cluster scanning
features.description: Assesses posture by querying the Kubernetes API through kubeconfig or in-cluster config — no pods, sidecars, or DaemonSets required, and all checks are strictly read-only.

features.title: ~150 checks across 19 groups
features.description: Roughly 146 rules covering RBAC, pod/workload hardening, images, network, namespaces, secrets, service accounts, cluster config, storage, admission, nodes, availability, mesh, deprecated APIs, and runtime security.

features.title: v2.0.0 policy engine
features.description: Define custom checks in a YAML policy DSL, run OPA/Rego policies via the opa binary, and validate Kyverno policy health — all pluggable via --policy-dir, --rego-dir, and cluster detection.

features.title: Graph-based advanced RBAC analysis
features.description: Builds a SA→RoleBinding→Role permission graph to surface multi-hop escalation paths, dormant service accounts, orphaned bindings, and overly broad roles beyond simple wildcard matching.

features.title: Supply chain & image security
features.description: Drives Trivy/Grype CVE scanning, cosign signature verification, SBOM generation, EOL/insecure base-image detection, and registry allow-list enforcement via --trusted-registries.

features.title: Six compliance frameworks
features.description: Every finding is mapped to CIS, NSA/CISA, MITRE ATT&CK for Containers, SOC 2, PCI-DSS v4.0, and NIST 800-190, with a per-framework coverage dashboard in the HTML report.

features.title: Baseline profiles & exceptions
features.description: Built-in dev/staging/production profiles tune severity thresholds and suppressions, while a YAML/JSON exception file allow-lists accepted findings using glob matching on rule ID and resource.

features.title: RBAC drift & scan diffing
features.description: Save an RBAC baseline and compare later to detect permission drift, and diff two scan JSONs to see new, resolved, and persistent findings with severity deltas.

features.title: Multi-cluster scanning
features.description: Scan several contexts in a single run with --contexts, producing consolidated summaries and per-cluster reports.

features.title: Five output formats
features.description: Colored console, machine-readable JSON, interactive dark-themed HTML, SARIF v2.1.0 for GitHub Security, and PDF executive summaries (reportlab with a stdlib fallback).

features.title: CI/CD gating & notifications
features.description: Returns exit code 1 on CRITICAL/HIGH findings for pipeline gates, and pushes severity summaries to Slack and Microsoft Teams webhooks.

features.title: Managed-cluster awareness
features.description: Gracefully handles EKS/GKE/AKS where the API server is not a visible pod, downgrading control-plane checks to informational findings instead of failing.

kspm-scanner.architecture_summary: A single-file Python scanner (kspm_scanner.py, ~5,600 LOC) built around a Finding dataclass and a KSPMScanner class that connects to the Kubernetes API, runs ~20 grouped `_check_*` methods, applies the v2.0.0 policy engine and profile/exception filters, then serializes results through five reporter methods. Data flows from live API collection to per-group rule evaluation to compliance-mapping enrichment to multi-format export.

architecture_layers.name: API connection & collection
architecture_layers.description: Loads kubeconfig or in-cluster config via the kubernetes client, resolves one or many contexts, and lists workloads, RBAC objects, network/admission/policy resources read-only.

architecture_layers.name: Check engine (_check_* methods)
architecture_layers.description: ~20 grouped methods (_check_rbac, _check_workloads/_check_pod_spec, _check_network_security, _check_advanced_rbac, _check_supply_chain, _check_kyverno_policies, etc.) emit Finding objects.

architecture_layers.name: Policy engine (v2.0.0)
architecture_layers.description: _run_custom_policies loads YAML DSL rules, _run_rego_policies shells out to opa eval, and Kyverno validation checks installed policy health — all merged into the findings stream.

architecture_layers.name: Compliance mapping
architecture_layers.description: Six class-level dicts (CIS_MAP, NSA_CISA_MAP, MITRE_MAP, SOC2_MAP, PCIDSS_MAP, NIST_800_190_MAP) enrich each finding; compliance_summary() computes per-framework coverage.

architecture_layers.name: Filtering & post-processing
architecture_layers.description: _apply_profile applies dev/staging/production thresholds, _apply_exceptions allow-lists findings via glob, and diff/baseline logic compares against prior scans.

architecture_layers.name: Reporters
architecture_layers.description: save_json, save_html, save_sarif, save_pdf, plus console output and notify_slack/notify_teams render findings; exit code is derived from CRITICAL/HIGH counts.

project_structure.description: The entire scanner — Finding class, KSPMScanner engine, all check groups, policy engine, compliance maps, and all reporters (~5,600 LOC, single file by design).
Full documentation: feature list, 19 check-group table, policy-engine DSL examples, CIS/compliance mappings, CLI reference, and required RBAC.
Architecture and contributor guide — core pattern, rule-ID convention table, severity model, output formats, and versioned roadmap (v1.1.0→v2.0.0).
Unittest suite (82 tests in TestKSPMScanner) exercising rule logic against mocked Kubernetes objects.
Integration harness that mocks the kubernetes client with a deliberately insecure fake cluster to drive every check group end-to-end.
Project banner rendered at the top of the README.
MIT license.
Excludes IDE and local artifact files from the repo.

security_controls.detail: Detects cluster-admin bindings, wildcard permissions, secrets access, pod exec/attach, escalate/bind/impersonate verbs, anonymous/unauthenticated bindings, CSR approval, and SA token creation.
Builds a permission graph to find dormant SAs, cross-namespace and multi-hop escalation paths, overly broad roles, orphaned bindings, match-all aggregate selectors, and RBAC baseline drift.

Flags privileged containers, root user, hostNetwork/PID/IPC, privilege escalation, dangerous capabilities (SYS_ADMIN/ALL/NET_RAW), writable root FS, missing limits/probes, seccomp, AppArmor/SELinux, and unsafe sysctls.
Missing NetworkPolicies, allow-all ingress/egress, LoadBalancer/NodePort exposure, ExternalIPs, Ingress without TLS, wildcard hosts, and ExternalName services.

API server anonymous auth, insecure port, audit logging, admission controllers, encryption at rest, profiling, plus Dashboard exposure and Tiller (Helm v2) detection — degraded to informational on managed clusters.
Secrets in env vars, sensitive ConfigMap keys, incomplete TLS secrets, Pod Security Admission label/level checks, missing ResourceQuota/LimitRange, and risky default service-account usage.
Trivy/Grype image CVE scanning, cosign signature verification, SBOM generation, EOL/insecure base-image detection, registry allow-list enforcement, and image-verification admission-policy checks.
Webhook failurePolicy/scope/timeout issues (K8S-ADM), kubelet/runtime/kernel version and node-health checks (K8S-NODE), and PDB/HPA availability guardrails (K8S-PDB/HPA).
Istio/Linkerd sidecar and mTLS mode checks (K8S-MESH), deprecated/removed API versions and PSP remnants (K8S-API), and RuntimeClass/ephemeral debug-container detection (K8S-RC/EPH).
Kyverno policy-health checks (K8S-KYV-001–006), plus user-authored YAML DSL rules (K8S-CUSTOM) and OPA/Rego policies (K8S-REGO) evaluated at scan time.

python kspm_scanner.py --html report.html --json scan.json  # scan default kubeconfig, write reports
python kspm_scanner.py --profile production --severity HIGH  # strict CI gate, exit 1 on CRITICAL/HIGH

python kspm_scanner.py --policy-dir ./policies/ --rego-dir ./rego/ --exceptions exceptions.yaml  # custom policy engine
python kspm_scanner.py --contexts dev,staging,prod --baseline-compare rbac-baseline.json  # multi-cluster + RBAC drift

summary: Vulnerability Management Scanner is an open-source, authenticated active vulnerability scanner written in Python. Unlike static analyzers, it connects to live infrastructure over SSH, WinRM, Netmiko, SNMP, SMB and HTTP to read real configurations, enumerate installed software versions, probe services, and correlate everything against a local SQLite CVE database seeded from the NVD and CISA KEV feeds. In one CLI it unifies OS hardening review, network-device auditing, web-server and middleware assessment, database security review, and full DAST web-application testing.

The scanner spans nine active scan engines - Windows, Linux, Cisco IOS/IOS-XE/NX-OS, Palo Alto NGFW/Panorama, six web-server families, six middleware runtimes, three databases, an auto-discovery dispatcher, and a purpose-built DAST module. Findings flow through a single standardized Finding dataclass, so every check - whether a CIS benchmark item, a version-based CVE match, or a reflected-XSS proof-of-concept - lands in the same reporting, filtering, compliance-mapping and export pipeline.

It is built for security engineers, red/blue teams, and compliance auditors who need one tool that covers the infrastructure stack rather than a fleet of point products. Findings are enriched with CVSS, EPSS exploit-probability scores, CISA KEV flags, CWE tags, and mappings to NIST 800-53, ISO 27001, PCI DSS v4.0 and CIS Controls v8 - then exported as interactive Chart.js HTML, PDF, JSON, CSV, or SARIF v2.1.0 for GitHub Code Scanning.

Engineering rigor is a first-class feature: the core is stdlib-only with every transport dependency optional and gracefully degraded, a 1,000+ test pytest suite runs on a Python 3.9-3.12 CI matrix with ruff/mypy linting and dedicated CVE-seed integrity validation, and a plugin architecture lets teams add custom scanners via a simple decorator.

features.title: Nine active scan engines
features.description: One CLI dispatches authenticated scans across Windows, Linux, Cisco, Palo Alto NGFW, web servers, middleware, databases, DAST, plus an auto-discovery orchestrator.

features.title: Auto-discovery and dispatch
features.description: Scans IP ranges (CIDR/range/list), fingerprints services by port and banner, classifies host type, and routes each host to the right scanner in parallel.

features.title: Full DAST web-app testing
features.description: A BFS crawler feeds 82 dynamic checks across 11 categories - injection, XSS, SSRF, XXE, JWT, API, access control, auth, file inclusion, info disclosure and misconfig.

features.title: Palo Alto NGFW auditing
features.description: 92 PAN-OS rules over the XML API cover firewall CVEs (including CVE-2024-3400), policy hygiene, security-profile attachment, decryption, HA and management hardening.

features.title: Local CVE intelligence engine
features.description: SQLite-backed version matching against 32,000+ NVD CVEs with a 510-entry offline seed bundle, CISA KEV flags and FIRST.org EPSS exploit-probability overlay.

features.title: Six CIS benchmark packs
features.description: Bundled hardening checks for Windows, Linux, Cisco, Oracle DB, MySQL and MongoDB drive compliance-grade configuration review.

features.title: Compliance framework mapping
features.description: Opt-in --compliance enrichment maps findings via ~60 CWE and ~30 category rules to NIST 800-53 Rev 5, ISO 27001:2022, PCI DSS v4.0 and CIS Controls v8.

features.title: Multi-format reporting
features.description: Self-contained dark-theme HTML with a Chart.js dashboard, PDF, JSON, CSV, colored console, and SARIF v2.1.0 for GitHub Code Scanning and VS Code.

features.title: DAST safety controls
features.description: Mandatory scope allowlist, adaptive token-bucket rate limiting, hard request cap, circuit breaker, retry backoff and a pre-scan warning banner keep active testing in-bounds.

features.title: Baseline diff scanning
features.description: Compare a scan against a previous JSON baseline to track NEW, FIXED and UNCHANGED findings and measure remediation progress over time.

features.title: Scan profiles and config files
features.description: quick/standard/full/compliance/cve-only presets gate check categories, driven by YAML/TOML config files with CLI-override precedence and a built-in parser (no PyYAML).

features.title: Extensible plugin architecture
features.description: Register custom scanners with the @scanner_plugin decorator via auto-discovery, --plugin-dir, or entry points, validated against the ScannerBase contract.

vuln-mgmt-scanner.architecture_summary: A single argparse CLI (__main__) dispatches to nine scanner orchestrators that all extend a shared ScannerBase and emit a standardized Finding dataclass. Scanners talk to live hosts through six pluggable transports, enrich findings against a SQLite CVE database (synced from NVD 2.0 with CISA KEV and EPSS overlays), optionally attach compliance mappings, then feed one reporting pipeline that renders HTML/PDF/JSON/CSV/SARIF. The DAST module is a self-contained crawl-then-check engine with its own scope, rate-limiting and circuit-breaker safety layer.

architecture_layers.name: CLI & orchestration (__main__)
architecture_layers.description: Argparse entry point exposing 13 subcommands; wires config files, scan profiles, baseline diff, credentials and export flags into each scan run.

architecture_layers.name: Core engine (core/)
architecture_layers.description: Finding dataclass, ScannerBase ABC, six transports (SSH/WinRM/Netmiko/SNMP/SMB/HTTP), credential manager, discovery, CVE database + NVD/KEV/EPSS sync, compliance, config, baseline, scan profiles and plugin registry.

architecture_layers.name: Active scanners (scanners/)
architecture_layers.description: auto, windows, linux, cisco, paloalto, webserver, middleware, database and dast dispatchers that fingerprint targets and invoke the right check libraries in parallel.

architecture_layers.name: Check libraries (webservers/ middleware/ databases/)
architecture_layers.description: Per-platform modules with embedded CVE lists and configuration/hardening rules for IIS/Apache/Nginx/Tomcat/WebLogic/WebSphere, Java/.NET/PHP/Node/Laravel/Oracle, and Oracle DB/MySQL/MongoDB.

architecture_layers.name: DAST engine (dast/)
architecture_layers.description: WebCrawler, URL discovery, DastHTTPClient, ScopePolicy, adaptive RateLimiter, CircuitBreaker and WAF fingerprinting, driving 11 check-category modules with ThreadPoolExecutor dispatch and evidence capture.

architecture_layers.name: CVE data & benchmarks (cve_data/ benchmarks/)
architecture_layers.description: 21 curated seed JSON files (510 CVEs), 47-string CPE mapping for NVD sync, and six CIS benchmark JSON definitions.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting & export (core/reporting.py, templates/)
architecture_layers.description: Self-contained Chart.js HTML dashboard, weasyprint PDF, JSON/CSV, colored console, and SARIF v2.1.0 with fingerprints, CWE tags and CVSS security-severity.

project_structure.description: Argparse CLI entry point registering all scan and CVE subcommands and dispatch logic.
Shared engine: Finding, ScannerBase, transports, CVE database + sync, compliance, config, baseline, profiles, plugins.
Nine scanner orchestrators including auto_scanner, paloalto_scanner and dast_scanner.
DAST engine - crawler, http_client, config (scope/rate/circuit), waf_detect, and checks/ (11 categories).
Six web-server check modules: IIS, Apache, Nginx, Tomcat, WebLogic, WebSphere.

summary: Linux CIS Hardening is a pair of production-grade Bash scripts that lock down Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 and 9 (plus AlmaLinux, Rocky Linux, and CentOS Stream derivatives) against the official CIS Benchmarks — RHEL 8 v3.0.0 and RHEL 9 v2.0.0. Instead of a 100-page checklist and a weekend of manual edits, an operator runs one script as root and the host is systematically hardened across the kernel, network stack, SSH, authentication, auditd, logging, cron, firewall, and file-integrity layers.

The scripts are built for real fleets, not lab demos. Every change is idempotent (re-runnable without drift), each config file is backed up with a timestamp before it is touched, SSH is validated with `sshd -t` before the daemon is ever restarted to prevent remote lockout, and package operations tolerate already-absent packages so a run never aborts halfway. RHEL 9 goes well beyond RHEL 8, layering in a modern defense-in-depth stack: AIDE file-integrity monitoring, fapolicyd application allowlisting, USBGuard device control, a default-deny nftables firewall, and dnf-automatic security patching.

Beyond remediation, each script finishes with an informational audit phase that inventories world-writable files, SUID/SGID binaries, empty passwords, duplicate UIDs/GIDs, orphaned home directories, and dangerous dotfiles (`.rhosts`, `.netrc`, `.forward`) — written to `/tmp/<hostname>_audit/` for review rather than auto-remediated, so nothing surprising happens to a running production box.

It targets platform engineers, SREs, and security teams who need repeatable, auditable, CIS-aligned baselines for RHEL estates. It is fully transparent: two readable, dependency-free shell scripts plus a 524-line README that documents every control and maps it back to its CIS section number.

features.title: Dual RHEL 8 & RHEL 9 coverage
features.description: Separate purpose-built scripts track CIS RHEL 8 Benchmark v3.0.0 and CIS RHEL 9 Benchmark v2.0.0, each tuned to the distro's native tooling (yum vs dnf, direct sshd_config vs drop-in).

features.title: Lockout-safe SSH hardening
features.description: SSH config is validated with `sshd -t` before the daemon is restarted, so a bad directive never strands you out of a remote box.

features.title: Idempotent, re-runnable design
features.description: Shared helper functions update-or-append config keys in place, so the scripts can run repeatedly without duplicating lines or causing config drift.

features.title: Timestamped backups before every change
features.description: sshd_config, auditd.conf, sysctl, chrony, nftables and more are copied to a per-host audit directory with a timestamp, giving a clear rollback trail.

features.title: Comprehensive auditd rule sets
features.description: Ships 51 (RHEL 8) and 58 (RHEL 9) audit rules covering time changes, identity files, logins, permission/ownership changes, mounts, deletions, privileged commands, and kernel-module operations.

features.title: Deep kernel & network sysctl hardening
features.description: Applies 32–35 sysctl parameters — full ASLR, kptr/dmesg restrictions, ptrace scoping, reverse-path filtering, SYN-cookie protection, redirect/source-route rejection, and IPv6 disable.

features.title: RHEL 9 defense-in-depth stack
features.description: Installs and configures AIDE integrity monitoring, fapolicyd allowlisting, USBGuard, a default-deny nftables firewall, and dnf-automatic security-only patching in a single pass.

features.title: Non-destructive audit reporting
features.description: A final phase inventories world-writable files, SUID/SGID binaries, duplicate UIDs/GIDs, and risky dotfiles as findings for manual review — deliberately not auto-remediated.

features.title: Strong cryptographic posture
features.description: Enforces the system-wide FUTURE crypto policy plus curated SSH Ciphers, ETM-preferred MACs, and modern Curve25519/large-group KexAlgorithms.

features.title: Attack-surface reduction
features.description: Removes legacy servers (rsh, telnet, tftp, ypserv), compilers, and unneeded daemons, and blacklists legacy filesystem and network-protocol kernel modules.

features.title: Pre-flight guardrails
features.description: Each script enforces root privileges and gates on the detected OS version before making any change, refusing to run on an unsupported release.

features.title: Operator-aware post-run guidance
features.description: On completion the scripts print explicit next steps — replace the banner company name, tune nftables ports, review container namespace settings, and reboot to activate module/sysctl/crypto changes.

linux-hardening.architecture_summary: The tool is not an application but two self-contained, dependency-free Bash scripts (RHEL8 Hardening Script at 776 lines, RHEL9 Hardening Script at 941 lines) run directly on the target host as root. Each follows the same pipeline: a strict-mode preamble with pre-flight guardrails, a set of shared idempotent config-editing helpers, a sequence of numbered remediation sections that write to canonical system paths, then a backup trail and an informational audit-reporting stage. There is no build step or external runtime — the scripts orchestrate native RHEL tooling (sysctl, augenrules, authselect, update-crypto-policies, nmcli, nft, dnf/yum).

architecture_layers.name: Pre-flight guardrails
architecture_layers.description: `set -euo pipefail`, root/EUID check, and `/etc/os-release` version gate that abort the run on unsupported releases before any change is made.

architecture_layers.name: Idempotent config helpers
architecture_layers.description: Reusable functions (`_set_conf_option`/`_set_kv`, `_set_ini`, `_set_sshd`) that update-or-append keys in key=value, INI, and sshd_config files without creating duplicates.

architecture_layers.name: Remediation sections
architecture_layers.description: 26 (RHEL 8) and 35 (RHEL 9) numbered blocks that write modprobe blacklists, remove/install packages, disable services, and configure PAM, SSH, sysctl, cron, and the firewall.

architecture_layers.name: Backup & rollback trail
architecture_layers.description: Every mutated file (sshd, auditd, sysctl, chrony, nftables, PAM) is copied to `/tmp/<hostname>_audit/` with a date-time stamp before modification.

architecture_layers.name: Defense-in-depth services (RHEL 9)
architecture_layers.description: Initialises AIDE, enables fapolicyd and USBGuard, loads a default-deny nftables ruleset, and schedules dnf-automatic security updates.

architecture_layers.name: Informational audit reporting
architecture_layers.description: Final read-only stage that scans filesystems and account databases, writing findings logs (SUID/SGID, world-writable, duplicate UIDs, risky dotfiles) without remediating them.

project_structure.description: 776-line Bash script implementing the CIS RHEL 8 Benchmark v3.0.0 baseline (yum-based, direct sshd_config edits).
941-line Bash script implementing the CIS RHEL 9 Benchmark v2.0.0 baseline (dnf-based, drop-in configs, AIDE/fapolicyd/USBGuard/nftables).
524-line reference documenting every hardening control, its CIS section, audit artefacts, an RHEL 8 vs 9 comparison matrix, and a production disclaimer.
Excludes IDE folders, editor temp files, and secrets (.env, *.pem, *.key) from the repository.

security_controls.detail: Full ASLR (randomize_va_space=2), kptr_restrict=2, dmesg_restrict, yama ptrace_scope (1 on 8, 2 on 9), perf_event_paranoid=3, sysrq off, suid_dumpable=0, protected hardlinks/symlinks (plus fifos/regular=2 and user.max_user_namespaces=0 on RHEL 9).
Reverse-path filtering, TCP SYN cookies, disabled IP forwarding, ICMP redirect/source-route rejection, martian-packet logging, broadcast-ICMP ignore, and full IPv6 disable via sysctl and modprobe.

PermitRootLogin/EmptyPasswords/UserEnvironment/HostbasedAuth disabled, MaxAuthTries 4, LoginGrace 60, client-alive timeouts, all forwarding off, VERBOSE logging, and curated Ciphers/MACs(ETM)/KexAlgorithms — validated with sshd -t before restart.

auditd enabled with 8192 buffer and panic-on-loss failure mode (-f 2); 51/58 rules track time-change, identity files, logins/faillock, perm/ownership, unsuccessful access, mounts, deletions, privileged commands, module load/unload, and SELinux MAC policy; RHEL 9 adds ENRICHED format and finit_module.

pwquality minlen=14 with credit/repeat rules, pam_faillock (deny=5, unlock_time=900 on RHEL 9), su restricted to wheel via pam_wheel, login.defs password aging (90/7/7) and SHA512 with 5000 rounds on RHEL 9.

Default-deny stateful nftables ruleset (drop policy, loopback allow, invalid-packet drop, established/related accept, rate-limited ICMP, SSH allow, log-and-drop) with firewalld/iptables disabled to avoid conflicts.
AIDE database initialised with a daily cron integrity check emailed to root, fapolicyd application allowlisting enabled, and USBGuard policy generated from connected devices.

Removes legacy services (rsh, telnet-server, tftp, ypserv, talk, xinetd), compilers, and unneeded daemons (bind, samba, squid, net-snmp, postfix); blacklists cramfs/freevxfs/jffs2/hfs/squashfs/udf/usb-storage and dccp/sctp/rds/tipc modules; disables wireless via nmcli.
rsyslog auth/kern/daemon routing with 0640 file mode, persistent compressed journald forwarded to syslog, and (RHEL 9) hardened chrony NTP bound to localhost running as the unprivileged chrony user.

Read-only reporting of world-writable and un-owned/un-grouped files, SUID/SGID binaries, empty passwords, duplicate UIDs/GIDs/names, missing groups, orphaned home dirs, root PATH integrity, and .rhosts/.netrc/.forward presence.

summary: Windows CIS Hardening & Scanners is a dependency-free PowerShell toolkit that both *applies* and *audits* CIS Benchmark security baselines across the modern Windows estate. It pairs an active hardening script for Windows 11 Enterprise with three read-only compliance scanners for Windows Server 2016, 2019, and 2022 - so the same team can enforce a baseline on endpoints and continuously verify it on servers using one consistent, self-contained toolset.

The Windows 11 script (CIS_Win11_Hardening.ps1) applies 170+ settings from the CIS Microsoft Windows 11 Enterprise Benchmark v4.0.0 across ten categories - account and lockout policy, UAC/NTLM/SMB/LSA security options, service disablement, the three firewall profiles, advanced audit policy, computer and user administrative templates, TLS/cryptography, legacy network protocol shutdown (LLMNR, NetBIOS, mDNS), and Windows Defender Attack Surface Reduction rules. It is idempotent, backs up the full security state before any change, supports Level 1 and Level 2 profiles, offers a -WhatIf dry run, and ships as a single file ready to drop into an AD GPO Computer Startup Script.

The three server scanners are strictly read-only - safe to run on production - and audit 318 (2016), 360 (2019), and 392 (2022) controls, 1,070 in total. They share a data-driven engine: every control is a structured object (CIS ID, profile, severity, check type, expected value, operator, recommendation, and CWE) evaluated by a generic comparison engine. They auto-detect server role, translate well-known SIDs to friendly names for User Rights checks, and emit colored console output, machine-readable JSON, and a self-contained interactive HTML report - returning a CI/CD-friendly non-zero exit code when CRITICAL or HIGH controls fail.

The toolkit is built for Windows platform engineers, security operations, and compliance teams who need auditor-defensible CIS coverage without agents, cloud consoles, or third-party modules. With 1,240+ total controls and settings expressed in ~6,000 lines of native PowerShell, it is easy to review, easy to GPO-deploy, and honest about its blast radius - the scanners never touch the system, and the hardening script clearly flags the Level 2 changes that can break RDP, Bluetooth, and printing.

features.title: Active Windows 11 CIS Hardening
features.description: Applies 170+ settings from the CIS Windows 11 Enterprise Benchmark v4.0.0 across ten security categories in a single self-contained script.

features.title: Three Read-Only Server Scanners
features.description: Audits Windows Server 2016, 2019, and 2022 against their respective CIS Benchmarks - 1,070 controls total - without ever modifying system state.

features.title: Level 1 / Level 2 Profiles
features.description: Both hardening and scanning support CIS L1 (broadly compatible) and L2 (high-security) profiles, with Member Server and Domain Controller variants for servers.

features.title: Data-Driven Control Engine
features.description: Each control is a structured object evaluated by a generic comparison engine supporting EQ, RANGE, SUBSET, IN, MATCH, EMPTY and more, so coverage grows by adding data, not code.

features.title: Idempotent Hardening with Full Backup
features.description: The Win11 script skips already-compliant settings and exports secedit, auditpol, firewall, and registry state before any change so every run is reversible and repeatable.

features.title: WhatIf Dry-Run & Backup-Only Modes
features.description: Preview every proposed change without touching the system, or export the current baseline for review before committing to a rollout.

features.title: Server Role Auto-Detection
features.description: Scanners read Win32_ComputerSystem.DomainRole to classify Domain Controller, Member Server, or Standalone and apply only the relevant CIS controls.

features.title: User Rights Assignment with SID Translation
features.description: Resolves 30+ well-known SIDs from secedit exports to friendly names so privilege-assignment checks are readable and accurate.

features.title: Interactive HTML & JSON Reporting
features.description: Scanners emit a self-contained dark-theme HTML report with filtering plus machine-readable JSON carrying CIS ID, status, expected vs actual, CWE, and recommendation.

features.title: CWE-Mapped Severity Scoring
features.description: Every control carries a CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW severity and a CWE reference, and scanners compute a color-coded compliance percentage.

features.title: GPO-Ready Deployment
features.description: The hardening script is a single file with no external dependencies, designed to run as an Active Directory Computer Startup Script with an optional Windows 11 WMI filter.

features.title: CI/CD-Friendly Exit Codes
features.description: Scanners return exit code 1 when any CRITICAL or HIGH control fails and 0 otherwise, so compliance can gate a pipeline.

windows-hardening.architecture_summary: The toolkit is four independent, self-contained PowerShell scripts sharing common design patterns rather than a shared library. The Win11 hardening script is an imperative sequence of Set-* section functions that read current state, compare, and apply via native tools (secedit, registry, Set-Service, netsh, auditpol, Set-MpPreference). The three server scanners share a data-driven architecture: CIS controls are declared as arrays of PSCustomObjects, dispatched by check type to collectors that read live state, evaluated by a generic Compare-Value operator engine, accumulated as results, and rendered to console, JSON, and HTML.

architecture_layers.name: Control Definitions (data layer)
architecture_layers.description: Per-section arrays of PSCustomObjects carrying CIS_ID, Title, Profile, Category, Severity, CheckType, CheckKey, Operator, Expected, Recommendation, and CWE - the single source of truth for coverage.

architecture_layers.name: State Collectors
architecture_layers.description: Type-specific readers - Get-RegistryValue, Get-SeceditExport/Value, Get-AuditpolSetting, Get-ServiceStartType, plus firewall and User Rights readers - that pull live configuration from the OS.

architecture_layers.name: Comparison Engine (Compare-Value)
architecture_layers.description: A generic evaluator implementing EQ/NE/GE/LE/GT/LT/RANGE/MATCH/IN/EMPTY/SUBSET operators to decide PASS/FAIL/WARN for each control independent of check type.

architecture_layers.name: Profile & Role Resolver
architecture_layers.description: Test-ProfileApplicable and Get-ServerRole gate which controls run based on the selected L1/L2/MS/DC/BL/NG profile and the detected Domain Controller / Member Server role.

architecture_layers.name: Result Accumulator
architecture_layers.description: Add-ScanResult and Invoke-Controls collect outcomes into an ArrayList and maintain Passed/Failed/Warning/Error counters and the compliance score.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting Layer
architecture_layers.description: Show-ConsoleReport, Export-JsonReport, and Export-HtmlReport render colored console output, machine-readable JSON, and a self-contained interactive HTML report; exit code is derived from CRITICAL/HIGH failures.

architecture_layers.name: Hardening Apply Layer (Win11)
architecture_layers.description: Set-RegistryValue, Set-AuditSubcategory, and the Set-* section functions perform idempotent, WhatIf-aware changes after Backup-CurrentSettings snapshots the baseline.

project_structure.description: Active hardening script (~1,858 lines) applying 170+ CIS Windows 11 Enterprise v4.0.0 settings across 16 Set-* section functions with backup, WhatIf, and L1/L2 profiles.
Read-only scanner (~1,347 lines) auditing 318 controls against CIS Windows Server 2016 Benchmark v3.0.0 (build 14393).
Read-only scanner (~1,397 lines) auditing 360 controls against CIS Windows Server 2019 Benchmark v3.0.1 (build 17763).
Read-only scanner (~1,444 lines) auditing 392 controls against CIS Windows Server 2022 Benchmark v3.0.0 (build 20348), including post-PrintNightmare and Next Generation Security checks.
Full documentation - tools-at-a-glance table, settings coverage, GPO deployment steps, parameters, output formats, and disclaimers.
Project banner used in the README header.

security_controls.detail: Password history (>=24), max/min age, length (>=14), complexity, reversible-encryption disabled, lockout duration/threshold/reset, and Administrator account lockout - enforced via secedit and evaluated by the scanners.
46 privilege-assignment checks in the 2022 scanner (SeDebugPrivilege, SeTcbPrivilege, SeImpersonatePrivilege, deny-logon rights, etc.) with SUBSET/EMPTY logic and well-known SID-to-name translation.
UAC, NTLM/LAN Manager auth level, SMB client/server signing (CRITICAL), LSA protection, anonymous access restriction, Microsoft-account blocking, and interactive-logon banners and inactivity limits.
Disables risky services - 24 at L1, extended to 37 at L2 adding Bluetooth, RDP, Print Spooler, WinRM, and SMB Server on Windows 11; scanners verify service start types.
Enforces and audits Domain, Private, and Public profiles (~30 firewall settings on Win11) via registry and netsh, including logging and default inbound/outbound actions.
Configures and checks ~29-33 audit subcategories via auditpol (logon/logoff, account management, privilege use, object access, policy change, system) to guarantee forensic visibility.
~80 computer-scope and ~10 user-scope registry policies covering PowerShell logging, LAPS, credential/lateral-movement mitigations, autoplay, and telemetry controls.
Applies 13 + 7 Defender ASR rules via Set-MpPreference and registry on Win11; server scanners check Tamper Protection, Controlled Folder Access, and ransomware/WMI-persistence ASR rules.
Disables TLS 1.0/1.1 and weak ciphers (24 registry settings) and shuts down LLMNR, NetBIOS-over-TCP, and mDNS name-resolution poisoning vectors (8 settings).
Scanner NG/BL profiles check Virtualization-Based Security, Credential Guard, HVCI, System Guard Secure Launch, Kernel DMA Protection, and BitLocker drive encryption.

tech_stack.label: Language

summary: Active network scanning is genuinely dangerous in operational technology. A single unexpected probe can crash a PLC, trip a protection relay, or break a single-master SCADA session and disrupt a live control loop. This project takes the opposite approach: it is a set of purely passive OT/ICS security scanners that never transmit a single packet. Everything is derived offline from captured traffic (PCAP / PCAPNG) collected via TAPs, SPAN ports, or existing IDS/NDR sensors, making it safe to run against production plants, substations, and refineries.

The flagship is the Unified OT Scanner (v2.0, ~22,700 lines) that merges and extends two earlier single-purpose tools into one pipeline. From a capture alone it discovers industrial assets, fingerprints vendors and firmware, decodes 20 industrial protocols, maps the network to the Purdue model, matches known ICS CVEs, detects ICS malware behavior, scores composite risk, and reconstructs multi-hop attack paths from IT entry points to safety-critical devices. Two legacy scanners (PLC-focused and RTU/IED-focused) remain in the repo for reference.

The tool is built for OT/ICS security engineers, SOC analysts covering industrial networks, and asset owners in energy, manufacturing, utilities, and building automation who cannot risk active scanning. Its output is broad and operational: interactive HTML and GraphML topology, SIEM-ready CEF/LEEF/STIX/ECS/Splunk feeds, ServiceNow CMDB import, auto-generated Palo Alto / Fortinet / Cisco firewall policy, and NERC CIP / IEC 62443 / NIST 800-82 compliance reports.

It is notably self-contained and honest about its footprint: stdlib-only Python (only scapy/dpkt/colorama), graceful degradation when optional engines are missing, 76 offline unit tests, and a GitHub Actions matrix across Python 3.8/3.10/3.12. A CISA KEV auto-refresh importer keeps the 'actively exploited' and EPSS data current from authoritative sources instead of hand curation.

features.title: Purely passive, offline-safe analysis
features.description: Analyzes captured PCAP/PCAPNG traffic without ever sending a packet, so it is safe to run in live SCADA and production control environments.

features.title: 20 industrial protocol decoders
features.description: Parses 17 IP-layer plus 3 Layer-2 ICS protocols including Modbus, S7comm, EtherNet/IP, DNP3, IEC-104, IEC 61850 MMS/GOOSE/SV, OPC-UA, BACnet, MQTT, PROFINET, HART-IP, GE-SRTP, Niagara Fox, and KNXnet/IP.

features.title: Vendor and firmware fingerprinting
features.description: A 7-step fingerprint pipeline plus a 144-entry OUI database identifies device vendor, model, and firmware, with deep S7comm SZL and DNP3 Group 0 attribute extraction.

features.title: 29 behavioral vulnerability rules
features.description: Detects insecure OT behavior such as unauthenticated DNP3/IEC-104 control, GOOSE without IEC 62351-6, cleartext protocols, and OPC-UA/MQTT without security across four protocol check modules.

features.title: ICS CVE matching with EPSS and CISA KEV
features.description: Matches devices against 92 curated ICS CVEs enriched with EPSS scores, CISA KEV flags, and exploit maturity, classifying each hit as Now / Next / Never priority.

features.title: ICS malware threat detection
features.description: Matches observed traffic against 10 ICS malware behavioral signatures (Industroyer, TRITON, Havex, Stuxnet, Pipedream, FrostyGoop, Fuxnet, IOControl, CosmicEnergy, BlackEnergy) mapped to MITRE ATT&CK for ICS.

features.title: Purdue model topology mapping
features.description: Automatically infers /24 zones, assigns Purdue levels 0-5, detects cross-zone violations, and exports a color-coded GraphML topology for Gephi/yEd/Cytoscape.

features.title: Multi-hop attack path analysis
features.description: Uses BFS pathfinding to trace routes from IT entry points to crown-jewel OT devices, scoring each path and mapping a per-hop MITRE kill chain with ordered remediation.

features.title: Firewall policy generation
features.description: Auto-generates segmentation rules from observed flows and exports them as Palo Alto PAN-OS XML, Fortinet CLI, Cisco IOS ACL, or JSON, each mapped to IEC 62443 / NERC CIP / NIST 800-82.

features.title: Configuration snapshots and drift detection
features.description: Persists device configuration snapshots, marks last-known-good baselines, and raises drift alerts (firmware, module, program, function-code, protocol, peer, risk changes) with MITRE mapping.

features.title: Broad SIEM and CMDB integration
features.description: Exports 11 formats including CEF, LEEF, STIX 2.1, Splunk HEC, Elastic ECS, ServiceNow CMDB, and generic webhooks for downstream SOC and asset-management tooling.

features.title: Offline project-file asset discovery
features.description: Parses Siemens TIA Portal, Rockwell L5X, Schneider XEF, CSV, and JSON engineering files to enrich the inventory with ground-truth devices that bypass fingerprinting.

ot-security-scanner.architecture_summary: A single PCAP-analysis engine (PCAPAnalyzer in core.py) reads frames through a dual scapy/dpkt backend, dispatches them to Layer-2 and IP protocol analyzers, and builds a per-device registry and communication-flow table. A finalization pipeline then runs fingerprinting, vulnerability checks, CVE matching, topology mapping, risk scoring, threat detection, and access auditing before nine analysis engines and a reporting/export layer render the results. Every sub-engine is loaded via try/except ImportError for graceful degradation.

architecture_layers.name: Capture ingestion (core.py)
architecture_layers.description: Dual scapy/dpkt packet reader that separates Layer-2 frames from IP/TCP/UDP packets and populates the OTDevice registry and CommFlow table.

architecture_layers.name: Protocol analyzers (protocols/)
architecture_layers.description: 20 industrial protocol decoders plus an IT protocol detector (36+ protocols) and a DPI behavior tracker, all subclassing BaseProtocolAnalyzer/BaseL2Analyzer.

architecture_layers.name: Fingerprint pipeline (fingerprint/)
architecture_layers.description: 7-step vendor identification backed by a 144-entry OUI database and protocol-specific device attributes.

architecture_layers.name: Vulnerability & CVE engines (vuln/, cvedb/)
architecture_layers.description: 29 behavioral rules across four check modules plus a 92-CVE matcher with EPSS/KEV boosting and Now/Next/Never prioritization.

architecture_layers.name: Analysis engines (risk/, threat/, attack/, access/, config/, policy/, topology/, project_files/)
architecture_layers.description: Nine engines covering composite risk, ICS malware detection, attack paths, secure-access audit, config drift, firewall policy, Purdue topology, and project-file parsing.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting & export (report/, export/, compliance/, delta/)
architecture_layers.description: Renders JSON/CSV/HTML/GraphML reports, 11 SIEM/CMDB export formats, 35-control compliance assessment, and baseline delta analysis.

security_controls.detail: 29 behavioral rules: 7 DNP3 (no Secure Authentication, unauthenticated control, Direct Operate bypass, restart/file-transfer, multi-master, UDP transport), 5 IEC 60870-5-104, 6 IEC 61850 (GOOSE without IEC 62351-6, simulation-flag abuse, TTL/confRev drift, MMS without TLS), plus 11 cross-protocol/OPC-UA/MQTT and 5 IT/OT convergence rules.

92 curated CVEs across 19 vendor groups (Siemens, Rockwell, Schneider, ABB, GE, SEL, Omron, Mitsubishi, Honeywell, Yokogawa and more) with EPSS scores, CISA KEV flags, exploit maturity, and Now/Next/Never prioritization.

10 malware behavioral signatures (Industroyer/CrashOverride, TRITON, Havex, BlackEnergy, Pipedream, Stuxnet, FrostyGoop, Fuxnet, IOControl, CosmicEnergy) plus unauthorized-command, reconnaissance, and baseline-anomaly detection.
14 ATT&CK for ICS techniques mapped across threat, attack-path, and config-drift findings (T0816, T0831, T0836, T0839, T0842, T0843, T0845, T0846, T0855, T0858, T0859, T0869, T0882, T0886).
Automatic Purdue level 0-5 assignment with 5 zone-violation rules enforcing IEC 62443-3-3 SR 5.1 and NERC CIP-005 segmentation.

BFS multi-hop pathfinding (6-hop limit) from IT entry points to crown-jewel OT devices, scored 0-100 on hop count, auth/encryption gaps, Purdue span, and CVE exploitability, with per-hop kill chain and remediation.
Detects RDP/SSH/VNC/TeamViewer/AnyDesk/OpenVPN/IPsec sessions, identifies jump servers, and rates each against 5 NERC CIP-005-6 R2 compliance rules.
Persistent snapshots with last-known-good baselines and 7 drift rules (firmware, module, program event, function-code shift, new protocol, peer change, risk escalation).
0-100 score fusing vulnerability severity, CVE CVSS, EPSS and CISA KEV boosts, asset criticality multipliers (1.5x safety), and Purdue exposure multipliers, offset by detected compensating controls.
6-stage segmentation rule generation (safety isolation through implicit deny) exported to Palo Alto, Fortinet, Cisco, and JSON, each mapped to IEC 62443-3-3, NERC CIP-005/007, and NIST 800-82.

summary: Detection Engineering is a production-grade library of Splunk SIEM detection content for security operations centers. It is not a scanner or an app — it is 468 hand-authored SPL correlation rules, each a self-contained YAML artifact carrying its detection query, MITRE ATT&CK mapping, severity and confidence scoring, false-positive tuning guidance, analyst investigation queries, and a step-by-step incident-response playbook. The goal is to close the gap between 'we collect the logs' and 'we would actually catch the attack,' shipping deploy-ready detections instead of theory.

Coverage spans the estates a real SOC has to defend: Windows endpoints across the full ATT&CK kill chain, Active Directory attack paths (BloodHound, DCShadow, RBCD, Shadow Credentials, Skeleton Key), the high-value credential-access attacks (Kerberoasting, Golden Ticket, DCSync, LSASS dumping, NTDS.dit extraction, Pass-the-Hash, NTLM relay, AD CS ESC1-ESC13), RHEL Linux across eight tactics via auditd and Sysmon-for-Linux, Apache web attacks, ICS/OT protocols, four major firewall platforms, three EDR products, SAP NetWeaver, and npm/GitHub supply-chain worms.

It also tracks what is happening right now: bespoke rule packs for active campaigns and freshly disclosed CVEs, including APT28's Operation NeuSploit (CVE-2026-21509), the China-nexus UAT-8837 critical-infrastructure intrusion, the June 2026 Patch Tuesday set, and the self-replicating Shai-Hulud / TeamPCP supply-chain worm — each built from real threat-intelligence reporting with IOCs, C2 indicators, and cross-product signatures (ClamAV, Snort SIDs).

The audience is detection engineers, SOC analysts, and blue teams running Splunk Enterprise / Splunk Cloud with Enterprise Security. Every rule is written to drop into a scheduled search or ES correlation search, is tagged to precise ATT&CK sub-techniques rather than just tactics, and comes with the operational context (prerequisites, audit-policy setup, lookup tables, quick-deploy ordering) that makes the difference between an alert that fires and an alert that gets tuned into silence.

features.title: 468 deploy-ready SPL rules
features.description: Hand-authored Splunk correlation searches across 12 platform and attack categories, each with a ready-to-run SPL query rather than pseudo-logic.

features.title: Precise MITRE ATT&CK mapping
features.description: Every rule is tagged to specific technique and sub-technique IDs (270 enterprise techniques plus 27 ATT&CK-for-ICS techniques) for coverage tracking and reporting.

features.title: Investigation queries per rule file
features.description: Each detection ships with follow-up SPL drilldowns so analysts can pivot from a notable event straight into triage without writing search-time SPL.

features.title: Built-in incident-response playbooks
features.description: Rules carry recommended_actions and optional remediation commands, turning a fired alert into a guided response procedure for the SOC.

features.title: False-positive tuning guidance
features.description: Documented known-FP sources, allowlist lookup tables, and report-mode baselining instructions so detections survive contact with a real environment.

features.title: Splunk ES notable events + risk scoring
features.description: Rules define notable titles with field substitutions, confidence (0-100) and risk scores, integrating cleanly with the Enterprise Security risk framework.

features.title: Active-campaign and CVE rule packs
features.description: Dedicated multi-rule packs for live threats — APT28 CVE-2026-21509, UAT-8837, Shai-Hulud npm worm, SAP CVE-2025-31324, and the June 2026 Patch Tuesday set.

features.title: IOC and cross-product enrichment
features.description: C2 IP/domain lists, file-hash lookups, and cross-tool signatures (ClamAV names, Snort SIDs) seed high-fidelity IOC-match detections.

features.title: Full ATT&CK kill-chain coverage on Windows & Linux
features.description: Detections span execution, persistence, privilege escalation, defense evasion, discovery, lateral movement, C2, collection, exfiltration and impact on both platforms.

features.title: Kill-chain correlation rules
features.description: Multi-stage rules chain related events (e.g., VSS creation plus ntds.dit copy, or LSASS access then NTLM logon) for the lowest-FP, highest-confidence alerts.

features.title: Consistent, self-documenting YAML schema
features.description: A uniform rule structure (name, description, mitre, severity, confidence, search, throttle, notable) makes rules reviewable, greppable, and portable.

features.title: Deployment prerequisites baked in
features.description: Each pack documents the exact audit policies, Sysmon/auditd config, SACLs, and lookup CSVs required before a rule can generate detections.

detection-engineering.architecture_summary: Detection Engineering is a declarative content library rather than a running application: each detection is a self-contained YAML document that bundles metadata, the SPL detection query, Splunk ES actions, tuning guidance, investigation drilldowns, and a response playbook. Rules are organized by platform and attack category under splunk_rules/, and are operationalized by copying their SPL into Splunk as scheduled or ES correlation searches, with CSV lookup tables providing environment-specific enrichment and allowlisting.

architecture_layers.name: Rule definition (YAML schema)
architecture_layers.description: Top-level rule_N_* keys declare name, description, mitre_attack, severity, confidence and risk_score — the reviewable, greppable metadata layer that also drives ATT&CK coverage tracking.

architecture_layers.name: SPL detection query
architecture_layers.description: The core `search:` block: a ready-to-deploy Splunk correlation search over the required index/sourcetype with stats, thresholds and lookups — the actual detection logic.

architecture_layers.name: Notable event & risk layer
architecture_layers.description: The `notable` block defines Splunk ES notable titles with $field$ substitutions, risk scores and confidence, wiring rules into the Enterprise Security risk framework.

architecture_layers.name: Tuning & enrichment layer
architecture_layers.description: Throttle windows, false-positive guidance, and CSV lookup tables (domain_controllers, approved_admin_ips, known-hash and IOC feeds) suppress noise and add context.

architecture_layers.name: Investigation query layer
architecture_layers.description: Per-file investigation_queries and drilldown searches give analysts pre-built pivots for triage after a rule fires.

architecture_layers.name: Response playbook layer
architecture_layers.description: recommended_actions and optional remediation_cmd fields turn each detection into a documented IR procedure for the responding analyst.

architecture_layers.name: Category organization
architecture_layers.description: The splunk_rules/ tree groups rules by platform/attack surface (windows, active_directory, credential_access, rhel_linux, apache_webserver, ics_ot, network_firewalls, edr_detection, recent_attacks, sap, supply_chain, network_security).

security_controls.detail: 85 rules detecting DCSync (4662 replication), Kerberoasting/AS-REP roasting (RC4 downgrade, 4769/4768), Golden Ticket forgery, LSASS dumping (Mimikatz/ProcDump/comsvcs), NTDS.dit extraction, Pass-the-Hash, NTLM relay and AD CS ESC1-ESC13.

100 rules across execution, persistence, privilege escalation, defense evasion, discovery, lateral movement, command-and-control, collection, exfiltration, impact, and LOLBin proxy execution, driven by Security Event Log, Sysmon and PowerShell logs.

23 rules for BloodHound/SharpHound recon, DCShadow, resource-based constrained delegation, Shadow Credentials, Skeleton Key, SID History injection, AdminSDHolder persistence and privileged-group modification.

63 rules across 8 tactics — sudo/SUID abuse, PwnKit, container/cgroup escape, cron/systemd/PAM/LD_PRELOAD persistence, shadow & SSH-key theft, auditd/SELinux tampering, reverse shells, DNS-tunnel exfil — via auditd EXECVE/SYSCALL/PATH.

37 Apache rules covering CVE-2021-41773/42013 path traversal, China Chopper/Godzilla/Behinder web shells, UNION/blind/error-based SQLi, XSS, credential stuffing, Slowloris DoS, and Shellshock/Log4Shell/Struts OGNL exploitation.

20 rules for IT-to-OT zone breach and unauthorized writes over Modbus, DNP3, S7comm, OPC UA, EtherNet/IP, IEC-104 and Codesys, including TRITON/TRISIS, Industroyer and PIPEDREAM/INCONTROLLER signatures.
31 firewall rules (PAN-OS CVEs, Cisco ArcaneDoor, FortiJump, Check Point) plus 24 EDR rules detecting CrowdStrike/Defender/Trend Micro sensor tampering, AMSI/ASR bypass and C2.

63 recent-attack rules for APT28 CVE-2026-21509 (12), UAT-8837 critical-infrastructure intrusion (9), June 2026 Patch Tuesday (17 behavioral rules across ~45 CVEs), and 2026 Defender/Netlogon LPE/RCE/DoS CVEs.
9 rules for the Shai-Hulud / TeamPCP npm & GitHub worm (audit-log, endpoint, C2/exfil) and 7 rules for SAP NetWeaver CVE-2025-31324 (metadatauploader webshell RCE, CVSS 10.0).
IOC lookups (C2 IPs/domains, file hashes, compromised npm packages) drive high-confidence matches, while multi-stage correlation rules chain related events to minimize false positives.

usage: git clone https://github.com/Krishcalin/Detection-Engineering — the repo is pure content; no build step is required.
Open a rule file (e.g. splunk_rules/credential_access/dcsync_attack_detection.yml) and copy a rule's `search:` SPL into Splunk > Search & Reporting.
Replace environment placeholders (e.g. YOURDC01$, YOUR-CA-01) and populate the referenced lookup CSVs (dc_ip_list.csv, approved_admin_ips.csv, IOC feeds).
Configure the documented prerequisites for that pack — audit policies, Sysmon/auditd rules, and SACLs — so the required events are generated.
Run the search over a lookback window in report mode to baseline, then save as a scheduled search or Splunk ES correlation search.
Deploy highest-fidelity rules first per each pack's quick-deploy ordering (e.g. tool-signature and IOC-match rules before broad behavioral rules).

summary: Before ransomware detonates or a nation-state actor moves laterally, one thing almost always happens first: they blind the defenses. Turning off Windows Defender, bypassing AMSI, unloading Sysmon, killing EDR services, or silencing ETW and the Event Log. MITRE ATT&CK catalogs this as T1562.001 (Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools), and it is among the most frequently observed techniques in real incidents. The open-source Atomic Red Team project documents 59 distinct tests for it. This tool audits your hosts for the fingerprints of every one.

CrowdStrike Red Team Validation is a single-file, zero-dependency Python auditor that reads offline host-configuration exports (services, registry, Defender preferences, Sysmon filter drivers, processes, launchd, sysctl, GuardDuty and more) and checks them against all 59 Atomic tests — organized into 18 categories spanning 6 platforms (Windows, Linux, macOS, AWS, ESXi, O365). It is purely a reader: it executes nothing, changes nothing, and touches no network, so it is safe to run on production or in an air-gapped forensic workflow.

Each hit becomes a structured finding with a severity, the exact tampered setting, a copy-paste remediation command, the ATT&CK technique, and the originating Atomic test GUID — so a blue team can confirm what a red team (or a real adversary) actually disabled. The catalog is explicitly threat-mapped: LockBit Black auto-logon keys, WhisperGate's AdvancedRun, Conti's Set-MpPreference sweep, FIN7's EDR-Freeze, BlackLotus HVCI tampering (CVE-2022-21894), and Awfulshred wiper's history/swap wipes are all represented.

It is built for detection engineers validating EDR coverage, incident responders triaging a suspected compromise, and purple teams turning Atomic Red Team executions into a repeatable verification pass. Because it ships with a sample_data set full of deliberate misconfigurations, you can see it light up 42 findings in one command before pointing it at a real host.

crowdstrike-redteam.architecture_summary: A single T1562001Auditor class drives a three-stage offline pipeline: it loads a directory of JSON host-configuration exports through an alias-aware file map, runs 59 per-test check methods grouped into 18 lettered categories, and each positive check appends a normalized finding record. The CLI entry point then severity-filters the findings and serializes a JSON report with a technique meta-block. There is no network, database, or external engine — all logic is in one ~969-line stdlib module.

architecture_layers.name: Export Loader (load_data)
architecture_layers.description: Maps 26 logical config keys to candidate filenames (e.g. registry.json / registry_export.json), reads each with UTF-8-BOM tolerance, and reports how many of the expected files loaded.

architecture_layers.name: Check Engine (run_all_checks)
architecture_layers.description: Invokes all 59 check_test_* methods in category order (A-R); each pulls the relevant export, applies its indicator logic, and emits findings only on a positive match.

architecture_layers.name: Category Modules (A-R)
architecture_layers.description: 18 thematic groups — Syslog/Linux, macOS, Sysmon, AMSI, Security Services, Defender Tampering, Defender Registry, Office, O365, EDR-Specific, Event Log, LockBit, HVCI, AWS, Defender-on-Linux, ESXi, ASR, ETW — encapsulating each platform's detection routines.

architecture_layers.name: Finding Builder (finding)
architecture_layers.description: Normalizes every hit into a dict with test number, check_id, title, severity, category, affected items/count, remediation, platform, Atomic GUID, technique, and timestamp.

architecture_layers.name: Report Serializer (main)
architecture_layers.description: Applies the --severity floor, prints a per-category summary table, and writes a JSON report containing a meta-block (technique, total tests, counts, source) plus the findings array.

security_controls.detail: Detects Set-MpPreference disables (realtime/behavior/script/block-at-first-seen), WinDefend service stop, DisableAntiSpyware registry, DISM/PowerShell feature removal, stale signatures (>14 days), deleted Defender scheduled tasks, and dangerous folder/extension/process exclusions.
Flags amsiInitFailed=True, a deleted AMSI provider registry key, IAmsi COM interface override, and AmsiEnable=0 — the common ways PowerShell script scanning is neutralized.
Catches uninstalled CrowdStrike Falcon (Windows/Linux/macOS), Backstab (Kasseika), and EDR-Freeze (FIN7) processes, plus a 14-name security-service disable sweep across MDE/MsSense/major AV vendors.

security_controls.area: Telemetry Blinding — ETW, Sysmon & Event Log
security_controls.detail: Detects disabled ETW Auto Loggers and providers, unloaded/uninstalled Sysmon filter drivers, and a stopped Windows Event Log service (WinPwn/Phant0m).

Checks rsyslog/syslogd, SELinux permissive/disabled, ASLR (randomize_va_space=0), SysRq, swap-off, page-cache drops, cleared shell history, plus macOS Carbon Black, LittleSnitch, OpenDNS, Gatekeeper, and Falcon daemons.
LockBit Black auto-logon and DisablePrivacyExperience keys, WhisperGate's AdvancedRun, and BlackLotus HVCI tampering (CVE-2022-21894) are individually flagged.
Detects AWS GuardDuty suspended/deleted, Microsoft Defender ATP (mdatp) stopped on Linux/macOS, and ESXi account-lockout policy disabled (AccountLockFailures=0).
Identifies missing Attack Surface Reduction (ASR) rules and Defender GPO registry keys set to disable protection via reg.exe or PowerShell.
Every finding is graded CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW and paired with a concrete remediation command (e.g. Set-MpPreference -DisableRealtimeMonitoring 0) plus the Atomic test GUID for traceability.

summary: CrowdStrike Falcon is one of the most capable EDR platforms on the market, yet its protection is only as strong as its configuration. Detect-only prevention policies, broad ML exclusions on temp folders, LOLBin process exclusions, sensors stuck in Reduced Functionality Mode, and hosts with no prevention policy assigned are all silent gaps that attackers exploit long after the license is paid for. The CrowdStrike Falcon Validation Scanner audits your real Falcon configuration exports against CrowdStrike's recommended settings, industry best practices, and known attacker techniques to surface those gaps before an adversary does.

The scanner runs entirely offline. It never touches the Falcon API or a live tenant; instead it ingests JSON exports (from the FalconPy SDK, the Falcon console, or Falcon Data Replicator) and evaluates them through 10 audit modules spanning prevention policy, sensor update policy, response/RTR, device control, exclusions, sensor health, admin & API security, custom IOAs, firewall, and MITRE ATT&CK coverage. Findings are severity-ranked (Critical/High/Medium/Low), mapped to stable check IDs, and rendered into an interactive HTML dashboard with a weighted risk score.

It is built for the people who own Falcon day-to-day: EDR administrators, detection engineers, SOC leads, and security auditors who need a repeatable, evidence-based way to prove their deployment is actually configured to block. Because it is pure-Python with zero third-party dependencies and ships with a sample dataset full of deliberate misconfigurations, it runs anywhere Python 3.8+ is available and can be dropped into a monthly configuration-drift review with a single command.

What makes it notable is its focus on the Exclusion Audit module - the single most dangerous and least-reviewed area of an EDR deployment. It flags ML, IOA, and Sensor Visibility exclusions that land on attacker-abused paths, wildcard patterns, and living-off-the-land binaries, calling out the difference between an ML exclusion (blind to one engine) and a Sensor Visibility exclusion (a complete telemetry blind spot). This is the layer where real breaches hide.

features.title: Prevention Policy Validation (10 checks)
features.description: Inspects every prevention policy for disabled NGAV/ML engines, behavioral IOA gaps, missing exploit mitigations, detect-only mode, ransomware protection, script monitoring, and unprotected or unassigned hosts.

features.title: Response & Device Control checks

crowdstrike-falcon.architecture_summary: A single CLI entry point (cs_scanner.py) loads all Falcon JSON exports through a DataLoader, then dispatches them to 10 auditor classes registered in a MODULE_MAP. Each auditor extends a shared BaseAuditor that standardizes finding records (check ID, severity, category, affected items, remediation, references). Aggregated findings are optionally severity-filtered and passed to an HTML ReportGenerator, with graceful JSON fallback if the reporter is unavailable.

architecture_layers.name: CLI orchestrator (cs_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: Parses arguments (--data-dir, --modules, --severity, --output), prints the banner, dispatches selected modules via MODULE_MAP, aggregates and severity-filters findings, and prints the Critical/High/Medium/Low summary.

architecture_layers.name: Data loader (modules/base.py)
architecture_layers.description: DataLoader resolves 19 config file types via a FILE_MAP with filename aliases, reads UTF-8/BOM JSON, and reports how many config files loaded successfully.

architecture_layers.name: BaseAuditor contract
architecture_layers.description: Shared base class defining severity constants and a finding() factory that produces normalized finding dicts (check_id, title, severity, category, affected_items/count, remediation, references, timestamp).

architecture_layers.name: Policy validation engines (modules/policy_validation.py)
architecture_layers.description: Modules 1-4: PreventionPolicyAuditor, SensorUpdateAuditor, ResponsePolicyAuditor, and DeviceControlAuditor validate policy configuration against recommended defaults.

architecture_layers.name: Advanced validation engines (modules/advanced_validation.py)
architecture_layers.description: Modules 5-10: ExclusionAuditor, SensorHealthAuditor, AdminSecurityAuditor, CustomIoaAuditor, FirewallPolicyAuditor, and MitreCoverageAuditor cover exclusions, fleet health, identity, and ATT&CK mapping.

architecture_layers.name: Report generator (modules/report_generator.py)
architecture_layers.description: ReportGenerator computes a weighted risk score and category/severity breakdowns, then renders a self-contained interactive dark-themed HTML dashboard.

security_controls.detail: Detects disabled Cloud/Sensor Anti-Malware, Adware/PUP, and ML sliders set to Cautious/Disabled instead of Moderate/Aggressive that let zero-days bypass the ML engine (PREV-002, PREV-005).
Flags policies where behavioral settings detect but do not block, and ransomware protection in detect-only/disabled mode - the #1 post-deployment gap (PREV-006, PREV-009).

Checks Suspicious Processes, Registry Ops, Scripts, Intel Threats, Kernel Drivers, script-based execution monitoring, and memory mitigations ASLR/DEP/Heap-Spray/Null-Page/SEH (PREV-003, PREV-004, PREV-010).
Identifies hosts with a sensor but no prevention policy (completely unprotected) and prevention policies not assigned to any host group (PREV-007, PREV-008).

Audits ML/IOA/SV exclusions against 17 attacker-abused paths, 14 executable extensions, wildcard patterns, global scope, and excessive counts - distinguishing ML blind spots from total Sensor Visibility telemetry blindness (EXC-001 through EXC-006).
Critical check for exclusions covering 14 commonly abused binaries (powershell.exe, cmd.exe, mshta.exe, certutil.exe, regsvr32.exe, bitsadmin.exe, bash, python and more) (EXC-007).
Flags sensors not in normal status, hosts in Reduced Functionality Mode (near-zero detection after kernel drift), and stale sensors offline 30+ days (SENSOR-001 to SENSOR-003).
Detects pinned/legacy sensor versions, fleet version sprawl, and disabled uninstall/maintenance protection that permits silent sensor removal (UPD-002 to UPD-004).
Surfaces excessive Falcon Admins, console users without MFA, OAuth2 API clients with write/admin/wildcard scopes, and default-only RBAC (ADMIN-001 to ADMIN-004).
Maps top 10 techniques (T1059, T1053, T1003, T1021, T1055, T1078, T1486, T1082, T1047, T1218) to required Falcon settings and reports coverage gaps (MITRE-001).

summary: EASM Scanner is a comprehensive External Attack Surface Management platform written in pure Python, inspired by commercial solutions like CyCognito, Censys and Mandiant ASM. From a single command it runs a fully automated 14-step pipeline that ingests seeds (domains, IPs, CIDRs, ASNs), discovers and maps internet-facing assets, enriches them, assesses them for vulnerabilities, and produces risk-scored findings — turning an unknown, sprawling internet footprint into a prioritized, actionable inventory.

Its defining design choice is zero hard dependencies on external tooling. The scanner wraps high-performance Go tools from ProjectDiscovery (subfinder, httpx, dnsx, naabu, nuclei, tlsx, asnmap, gowitness) as optional accelerators, but ships a pure-Python fallback for every capability — so it works out of the box with only `requests` and `dnspython`. Every module is self-contained with its own dataclasses, making the codebase easy to read, test and extend.

What sets it apart from a plain scanner is the blue-team and intelligence tooling built on top. It performs unknown-asset discovery (siblings, subsidiaries, shadow IT) via five passive pivots with confidence scoring; cross-references your own surface against six free threat-intel feeds; and pairs every exposure with its defensive counterpart — MITRE ATT&CK technique tags plus ready-to-deploy Sigma rules. Findings flow into a multi-factor risk score with auto-escalation, and out through a FastAPI dashboard, multi-channel alerts, SIEM/STIX exports and Jira ticketing.

It is built for security engineers, red/purple teams and SOC leaders who need to continuously see what an attacker sees — with diff-based scheduling and risk-trend-over-time so they can answer the question every CISO asks: 'is our footprint actually shrinking?'

features.title: 14-Step Automated Pipeline
features.description: One command carries seeds from ingestion through discovery, enrichment, vulnerability assessment and risk-scored findings across four phases.

features.title: Zero-Dependency by Design
features.description: Runs with just requests + dnspython; every Go/ProjectDiscovery tool wrapper has a pure-Python fallback so it works without any external tooling.

features.title: Unknown-Asset Discovery (Attack Surface Intelligence)
features.description: Finds org-owned assets you didn't know about via five passive pivots — certificate-SAN, reverse-WHOIS, passive DNS, favicon-hash and ASN-to-org — with method-aware confidence scoring.

features.title: Threat-Intelligence Enrichment
features.description: Matches your own IPs/domains against six free feeds (Feodo, ThreatFox, URLhaus, FireHOL, Spamhaus DROP, Tor) to flag assets that are already known-bad, no API keys required.

features.title: Detection Pairing (Blue-Team Artifacts)
features.description: Emits the defensive counterpart of each exposure — MITRE ATT&CK technique tags plus generated, deploy-ready Sigma rules written one YAML per finding.

features.title: CVE & Nuclei Vulnerability Assessment
features.description: Version-fingerprints against 25+ CVE entries enriched with EPSS and CISA KEV, and runs Nuclei via Go binary with built-in Python templates as fallback.

features.title: Subdomain Takeover Detection
features.description: Detects dangling DNS/CNAME records vulnerable to takeover across 25 cloud providers including AWS, Azure, GitHub Pages, Heroku, Netlify, Vercel and Shopify.

features.title: Web Misconfiguration & Cloud Storage Enumeration
features.description: Checks 39 sensitive paths (.env, .git, backups, admin, Swagger), CORS, open redirect and directory listing, plus S3/Azure Blob/GCS public-access testing.

features.title: DNS Security & Default Credential Testing
features.description: Validates SPF/DKIM/DMARC, tests AXFR zone transfer and CAA, and safely probes default credentials across 8 service types with 36 credential pairs.

features.title: Multi-Factor Risk Scoring
features.description: A four-component 0-100 score (severity, asset criticality, exploitability, temporal) with five auto-escalation rules for KEV, default creds, takeover and public buckets.

features.title: REST API, Dashboard & Multi-Channel Reporting
features.description: Ships a 15-endpoint FastAPI server, a dark-themed SPA dashboard, and export to Slack/Teams/Email, Splunk/Elasticsearch/Syslog, STIX 2.1 and Jira.

features.title: Diff-Based Scheduling & Risk Trends
features.description: SQLite-backed scan history computes new/resolved/unchanged findings across runs and reports attack-surface exposure-reduction % with ASCII sparklines.

easm-scanner.architecture_summary: A single orchestrator (easm_scanner.py) drives a 14-step, four-phase pipeline over self-contained modules that each return their own dataclass results. Assets and Findings are the shared data model, persisted through a SQLite-backed asset store; every phase enriches the same in-memory inventory. Go/ProjectDiscovery tools are optional accelerators, each fronted by a pure-Python fallback, and results fan out to a FastAPI/dashboard layer and to alerting, SIEM, STIX and Jira exporters.

architecture_layers.name: Orchestrator & CLI (easm_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: ~2,300-line main entry point that parses CLI flags, runs the 14-step pipeline, applies safety caps, and emits console/HTML/JSON reports.

architecture_layers.name: Data Model (models/)
architecture_layers.description: Asset and Finding dataclasses — assets carry domain/ip/port/url/cert/asn/cidr; findings carry rule_id, severity ranking, CWE/CVE, evidence and attributes.

architecture_layers.name: Phase 1 — Discovery modules
architecture_layers.description: seed_manager, asn_mapper, subdomain_discovery, dns_resolver, port_scanner, http_prober and ct_monitor enumerate and map the surface into asset_store.

architecture_layers.name: Phase 2 — Enrichment modules
architecture_layers.description: whois_enrichment, tls_analyzer, geoip_enrichment, tech_fingerprint, attribution_engine and asset_graph attribute and relate assets with confidence scoring.

architecture_layers.name: Phase 3 — Vulnerability Assessment modules
architecture_layers.description: vuln_detector, nuclei_scanner, subdomain_takeover, misconfig_detector, default_creds, dns_security and cloud_enum produce findings; risk_scorer prioritizes them.

architecture_layers.name: Intelligence & Detection modules
architecture_layers.description: intel_discovery (unknown-asset pivots), threat_intel (IOC feed matching) and detection_pairing (ATT&CK + Sigma) add offensive discovery and defensive artifacts.

architecture_layers.name: Phase 4 — Reporting & Integration
architecture_layers.description: api/server + api/dashboard + templates/dashboard.html surface results; alerting, siem_export, stix_export, jira_integration, scheduler and trends push them out.

security_controls.detail: Flags internet-exposed database ports and SMB (CRITICAL), plus RDP, Telnet and VNC (HIGH) and FTP (MEDIUM) via TCP scan with banner grabbing.
Detects dangling DNS/CNAME records pointing to unclaimed resources across 25 cloud providers (AWS, Azure, GitHub Pages, Heroku, Netlify, Vercel, Shopify, Fastly, ...).
Version-fingerprints services against 26 CVE entries for ~10 products, enriched with EPSS exploitability scores and CISA KEV cross-referencing.
Checks 39 sensitive paths (.env, .git, backups, config, debug, admin, Swagger) plus CORS misconfiguration, open redirect and directory listing.
Tests default logins across 8 service types (SSH, FTP, HTTP, SNMP, MySQL, PostgreSQL, Redis, MongoDB) using 36 credential pairs, capped at 50 targets.
Validates SPF (missing/permissive), DMARC (missing/weak), DKIM presence and AXFR zone transfer, plus CAA and dangling-MX checks.
Detects services without TLS, self-signed, expired and soon-to-expire certificates, and weak RSA keys (< 2048 bits) via cert-chain analysis.
Reports missing HSTS, CSP, X-Content-Type-Options and X-Frame-Options headers plus server-version disclosure.
Enumerates public S3/Azure Blob/GCS buckets, and matches owned assets against botnet C2, malware IOC, malware-URL, malicious-netblock and Tor-exit feeds.
4-component 0-100 score (severity 40% / criticality 35% / exploitability 15% / temporal 10%) with 5 auto-escalation rules for KEV, default creds, takeover, public buckets and critical-CVE+exploit.
