summary: Cyber Risk Quantification (CRQ) Engine translates technical cyber risk into the language boards actually fund against: money. It implements the FAIR (Factor Analysis of Information Risk) taxonomy end-to-end — Risk = Loss Event Frequency x Loss Magnitude — and runs Monte Carlo simulation over min/likely/max inputs to produce annualized loss expectancy (ALE) with full percentile distributions, loss exceedance curves, and per-control ROI. Instead of a red/amber/green heat square, a CISO gets 'this ransomware scenario carries a P90 annual loss of $X, which is Y% of revenue.'

Every uncertain input — threat contact frequency, probability of action, threat capability, resistance strength, and each loss component — is expressed as a three-point estimate and sampled through a PERT (modified beta) distribution, so the output honestly models uncertainty rather than pretending to a single false-precision number. Ten thousand iterations per scenario (configurable, seedable for reproducibility) yield the ALE mean, standard deviation, and a seven-point percentile band from P5 (best case) to P95 (worst case).

Beyond raw exposure, the engine analyzes security controls as investments: it computes each control's resistance-strength boost (with diminishing returns for overlapping controls), estimated ALE reduction across the scenarios it applies to, and a dollar ROI ranking so teams can prioritize spend. Results ship as a colored console executive summary, a fully structured JSON report for pipelines and dashboards, and a dark-themed HTML dashboard with inline SVG charts (risk-score donut gauge, likelihood x impact heatmap, aggregate loss exceedance curve, and control-effectiveness bars).

It is a single Python file with zero external dependencies — no pandas, no numpy, no pip install — making it trivial to drop into an air-gapped GRC environment or a CI job. Severity-based exit codes and the Phalanx Cyber scanner CLI convention (--json / --html / --severity / --seed / --verbose) make it as automatable as a vulnerability scanner, while the bundled sample models a realistic financial-services organization across eight threat scenarios.

features.title: Full FAIR Taxonomy
features.description: Implements the complete FAIR decomposition — TEF (Contact Frequency x Probability of Action), Vulnerability from Threat Capability vs Resistance Strength, LEF, and primary/secondary Loss Magnitude — not just a top-line formula.

features.title: Monte Carlo Simulation
features.description: Runs 10,000 iterations per scenario by default (configurable via --simulations) to build a full probability distribution of annual loss rather than a single point estimate.

features.title: PERT Distribution Sampling
features.description: Every min/likely/max input is sampled through a modified-beta PERT distribution, capturing real-world uncertainty and weighting toward the most-likely value.

features.title: Loss Exceedance Curves
features.description: Computes the probability of exceeding any given loss threshold, per scenario and aggregated, so leaders can reason about tail risk instead of averages.

features.title: Control Effectiveness & ROI Analysis
features.description: Models each security control's resistance-strength boost with diminishing returns, estimates its ALE reduction, and ranks controls by dollar ROI for investment decisions.

features.title: Log-Scale Risk Scoring
features.description: Normalizes each scenario's P90 loss against organization revenue into a 0-100 risk score with CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW severity bands.

features.title: Industry-Calibrated Threat Modeling
features.description: Applies per-industry loss multipliers (financial services, healthcare, energy, government and more) and seven pre-defined threat-community capability profiles.

features.title: Interactive HTML Dashboard
features.description: Generates a self-contained dark-themed dashboard with inline SVG risk gauge, likelihood x impact heatmap, loss exceedance curve, and control bars — no JS libraries or CDN.

features.title: Structured JSON Reporting
features.description: Emits a full machine-readable report (percentiles, loss breakdowns, exceedance points, control analysis) for ingestion into GRC platforms, SIEMs, or BI dashboards.

features.title: Reproducible & Deterministic
features.description: A --seed flag pins the RNG so a given scenario file yields identical numbers across runs — essential for audit trails and regression testing.

features.title: Zero Dependencies
features.description: Pure Python 3.10+ standard library in a single file — deployable in air-gapped or locked-down environments with no pip install.

features.title: Automation-Friendly CLI
features.description: Severity filtering plus exit codes (0 clean, 1 CRITICAL/HIGH found, 2 input error) let it gate CI pipelines just like a security scanner.

crq-engine.architecture_summary: A single-file Python application layered into a pure-math FAIR engine and an orchestration/reporting engine. Data flows: JSON scenario file -> validation/load -> per-scenario Monte Carlo simulation (PERT sampling of FAIR factors) -> percentile/exceedance/risk-score computation -> control cost-effectiveness analysis -> render to console, JSON, and HTML/SVG. Typed dataclasses carry results between stages.

architecture_layers.name: FAIREngine (core math)
architecture_layers.description: Implements FAIR formulas and Monte Carlo: PERT/beta sampling, vulnerability = clamp((TC-RS+50)/100), TEF/LEF/LM computation, percentiles, loss exceedance curves, control resistance-strength boost, and log-scale risk scoring.

architecture_layers.name: CRQEngine (orchestration & reporting)
architecture_layers.description: Loads and validates scenario JSON, resolves which controls apply to each scenario, runs all simulations, performs control ROI analysis, applies severity filtering, and renders console/JSON/HTML outputs.

architecture_layers.name: Typed data model
architecture_layers.description: Dataclasses PERTParams, SimulationResult, ControlAnalysis, and Finding carry three-point inputs and simulation outputs (distributions, percentiles, breakdowns, ROI) through the pipeline.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting & SVG rendering
architecture_layers.description: Console renderer (ANSI colors, text gauges, ROI tables) plus HTML dashboard builder that hand-generates inline SVG for the risk donut gauge, 5x5 heatmap, loss exceedance curve, and control bar charts.

architecture_layers.name: CLI driver
architecture_layers.description: argparse front-end (main) handling scenario path, --json/--html/--severity/--simulations/--seed/--verbose/--version, input validation, and severity-based process exit codes.

security_controls.detail: Quantifies each scenario through the full FAIR chain: Threat Event Frequency = Contact Frequency x Probability of Action; Vulnerability = clamp((Threat Capability - Resistance Strength + 50)/100, 0, 1); LEF = TEF x Vulnerability; Loss Magnitude = (primary + secondary) x industry multiplier.
Separates primary loss (productivity, response, replacement) from secondary loss (regulatory fines, reputation, competitive advantage), each as a three-point PERT-sampled estimate.
Seven built-in threat actor profiles (nation_state, organized_crime, hacktivist, malicious/accidental insider, script_kiddie, competitor) with base capability ranges and persistence levels.
Assigns CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW by P90 ALE as a percentage of revenue (>=5% CRITICAL, >=2% HIGH, >=0.5% MEDIUM, else LOW) and a 0-100 log-scale risk score.

Each control boosts Resistance Strength up to ~15 points, with combined boost modeling overlap and capped at 40 points, then estimates ALE reduction (capped at 30%) and dollar ROI = (ALE reduction - annual cost) / annual cost.
Builds exceedance curves over 20 loss thresholds per scenario plus an aggregate curve, quantifying the probability of exceeding any loss level.
PERT (modified beta) sampling across 10,000 iterations yields ALE mean, standard deviation, and a P5-P95 percentile band instead of false-precision point estimates.

Eight sector calibrations (financial_services 1.3, healthcare 1.25, energy 1.2, government 1.15, technology 1.1, retail 1.0, manufacturing 0.95, education 0.85) plus a default, scaling loss magnitude to sector context.

summary: Windows Red Teaming is an open-source, MITRE ATT&CK-aligned assessment framework for authorized red team engagements against Windows 10, 11, Server 2019, and Server 2022. Every check maps to an ATT&CK technique, and the tool pairs two complementary engines under one CLI: 50 hand-written Python audit modules that perform deep, read-only security-posture checks, and a 226-test library of Atomic Red Team-style YAML tests that execute real adversary behavior with input arguments, dependencies, and automatic cleanup. Together they span all 13 ATT&CK tactics from Reconnaissance through Impact.

Its defining trait is safe-by-default, dual-mode execution. Analysts can run a fully passive `check` scan that is production-safe, preview exactly what would run with `--dry-run` (zero target contact), or opt in to active `--simulate` mode where Python modules and YAML atomics execute technique behavior and then automatically revert it. An OS-guard auto-skips modules that don't apply to the target, an authorization banner is confirmed before every scan, and every action is written to a timestamped evidence chain for full attribution.

The tool is aimed at red teamers, purple teamers, and detection engineers who need repeatable, attributable technique execution mapped to a framework rather than ad-hoc scripts. It exports ATT&CK Navigator layers to visualize coverage, generates HTML/JSON/CSV reports, and maps findings to CIS Benchmark v8 and NIST 800-53 Rev 5 controls so results land equally well with a SOC or a compliance auditor.

Notably, it ships modules and atomic tests for four February 2026 Patch Tuesday CVEs (RDP EoP, DWM SYSTEM privesc, MSHTML and SmartScreen security bypasses), and its auto-discovery design means dropping a new Python module into modules/<tactic>/ or a YAML file into atomics/<technique>/ extends coverage with no code changes to the engine.

features.title: Dual-mode scanning engine
features.description: A single ScanEngine orchestrates 50 Python posture-audit modules and 226 YAML atomic tests, so one scan covers both deep audit logic and broad technique execution.

features.title: Check / simulate / dry-run modes
features.description: Passive read-only check mode is safe for production, --simulate actively executes techniques with rollback, and --dry-run previews the exact plan with zero target contact.

features.title: Atomic Red Team-style YAML tests
features.description: Data-driven tests support #{arg} templating with defaults and CLI overrides, prereq/get-prereq dependencies, and three executor types (powershell, command_prompt, manual).

features.title: Auto-discovery of modules and atomics
features.description: Dropping a Python module in modules/<tactic>/ or a YAML file in atomics/<technique>/ is picked up automatically with no changes to the engine.

features.title: Guaranteed cleanup and evidence chain
features.description: Both Python cleanup() and YAML cleanup_command run after every simulation (even on error), while every action is logged with timestamp, target, technique ID, and result.

features.title: ATT&CK Navigator layer export
features.description: MitreMapper emits ATT&CK Navigator v4.5 JSON layer files so coverage and results can be visualized directly on the MITRE technique matrix.

features.title: Multi-format reporting
features.description: Reporter renders a dark-themed Jinja2 HTML report with executive summary plus machine-readable JSON and CSV outputs for pipelines and SIEMs.

features.title: Compliance mapping
features.description: compliance_mapper translates ATT&CK findings into CIS Benchmark v8 and NIST 800-53 Rev 5 controls for audit-ready reporting alongside red team results.

features.title: Recent CVE coverage
features.description: Dedicated modules and atomic tests target four February 2026 CVEs (RDP EoP, DWM privesc, MSHTML and SmartScreen bypasses) with patch checks and IoC enumeration.

features.title: Session abstraction (local + WinRM)
features.description: LocalSession runs via subprocess while WinRMSession uses pypsrp for remote PowerShell, with a common interface for cmd, PowerShell, registry, and file access.

features.title: Scan profiles and technique filters
features.description: Built-in quick, full, and stealth profiles plus per-tactic and per-technique filters let operators tune footprint and scope from the CLI or YAML config.

features.title: OS-aware execution
features.description: Modules declare SUPPORTED_OS and REQUIRES_ADMIN, and the engine auto-skips targets an assessment doesn't apply to across Win10/11 and Server 2019/2022.

windows-red-teaming.architecture_summary: A Click CLI (main.py) drives a central ScanEngine that discovers Python technique modules from the modules/ package tree and YAML atomic tests from atomics/, applies tactic/technique/profile filters, opens a Local or WinRM session, detects the target OS, then runs passive check() first and (only with --simulate) active simulate()+cleanup() plus uncovered YAML atomics. Results flow through data models into the reporter, MITRE mapper, and compliance mapper for HTML/JSON/CSV, ATT&CK Navigator, and CIS/NIST outputs, with an EvidenceLogger recording every action.

architecture_layers.name: CLI (main.py, Click)
architecture_layers.description: Entry point exposing scan, run-atomic, list-modules, and report commands with profile, tactic, technique, simulate, dry-run, and format options.

architecture_layers.name: ScanEngine (core/engine.py)
architecture_layers.description: Orchestrator that auto-discovers Python modules and YAML atomics, applies filters, sequences check/simulate phases, and produces a plan() for dry-run.

architecture_layers.name: Python modules (modules/, BaseModule)
architecture_layers.description: 50 technique modules across 12 tactics, each implementing check(), simulate(), cleanup(), and get_mitigations() with declared SUPPORTED_OS and severity.

architecture_layers.name: Atomic framework (core/atomic_runner.py + atomic_models.py)
architecture_layers.description: Loads 226 YAML tests across 66 techniques, resolves input arguments and dependencies, executes via executor type, and runs cleanup commands.

architecture_layers.name: Session layer (core/session.py)
architecture_layers.description: BaseSession abstraction with LocalSession (subprocess) and WinRMSession (pypsrp) providing cmd, PowerShell, registry, and file operations.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting & mapping (reporter, mitre_mapper, compliance_mapper)
architecture_layers.description: Generate HTML/JSON/CSV reports, ATT&CK Navigator v4.5 layers, and CIS Benchmark v8 / NIST 800-53 compliance mappings.

architecture_layers.name: Config & evidence (core/config.py, core/logger.py)
architecture_layers.description: YAML settings, profiles, and technique toggles feed ScanConfig, while structlog and EvidenceLogger persist a timestamped audit chain.

Every Python module implements get_mitigations() returning MITRE M-code remediations that surface as collapsible detail sections in the HTML report.

summary: RHEL-RT is an open-source, Python-based active security scanner purpose-built for red-team assessments of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 and 9. Instead of a generic Linux audit, every one of its 190 modules is written for the RHEL stack — SELinux, firewalld, auditd, PAM, FIPS mode, crypto policies, systemd sandboxing, and Podman/Docker — and every finding is mapped to a technique in the MITRE ATT&CK Linux Matrix so results speak the language security teams already use.

Safety is a first-class design decision. The scanner is read-only by default: modules run passive `check()` logic that never touches the target, and destructive or state-changing behavior is gated behind an explicit `--simulate` flag with reversible `cleanup()`. It scans the local host directly or reaches remote fleets over SSH (paramiko), so an operator can sweep a single box or a subnet of hardened servers from one workstation.

The output is built for action, not just observation. Findings render as dark-themed HTML with per-technique detail pages, export to JSON/CSV with compliance columns, and ship as an ATT&CK Navigator layer for heatmap visualization. Most notably, the tool auto-generates Ansible remediation playbooks — filtered by severity or compliance tag — that use proper Ansible modules rather than raw shell, turning an assessment into a fixable change set.

For a blue-team lead, a RHEL platform owner, or a red-teamer under authorized engagement, RHEL-RT closes the loop from ATT&CK-aligned detection to STIG/CIS/NIST compliance evidence to ready-to-run remediation — backed by a 570-test suite and a multi-version CI pipeline that keeps the check logic honest.

rhel-red-teaming.architecture_summary: RHEL-RT is a layered Python pipeline: a Click CLI drives an orchestration engine that auto-discovers technique modules, each module runs its checks through a session abstraction that transparently executes commands locally or over SSH, and the resulting findings flow into MITRE/compliance mappers, a Jinja2 reporting engine, and an Ansible playbook generator. All modules inherit a common BaseModule contract (check / simulate / cleanup / get_mitigations), which keeps the scanner, mapping, and reporting layers cleanly decoupled.

architecture_layers.name: CLI & orchestration engine (main.py, core/engine.py)
architecture_layers.description: Click-based command interface that auto-discovers modules, applies scan profiles, and orchestrates check/simulate runs across targets.

architecture_layers.name: Session layer (core/session.py)
architecture_layers.description: Unified execution abstraction that runs commands on the local host or a remote RHEL target over SSH via paramiko.

architecture_layers.name: Technique modules (modules/, modules/base.py)
architecture_layers.description: 190 tactic-organized modules inheriting BaseModule, each implementing passive check(), gated simulate(), cleanup(), and get_mitigations().

architecture_layers.name: Data models (core/models.py)
architecture_layers.description: Typed Target, Finding, ModuleResult, Severity, Status, and Tactic structures that carry results through the pipeline.

architecture_layers.name: Mapping & compliance (core/mitre_mapper.py, core/compliance.py)
architecture_layers.description: Converts findings into an ATT&CK Navigator layer and cross-maps each technique to CIS, NIST, and DISA STIG controls.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting engine (core/reporter.py, templates/report.html)
architecture_layers.description: Renders dark-themed HTML with per-technique detail pages plus JSON and CSV exports using a Jinja2 template.

architecture_layers.name: Remediation generator (core/playbook_generator.py)
architecture_layers.description: Translates findings into Ansible playbooks using proper modules, with severity/tag filtering and per-technique or consolidated output.

summary: Active Directory is the single richest target in most enterprise networks, and the paths to Domain Admin are well known: Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, delegation abuse, AD CS certificate-template misconfigurations, DCSync, and stale-credential exposure. This tool turns that adversary playbook into a repeatable, read-only assessment. It connects to a Domain Controller over LDAP/LDAPS (and optionally WinRM), enumerates the exact objects and settings an attacker would abuse, and reports where the environment is exposed — without ever writing to the directory.

Under the hood it runs a modular battery of checks organised into 15 categories — domain configuration, Kerberos, password policy, privileged accounts, group policy, LDAP, DNS, trusts, AD Certificate Services (ESC1–ESC8), replication/DCSync, object permissions, service accounts, authentication hardening, lateral movement, and audit logging. Every finding ships with severity, the observed vs. expected state, a plain-language rationale, and a ready-to-paste PowerShell remediation command, so a defender can close the gap immediately.

Results roll up into a 0–100 posture score with an A–F letter grade, a self-contained interactive HTML report (dark theme, severity filtering and search), and machine-readable JSON. Severity-based exit codes (0 clean / 1 HIGH / 2 CRITICAL) make it drop-in for CI/CD gating. It is built for red teamers mapping an AD attack surface, blue teams hardening it, and consultants who need a fast, safe, evidence-backed domain review.

It is deliberately safe-by-design: assessment is performed exclusively through LDAP searches and PowerShell Get-* queries, with WinRM-dependent checks degrading gracefully to INFO notices when WinRM is unavailable. A Domain Users account gives roughly 70% coverage via LDAP; adding registry and audit-policy checks over WinRM brings the rest.

ad-attack-scenarios.architecture_summary: A single CLI orchestrator wires together two read-only collectors (LDAP/LDAPS and optional WinRM), a registry of 15 pluggable checker modules, a severity/scoring engine, and a report generator. Data flows one way: the CLI selects categories (flags or YAML profile), connects to the DC, hands the shared clients to each *Checker class whose run_all() emits structured findings, which are then aggregated, scored into a 0–100 posture grade, and rendered to HTML/JSON with a severity-based process exit code.

architecture_layers.name: CLI orchestrator (ad_security_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: Parses arguments, loads the YAML profile, resolves enabled categories from ALL_CHECKERS, drives connection setup, runs checkers, prints the summary, and sets the severity-based exit code.

architecture_layers.name: LDAP collector (utils/ldap_client.py)
architecture_layers.description: ADLdapClient performs read-only LDAP/LDAPS binds (simple or NTLM) and object/group searches, providing ~70% of coverage for domain, Kerberos, privilege and AD CS data.

architecture_layers.name: WinRM collector (utils/winrm_client.py)
architecture_layers.description: ADWinRMClient runs PowerShell Get-* commands for registry and audit-policy checks; optional and degrades gracefully when unreachable.

architecture_layers.name: Check modules (checks/, 15 modules)
architecture_layers.description: Each category is a *Checker class with a run_all() method that executes discrete _check_ routines and appends structured findings (id, severity, actual/expected, recommendation, remediation_cmd).

architecture_layers.name: Scoring engine (utils/severity.py)
architecture_layers.description: Computes the 0–100 posture score, maps it to an A–F grade, and tallies severity counts used by the summary and exit codes.

architecture_layers.name: Report generator (utils/report_generator.py)
architecture_layers.description: Renders the aggregated findings into a self-contained interactive HTML report and a JSON export with run metadata.

SID filtering and selective authentication on trusts, plus Pre-Windows-2000 compatible group and AdminSDHolder tampering on domain ACLs.
LAPS deployment, RDP restrictions, Restricted Admin / Remote Credential Guard, and gMSA adoption vs. privileged/stale service accounts.

summary: The Autonomous Penetration Testing Copilot is a single-file Python agent that turns an LLM into a hands-on operator against a Kali/Parrot attack box. Instead of a human stringing together nmap, nuclei, hydra, and searchsploit, the copilot connects over SSH (or runs locally), autonomously executes security tools, reads and analyses their output, plans the next move, and documents findings, all inside an agentic loop driven by Claude or OpenAI. It behaves less like a scanner and more like a junior pentester that never loses the plot.

What makes it notable is the memory and judgment layered on top of raw tool execution. A persistent Pentest Task Tree is re-injected into every prompt so the agent stays oriented even after chat history is trimmed. A credential vault stores discovered secrets and sprays them across every service it finds. Raw tool output does not become a finding until it survives a six-stage validation pipeline, gets risk-scored into P1-P4 priorities, and is de-duplicated against every other tool that saw the same issue, so noise from tools that cry wolf is filtered out before it reaches the report.

It is built for red teamers, security engineers, and pentest teams who want to compress the mechanical parts of an engagement, recon chaining, exploit lookup, credential reuse, kill-chain documentation, without giving up control. Findings map to OWASP Top 10, PTES, NIST 800-53, and CWE; attack steps map to eleven MITRE ATT&CK stages; and safety guardrails (dangerous-command pattern matching plus approval gating and a stealth mode) keep the autonomy on a leash. The whole thing ships as one ~5,330-line file with a pytest suite, an offline demo runner, and JSON + HTML reporting.

features.title: LLM-driven agentic kill chain
features.description: A single agentic loop (up to 25 iterations per turn) lets Claude or OpenAI call 31 tools, read results, and self-plan the next step end to end.

features.title: Persistent Pentest Task Tree (PTT)
features.description: A thread-safe hierarchical plan (todo/in_progress/done/blocked/skipped) is re-injected into every prompt so the agent's plan survives conversation-history trimming.

features.title: Dual-provider, any-endpoint LLM support

features.title: SSH or local execution engines
features.description: Drives a remote Kali/Parrot box over paramiko SSH or executes locally via subprocess, with named persistent shells and background subagents for parallelism.

features.title: Autonomous recon pipelines
features.description: Four multi-tool pipelines (full, quick, subdomain, stealth) auto-chain nmap, whatweb, wafw00f, nikto, ffuf, nuclei, subfinder and httpx with evidence auto-capture.

features.title: Credential vault + multi-protocol spray engine
features.description: Stores discovered credentials and sprays them against every discovered service across 15 hydra service modules, respecting stealth-mode rate limiting.

features.title: Six-stage vulnerability validation
features.description: Every raw finding passes inventory to analysis to sanity_check to ruling to feasibility to validated, eliminating false positives before anything is reported.

features.title: Exploit analysis and risk prioritization

features.title: Cross-tool finding correlation

autonomous-pentest.architecture_summary: Everything lives in one ~5,330-line pentest_copilot.py. A user message flows into a system prompt that dynamically injects context from 17 supporting systems (the Task Tree, credential vault, shells, findings, attack graph, and more), the LLM responds with text or a tool call, the tool is executed through an SSH or local executor after a stealth wait, its output is captured as evidence and fed back, and the loop repeats up to 25 iterations per turn before results are rendered to JSON and HTML reports.

architecture_layers.name: LLM Provider layer
architecture_layers.description: ClaudeProvider and OpenAIProvider abstract tool-calling; a Claude-format schema is auto-converted to OpenAI function format for GPT/Ollama/vLLM.

architecture_layers.name: Execution engines
architecture_layers.description: SSHExecutor (paramiko) drives a remote attack box while LocalExecutor (subprocess) runs on the local Kali/Parrot machine.

architecture_layers.name: Agent tools (31)
architecture_layers.description: LLM function-calling tools spanning core exec, parallelism/state, detection/exploitation, methodology/stealth, intelligence, validation/analysis, and planning.

architecture_layers.name: Supporting systems (17 classes)
architecture_layers.description: Stateful engines including CredentialVault, ReconPipeline, CredentialSprayEngine, VulnValidator, ExploitAnalyzer, FindingCorrelator, AttackGraph, and TaskTree.

architecture_layers.name: PentestAgent core loop
architecture_layers.description: build_system_prompt() injects all 17 systems, run_turn() runs the agentic loop, and session save/load persists findings and the task tree.

architecture_layers.name: Report generation
architecture_layers.description: Emits a JSON report (findings, creds, attack graph, history) and an HTML report styled with a Catppuccin Mocha dark theme.

architecture_layers.name: CLI interface
architecture_layers.description: Interactive chat loop with 22 slash commands and colored ANSI terminal output; exit code 1 on Critical/High findings for CI/CD gating.

security_controls.detail: 4 auto-chaining pipelines run nmap, whatweb, wafw00f, nikto, ffuf, nuclei, subfinder and httpx across a 58-tool registry in 7 categories.
CredentialSprayEngine sprays vault credentials via hydra across 15 service modules: SSH, FTP, HTTP, HTTPS, SMB, RDP, MySQL, MSSQL, Postgres, Telnet, VNC, SMTP, POP3, IMAP, LDAP.
SmartExploitSelector parses nmap output, queries ExploitDB/searchsploit and nuclei templates, and ranks results by reliability (Metasploit/remote/webapp).
VulnValidator forces every finding through a 6-stage pipeline (inventory to analysis to sanity_check to ruling to feasibility to validated) so only confirmed issues are reported.
ExploitAnalyzer scores Impact x Exploitability / Detection Time with 8 modifiers to assign P1-IMMEDIATE through P4-LOW ratings.
FindingCorrelator de-duplicates by host+port+CVE or normalized title and raises confidence from 50 to 95 percent as more tools agree.
AttackGraph records intrusion steps across 11 stages from Initial Access through Impact for full attack-path documentation.

COMPLIANCE_MAP covers 14 vulnerability categories (SQLi, XSS, RCE, Auth, IDOR, Misconfig, Crypto, SSRF, Deserialization, InfoDisclosure, PrivEsc, DefaultCreds, LFI, BruteForce), each mapped to OWASP/PTES/NIST/CWE.
13 DANGEROUS_PATTERNS regexes (rm -rf, mkfs, dd to disk, fork bombs, shutdown/reboot, iptables flush) trigger an approval prompt unless --auto-approve is set.
StealthController applies rate limiting, jitter, and IDS-evasion flags to recon tools, toggleable per session for evasive engagements.

summary: Oracle Fusion Cloud runs the financials, HR, supply chain, and customer data of thousands of enterprises, yet its security posture lives across two very different control planes: the IDCS / OCI IAM Identity Domain that governs authentication, and the Fusion, HCM, and EPM REST layers that govern roles, data security, and audit. Most SSPM platforms treat Oracle SaaS as a blind spot. This scanner closes it with 55 live security checks that authenticate to a real tenant and read policy directly from Oracle's own APIs.

Built as a single, dependency-light Python file, the scanner maps to the CIS Oracle Cloud Infrastructure SaaS Cloud Applications Benchmark v1.0.0 across four sections: Identity and Access Management, Configuration Management, Networking, and Logging and Monitoring. Every finding is enriched with mappings to NIST 800-53 Rev 5, ISO/IEC 27001:2022, and SOC 2 Type II, so a single run produces evidence usable by security engineering, GRC, and audit teams at once.

It is deliberately pragmatic about access. OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials is the primary auth path, with Basic Auth as a fallback, and it degrades gracefully: given only an IDCS URL it still runs the identity, network, and partial logging checks (32 of 55); add a Fusion URL and all 55 checks light up. Endpoints that return 403/404 log a warning and the scan continues, so a partially-permissioned service account still yields a useful report.

The audience is Oracle-heavy security teams, cloud posture engineers, and auditors who need a repeatable, scriptable read of Oracle SaaS hygiene. Output is a choice of ANSI console, machine-readable JSON, or a self-contained HTML dashboard, and a non-zero exit code on CRITICAL/HIGH findings makes it a drop-in CI/CD gate.

features.title: 55 live API-driven checks
features.description: Authenticates to a real Oracle tenant and evaluates 55 posture checks against live IDCS and Fusion Cloud REST responses rather than static config exports.

features.title: Four-section CIS coverage

features.description: Uses OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials as the primary path with HTTP Basic Auth as a fallback, plus automatic token refresh on expiry.

features.title: Dual API surface
features.description: Reads both the IDCS /admin/v1/ SCIM endpoints and the Fusion fscmRestApi/hcmRestApi/EPM interop REST endpoints in one pass.

features.title: Graceful degradation
features.description: Runs an IDCS-only mode (32 checks) when no Fusion URL is supplied and continues past per-endpoint 403/404 errors instead of aborting the scan.

features.title: Severity filtering

features.title: Rate-limit awareness
features.description: Handles HTTP 429 responses using the Retry-After header and paginates SCIM (startIndex/count) and Fusion (offset/limit) result sets.

oracle-saas-scanner.architecture_summary: A single-file scanner built around one OracleSaaSScanner class. Its scan() entry point authenticates once, then invokes 21 _check_* methods that pull policy from IDCS SCIM and Fusion REST endpoints, normalize each issue into a Finding object auto-enriched from COMPLIANCE_MAP, and finally serialize the collected findings through console, JSON, and HTML reporters.

architecture_layers.name: Authentication layer
architecture_layers.description: _authenticate() negotiates OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials against the IDCS token endpoint (falling back to Basic Auth) and refreshes the bearer token on expiry.

architecture_layers.name: API access layer
architecture_layers.description: _idcs_get/_idcs_single handle SCIM startIndex/count pagination while _fusion_get/_fusion_single handle offset/limit pagination, both with 403/404/429 handling.

architecture_layers.name: Check engine
architecture_layers.description: 21 _check_* methods grouped into the four CIS sections evaluate live API data and emit findings via self._add(), covering all 55 rule IDs.

architecture_layers.name: Finding + compliance model
architecture_layers.description: A __slots__-based Finding class carries rule metadata; the 55-entry COMPLIANCE_MAP auto-populates CIS/NIST/ISO/SOC2 mappings for each rule ID.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting layer
architecture_layers.description: print_report() renders ANSI console output, save_json() writes structured JSON, and save_html() produces a self-contained Oracle-branded HTML dashboard.

architecture_layers.name: CLI orchestration
architecture_layers.description: main() parses argparse flags/env vars, wires up the scanner, applies severity filtering, and sets exit codes for CI/CD gating.

security_controls.detail: Checks minimum length, upper/lower/numeric/special character requirements, expiration window, history depth, and account lockout threshold/duration against IDCS PasswordPolicies.
Flags MFA not enforced for all users or admins (CRITICAL), weak-only factors like SMS/Email, and Sign-On Policy MFA bypass rules (CRITICAL).
Detects missing SAML/SSO federation, OAuth clients with excessive admin scopes, and stale OAuth clients unmodified for 180+ days.
Identifies over-provisioned IDCS privileged groups and Fusion privileged roles, users inactive 90+ days, and terminated users still active.
Checks for missing IP-based restriction on admin Sign-On Policies and self-registration enabled in the Identity Domain.

Covers change-monitoring, unused/admin custom roles, approval workflows, sandbox promotion, flexfield security, unrestricted data roles, view-all security profiles, EPM snapshots, and privileged integration/scheduled accounts.

Evaluates Location-Based Access Control, broad CIDR perimeters, WAF coverage, wildcard CORS, IP filtering, token-endpoint exposure, session timeout, persistent sessions, and RFC1918 ranges in perimeters.

Checks Fusion/EPM/IDCS audit export, Separation-of-Duties violation monitoring (CRITICAL), retention period, real-time privileged-op alerting, login-failure monitoring, audit trail, SIEM integration, and diagnostic log level.
Findings are graded CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW, with CRITICAL reserved for MFA bypass and SoD gaps, driving both triage filtering and CI exit codes.

summary: Snowflake has become the crown jewels of the modern data stack — the single place where an organization's customer records, financial data, and analytics converge. That concentration makes account posture a top-tier risk: one over-privileged ACCOUNTADMIN, one password-only login without MFA, or one external stage without a storage integration can expose the entire warehouse. The Snowflake SSPM Scanner is a read-only SaaS Security Posture Management tool that audits a live Snowflake account against the CIS Snowflake Foundations Benchmark v1.0.0 and turns the result into a scored, remediation-ready report.

It ships as a single self-contained Python file. It connects through the official Snowflake Python Connector, runs 39 SQL-based audit checks across four CIS domains — Identity & Access Management, Monitoring & Alerting, Networking, and Data Protection — and produces color-coded console, machine-readable JSON, and a self-contained HTML dashboard. Every finding carries a severity, evidence, concrete remediation, and a four-framework compliance mapping (CIS, NIST 800-53 Rev 5, ISO 27001:2022, SOC 2).

The scanner is deliberately non-invasive: it issues only SHOW commands and SELECTs against SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE views and never modifies the account. It supports password, RSA key-pair, and externalbrowser SSO authentication, and its severity-weighted scoring engine collapses all 39 findings into a single 0-100 posture score with an A-F letter grade. A configurable --min-score gate and a hard exit code 1 on any CRITICAL failure make it drop-in ready for CI/CD.

It is built for cloud security engineers, data platform owners, and GRC teams who need a fast, auditable, zero-infrastructure way to prove Snowflake compliance and catch drift before an auditor — or an attacker — does.

features.description: Audits a Snowflake account against 39 controls from the CIS Snowflake Foundations Benchmark v1.0.0, spanning identity, monitoring, networking, and data protection.

features.title: Four-framework compliance mapping

features.title: Actionable findings

features.title: Graceful degradation

snowflake-scanner.architecture_summary: A single-file Python scanner organized as a three-phase pipeline: connect, scan, then score-and-report. A SnowflakeClient wraps the Snowflake Python Connector as a read-only SQL executor; a SnowflakeScanner runs 39 CIS check methods that each emit a Finding object; and a scoring plus reporting layer computes a severity-weighted posture score and renders console, JSON, and HTML output.

architecture_layers.name: SnowflakeClient (connector layer)
architecture_layers.description: Wraps snowflake-connector-python with connect() for password/key-pair/SSO auth and query()/query_scalar() helpers that return normalized dict rows.

architecture_layers.name: SnowflakeScanner (check engine)
architecture_layers.description: Holds the 39 check_X_Y methods grouped by CIS section (1.x IAM, 2.x Monitoring, 3.x Networking, 4.x Data Protection) and run_all() to execute them sequentially.

architecture_layers.name: Finding + COMPLIANCE_MAP (data model)
architecture_layers.description: A slotted Finding class carries rule_id, status, severity, evidence, and remediation; COMPLIANCE_MAP joins each rule ID to its CIS/NIST/ISO/SOC2 references.

architecture_layers.name: compute_score (scoring engine)
architecture_layers.description: Applies severity weights (CRITICAL 25 to LOW 3), awards full/half/zero credit for PASS/WARN/FAIL, excludes SKIP/ERROR, and derives the 0-100 score and A-F grade.

architecture_layers.name: Reporters (output layer)
architecture_layers.description: print_console_report (ANSI), write_json_report (machine-readable), and write_html_report (self-contained dark dashboard) render the findings and score.

architecture_layers.name: CLI (build_parser + main)
architecture_layers.description: argparse-based entry point resolving flags and env-var fallbacks, orchestrating the pipeline and setting exit codes for CI gating.

Confirms audit data exists for admin-role grants, MANAGE GRANTS usage, SSO password sign-ins, sign-ins without MFA, security-integration and network-policy changes, SCIM token creation, new share exposures, and unsupported client drivers.
Checks for an account-level network policy and user-level network policies scoped to service accounts to restrict source IPs.

Validates periodic rekeying, AES-256 key size, data retention (>=7 days and 90-day critical retention), storage-integration enforcement for stage creation/operations/external stages, PREVENT_UNLOAD_TO_INLINE_URL, Tri-Secret Secure, and data-masking plus row-access policies.
CRITICAL=25, HIGH=15, MEDIUM=8, LOW=3, INFO=0; PASS earns full weight, WARN half, FAIL zero, and SKIP/ERROR are excluded, yielding a 0-100 score and A-F grade.
Returns exit code 1 when any CRITICAL check FAILs or the posture score falls below the configurable --min-score threshold.
Issues only SHOW and SELECT queries against SNOWFLAKE.ACCOUNT_USAGE views, making the scan non-invasive to production accounts.

summary: SAP Ariba SSPM Security Scanner is a zero-dependency, offline SaaS Security Posture Management (SSPM) tool that assesses the security configuration of SAP Ariba procurement environments. Instead of connecting to a live tenant, it ingests JSON/CSV configuration exports (users, groups, SSO, MFA, API clients, approval workflows, payment controls, supplier settings, audit config and more) and evaluates them for misconfigurations, access risk, compliance gaps and drift from a security baseline.

Modeled on commercial SSPM platforms such as AppOmni, Obsidian, Adaptive Shield and Nudge Security, the scanner covers the full SaaS security spectrum for procurement: identity security, authentication and SSO, API and integration risk, procurement and workflow controls (SoD, three-way match, auto-approve thresholds), data protection and PII classification, supplier/third-party risk, audit logging, network exposure, alerting and configuration drift. Each finding carries a severity, affected-item list, remediation guidance and framework references.

It is aimed at procurement security teams, SAP GRC and internal-audit functions, and cloud/SSPM assessors who need to review an Ariba tenant's posture without live API credentials or agents. Because it is pure Python 3.8+ standard library with no external packages, it runs in air-gapped and change-controlled environments and slots cleanly into audit workflows.

The scanner is notable for pairing procurement-specific risk logic (maverick spend, competitive-bidding controls, supplier bank-detail verification as a BEC/invoice-fraud vector, Segregation-of-Duties conflicts across the req-to-pay lifecycle) with a polished, self-contained interactive HTML dashboard that computes a weighted risk score and lets assessors filter and drill into every finding.

sap-ariba-scanner.architecture_summary: A CLI orchestrator (ariba_scanner.py) loads all configuration exports through a single DataLoader, then dispatches to ten pluggable auditor classes that share a BaseAuditor contract. Each auditor emits structured findings (check_id, severity, affected items, remediation, references), which are aggregated, severity-filtered, and rendered into a self-contained interactive HTML dashboard.

architecture_layers.name: CLI orchestrator (ariba_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: Parses arguments, resolves the MODULE_MAP of ten auditors, runs selected modules, applies severity filtering, and triggers report generation.

architecture_layers.name: Data loader (modules/base.py)
architecture_layers.description: DataLoader resolves ~25 config keys via a FILE_MAP with alias filenames, parses CSV/JSON (BOM-tolerant, header-normalized) into an in-memory dict consumed by all auditors.

architecture_layers.name: BaseAuditor contract
architecture_layers.description: Shared base class providing severity constants, the finding() factory (with affected counts, remediation, references, timestamp), and baseline config lookup.

architecture_layers.name: Identity auditor (identity_access.py)
architecture_layers.description: IdentityAccessAuditor implements the eight IAM checks including admin sprawl, dormant/terminated accounts, SoD conflict matrix, and privilege creep.

architecture_layers.name: Core auditors (core_modules.py)
architecture_layers.description: AuthenticationAuditor, ApiIntegrationAuditor, ProcurementControlsAuditor, and DataProtectionAuditor cover SSO/MFA, OAuth/webhooks, approval/payment controls, and PII/encryption.

architecture_layers.name: Extended auditors (extended_modules.py)
architecture_layers.description: SupplierSecurity, AuditCompliance, NetworkSecurity, Notification, and ConfigDrift auditors handle third-party risk, logging/SIEM, Ariba Network exposure, alerting, and baseline drift.

architecture_layers.name: Report generator (report_generator.py)
architecture_layers.description: ReportGenerator computes the weighted risk score and renders a dark-themed, dependency-free HTML dashboard with filtering, category bars, and collapsible findings; falls back to JSON if unavailable.

security_controls.detail: Dormant accounts (>90d), admin sprawl vs. max threshold, orphaned users, terminated-employee active access, SoD conflicts across req-to-pay, shared/generic accounts, privilege creep, and empty/unused group hygiene.

SAML 2.0 SSO presence and enforcement, signed/encrypted assertions, IDP-initiated CSRF risk, MFA enablement/admin-MFA/bypass/SMS deprecation, password-policy baseline, session timeout >30m, IP restrictions, and X.509 certificate expiry.

Overly broad OAuth scopes (wildcard/admin/full), deprecated grant types (password/implicit), stale clients (>180d), API write access to sensitive entities, unauthenticated integrations, HTTP (non-HTTPS) webhooks, and missing rate limiting.

Missing approval requirements, auto-approval bypass of SoD, high auto-approve thresholds (>$10K), contract controls (expiry alerts/dual approval/competitive bidding), catalog change governance, payment three-way match and duplicate detection, and maverick spend.

Overly broad external sharing (ALL/PUBLIC), field-level encryption at rest, data retention/purge policies, PII auto-classification across 13 patterns, bulk-export restrictions, and supplier-portal data isolation.
Onboarding approval and due diligence, supplier portal MFA, bank-detail-change verification as a BEC/invoice-fraud control, and continuous supplier risk monitoring.
Audit logging enablement, coverage of 10 required event types, log retention >=365 days, SIEM/external forwarding, and compliance-framework mapping presence.
Ariba Network public-profile exposure, minimum TLS version (flags TLS 1.0/1.1), and automatic document sharing with trading partners.
Presence of security alert rules for events like mass login failures and admin changes, and configured notification channels.
Critical security settings baseline (SSO/MFA/audit/IP restrictions disabled) and per-setting drift comparison against a supplied JSON baseline.

summary: Most cloud security tooling treats Oracle Cloud Infrastructure as an afterthought, and the CNAPP products that do cover it are heavyweight SaaS agents that demand live API credentials into your tenancy. OCI CNAPP Security Scanner takes the opposite approach: it is a single, self-contained Python tool that assesses an entire OCI estate entirely offline, by reading the JSON that the standard `oci` CLI already produces. Nothing ever connects back to your cloud, which makes it safe to run in air-gapped review environments, on an auditor's laptop, or inside a CI pipeline.

The scanner implements all five pillars Gartner uses to define a Cloud-Native Application Protection Platform in one place: CSPM (posture and misconfiguration), CIEM (IAM entitlements and least privilege), CWPP (compute, containers, bastion, WAF), KSPM (OKE/Kubernetes posture), and IaC scanning of Terraform state and plans. That breadth is delivered through 96 discrete checks organized into 13 auditor modules, mapped to the CIS Oracle Cloud Infrastructure Foundations Benchmark v3.1.0 (~95% coverage) plus OCI best practices and industry frameworks such as NIST 800-53, ISO 27001, and SOC 2.

It is deliberately dependency-free (Python 3.8+ standard library only) and clone-and-run: there is nothing to install, pin, or keep patched. Every scan produces an interactive HTML dashboard with a weighted risk score, severity breakdown, and per-finding remediation guidance, and falls back to structured JSON when the report layer is unavailable. Module selection and severity filtering make it equally usable for a one-off audit or a repeatable check gate.

It suits cloud security engineers, OCI platform teams, and auditors who need a fast, defensible read on tenancy posture without granting a third-party product standing access to production. A bundled sample_data set of deliberately misconfigured exports means you can see a full report in seconds, then point it at real `oci` CLI output when ready.

ocp-cnapp-scanner.architecture_summary: A thin CLI orchestrator (oci_scanner.py) loads all OCI exports once through a DataLoader, then dispatches a MODULE_MAP of 13 auditor classes that each inherit a shared BaseAuditor contract; every auditor emits normalized finding dicts that are severity-filtered and rendered by a standalone HTML ReportGenerator (with a JSON fallback). Data flows one way: JSON exports to loaded dict to per-module checks to findings to dashboard.

architecture_layers.name: CLI orchestrator (oci_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: Parses arguments, holds the MODULE_MAP of 13 modules, runs the selected auditors, applies severity filtering, and drives report generation.

architecture_layers.name: DataLoader (modules/base.py)
architecture_layers.description: Iterates FILE_MAP to load every OCI CLI JSON export (with alternate filenames and BOM-tolerant reads) into a single in-memory dict.

architecture_layers.name: BaseAuditor contract (modules/base.py)
architecture_layers.description: Shared base class providing the finding() schema, severity constants, baseline lookups, and the _unwrap() helper that strips the OCI CLI data envelope.

architecture_layers.name: CSPM core auditors (modules/cspm_core.py)
architecture_layers.description: IamPolicyAuditor, NetworkVcnAuditor, ComputeAuditor, and StorageAuditor covering IAM/CIEM, VCN, compute, and storage posture.

architecture_layers.name: CIEM & telemetry auditors (modules/cspm_ciem.py)
architecture_layers.description: DatabaseAuditor, LoggingAuditAuditor, and CloudGuardAuditor covering database security, logging/audit/monitoring, and Cloud Guard detection.

architecture_layers.name: CWPP/KSPM/IaC auditors (modules/cwpp_iac.py)
architecture_layers.description: OkeSecurityAuditor, VaultKmsAuditor, WafEdgeAuditor, BastionAuditor, IacSecurityAuditor, and CisBenchmarkAuditor for workload, encryption, edge, and IaC/compliance checks.

architecture_layers.name: ReportGenerator (modules/report_generator.py)
architecture_layers.description: Renders findings into a self-contained interactive HTML dashboard with a weighted risk score and severity/category breakdowns, falling back to JSON.

security_controls.detail: Root-compartment resources, MFA enforcement, overly broad and tenancy-wide policies, API-key and auth-token/secret-key rotation, admin-group size and protection, unused credentials, multiple/admin API keys, federation, and password policy (expiry/reuse).

Default security-list egress, SSH/RDP open to 0.0.0.0/0 (security lists and NSGs), overly permissive NSG rules, missing VCN flow logs, internet/service gateways, route-table issues, and unrestricted ICMP.
Shielded Instance Secure Boot and Measured Boot, in-transit encryption, public IPs, legacy images, boot-volume customer-managed keys, IMDSv1 usage, and monitoring agent enablement.
Public buckets, versioning, lifecycle and replication policies, customer-managed key usage, pre-authenticated request review, plus block-volume and file-storage CMK checks.
Autonomous DB CMEK and IAM authentication, DB Systems automated backups, MySQL HeatWave private endpoints, private-subnet placement, and patch currency.

Audit-log retention (365 days), VCN flow logs, service connectors, events rules and alarms, log-group organization, default tags, notification topics, IAM/VCN event rules, Cloud Guard and object-storage write logging, and local-user auth notifications.
Cloud Guard enablement, detector and responder recipes, security zones, and unresolved Cloud Guard problems.
Public API endpoints, network-policy and pod-security enforcement, RBAC-to-OCI-IAM integration, node secure boot, workload identity, image validation, and node auto-upgrade.
Key rotation, customer-managed key usage, vault replication, overly broad key-access policies, and HSM protection level.

WAF policy/rules/rate-limiting/custom-rule coverage on load balancers, Bastion service usage/session limits/audit logging and direct-public-SSH detection, Terraform state/plan misconfiguration and local-state checks, and a CIS benchmark summary.

summary: Enterprises betting on Zscaler's Zero Trust Exchange collapse their perimeter into a cloud SASE fabric — but a single permissive URL rule, an SSL-inspection bypass, or an over-broad ZPA access policy quietly reopens the door attackers walk through. The Zscaler Security Scanner is an agentless, offline posture-assessment tool that ingests exported ZIA, ZPA, and DSPM configuration and flags the policy gaps, weak defaults, and least-privilege violations that drift into production over time.

The scanner runs 63 discrete checks across 12 audit modules spanning three platforms: ZIA secure web gateway (URL filtering, SSL/TLS inspection, cloud firewall, threat prevention/sandbox), ZPA Zero Trust access (access-policy least privilege, application segmentation, App Connector health), and the surrounding control plane (DLP, Cloud App Control, DSPM, admin/MFA hardening, IdP/SAML, and audit-log forwarding). Each finding is ranked CRITICAL to LOW and ships with remediation guidance, then rolled up into a self-contained HTML dashboard.

Because it works purely on JSON exports and has zero external dependencies (Python 3.8+ standard library only), it needs no live API credentials or network path to the tenant — making it safe to run in change-review, audit, and air-gapped assessment workflows. Security engineers, cloud/network architects, and auditors can point it at a config snapshot and get an evidence-backed picture of how far a deployment has drifted from a Zero Trust baseline.

It is designed for teams operating Zscaler at scale who need repeatable, reviewable posture assessments rather than one-off console spot-checks — the modular design lets you scan a single control area (say, ZPA access policy or SSL inspection) or the entire estate in one pass.

features.title: Agentless offline assessment
features.description: Analyzes exported ZIA/ZPA/DSPM JSON configuration with no live tenant credentials or network access, so it runs safely in audit and air-gapped review workflows.

features.title: ZIA secure web gateway audit
features.description: Inspects URL filtering, SSL/TLS inspection, cloud firewall, and threat-prevention policies for permissive defaults and inspection blind spots.

features.title: ZPA Zero Trust access review
features.description: Validates private-access policy for least privilege, device-posture checks, user-risk scoring, and default-deny enforcement across application segments.

features.title: Application segmentation & connector health
features.description: Flags wildcard app segments, oversized domain scopes, bypass-enabled segments, and unhealthy App Connectors that widen the private-access attack surface.

features.title: DLP and DSPM data-protection checks
features.description: Detects monitor-only DLP rules, missing dictionaries/EDM, and absent data classification or exposure monitoring in Data Security Posture Management.

features.title: Cloud App Control & Shadow IT
features.description: Surfaces risky sanctioned apps, disabled SaaS discovery, and missing tenant restrictions that let unmanaged cloud usage bypass policy.

features.title: Admin, MFA & IdP hardening
features.description: Catches unenforced admin MFA, excess super-admins, dormant accounts, stale API keys, missing IdP/SCIM, and weak SAML configuration.

features.title: Audit-log & compliance integrity
features.description: Verifies audit logging, NSS streaming, retention thresholds, and detects forwarding rules that bypass Zscaler inspection entirely.

features.title: Severity-ranked, remediation-rich findings
features.description: Every check emits a structured finding with CRITICAL–LOW severity, affected items, remediation steps, and references for fast triage.

features.title: Modular targeted scanning
features.description: Run the full estate or a single control area via the --modules flag (e.g. zpa-policy, zia-ssl) plus severity filtering for focused reviews.

features.title: Self-contained HTML dashboard
features.description: Aggregates results into a shareable HTML report with a summary rollup, falling back to structured JSON when needed.

features.title: Zero-dependency Python
features.description: Runs on Python 3.8+ standard library alone — no pip installs, no SDKs — making deployment and portability trivial.

zscaler-scanner.architecture_summary: The scanner is a staged pipeline: a CLI orchestrator selects modules and loads a directory of exported JSON configs through a DataLoader, hands them to twelve auditor classes built on a shared BaseAuditor/Finding framework, then aggregates severity-ranked findings into an HTML (or JSON) report. All logic is pure Python standard library with no live API calls.

architecture_layers.name: CLI & orchestration (zscaler_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: argparse entry point that parses --data-dir, --modules, --severity, --output and --config, then dispatches the selected auditors and applies severity filtering.

architecture_layers.name: Data ingestion (DataLoader)
architecture_layers.description: Loads exported ZIA/ZPA/DSPM JSON files from the data directory, tolerating multiple filename variants and reporting load statistics.

architecture_layers.name: Auditor framework (base.py — BaseAuditor / Finding)
architecture_layers.description: Shared base class defining SEVERITY_CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW and a finding() factory producing structured records with check ID, category, remediation, references and timestamp.

architecture_layers.name: Audit modules (zia_security, zia_zpa_modules, platform_dspm)
architecture_layers.description: Twelve auditor classes across three files implement run_all_checks() for URL/SSL/firewall/threat, DLP/app-control/ZPA-policy/ZPA-app, and admin/auth/DSPM/audit domains.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting (report_generator.py — ReportGenerator)
architecture_layers.description: Renders aggregated findings into a self-contained HTML dashboard with summary statistics, falling back to JSON output if report generation is unavailable.

project_structure.description: Main CLI entry point: module registry, DataLoader invocation, severity filtering, and report dispatch.
ZIA auditors: UrlFiltering, SslInspection, CloudFirewall, ThreatPrevention (22 checks).
DLP, Cloud App Control, and ZPA access-policy / app-security auditors (20 checks).
Admin security, Auth/IdP, DSPM, and audit/compliance auditors (21 checks).
Shared BaseAuditor/Finding framework, severity constants, and JSON DataLoader.

Contribution guidelines.

security_controls.detail: Flags unblocked high-risk categories (CRITICAL), allowed uncategorized URLs, disabled caution pages, missing safe-search enforcement, absent rules, and a permissive default allow-all rule.
Detects no inspection policy (CRITICAL), risky bypass rules, disabled certificate-pinning detection, a minimum TLS version set too low, and inspection gaps for Client Connector traffic.
Checks for missing firewall rules, a default rule that is not deny/block, absent DNS control rules, rules without logging, and large numbers of disabled rules.
Verifies Cloud Sandbox is configured and enabled, malicious-URL blocking is on (CRITICAL), advanced threat protection exists, and vulnerable-browser and file-type controls are active.
Identifies missing DLP policy, monitor-only (non-blocking) rules, absent standard dictionaries, no Exact Data Match, and disabled ICAP integration.

security_controls.area: Cloud App Control & Shadow IT (4 checks)
security_controls.detail: Surfaces missing app-control policy, risky applications allowed, disabled SaaS/Shadow-IT discovery, and unconfigured tenant restrictions.

security_controls.area: ZPA Access Policy — ZTNA least privilege (6 checks)
security_controls.detail: Catches no access-policy rules (CRITICAL), overly permissive rules, missing device-posture checks, no user-risk scoring, a default rule that is not deny, and excessive re-auth timeouts.

security_controls.area: ZPA Application Security & Connector Health (5 checks)
security_controls.detail: Flags wildcard application segments, unhealthy App Connectors, segments spanning too many domains, bypass-enabled segments, and segments missing server groups.

Enforces admin MFA (CRITICAL), limits super-admins and dormant accounts, requires admin-IP restrictions and API-key rotation, and validates IdP presence, SCIM provisioning, SAML strength, and surrogate-IP timeouts.

Confirms DSPM, data classification, and exposure monitoring are active, and validates audit logging, NSS streaming, 365-day retention, authenticated locations, and no Zscaler-bypassing forwarding rules.

summary: Responsible AI Framework is an early-stage, open-source Python project whose stated mission is to protect any AI application or AI agent with safety guardrails and governance controls. As enterprises rush LLM-powered apps and autonomous agents into production, they inherit a new class of risk — prompt injection, unsafe or biased outputs, data leakage, and agents that take actions no one reviewed. This project stakes out a vendor-neutral, code-first place to put the guardrails and governance controls that keep those systems trustworthy.

At its current stage the repository is a foundational scaffold: it ships the project charter (README), an MIT license, and a Python project baseline under a single initial commit — the mission and licensing are defined, and the implementation surface is being built out from here. That transparency matters for a portfolio: this is the seed of a responsible-AI control layer, captured at day zero rather than dressed up as a finished product.

The intended audience is AI/ML engineers, platform and MLOps teams, and AppSec/GRC practitioners who need a reusable, framework-agnostic way to wrap safety and accountability around models and agentic workflows — instead of re-implementing ad-hoc filters per application. Being Python-first and MIT-licensed, it is positioned to drop into existing model-serving and agent stacks and to grow into the guardrail-plus-governance backbone described in its charter.

features.title: AI Application & Agent Protection
features.description: Core mission is a reusable layer that wraps safety and control around both conventional AI applications and autonomous, tool-using agents rather than a single model call.

features.title: Safety Guardrails
features.description: Scoped to screen model inputs and outputs for unsafe, policy-violating, or malicious content so risky responses are caught before they reach users or downstream systems.

features.title: Governance Controls
features.description: Designed to codify organizational policy — what a model or agent is allowed to do — as enforceable controls rather than tribal knowledge in prompts.

features.title: Vendor-Neutral Python Framework
features.description: Built as framework-agnostic Python so it can integrate with existing model-serving and agent stacks without locking teams into a specific LLM provider.

features.title: Agentic Action Oversight
features.description: Explicitly targets AI agents, the highest-risk pattern, positioning the framework to gate and review the actions agents take on behalf of users.

features.title: MIT-Licensed & Open
features.description: Released under a permissive MIT license so teams can freely adopt, embed, and extend the responsible-AI controls in commercial and internal systems.

features.title: Extensible Foundation
features.description: Structured as a clean project baseline intended to grow into a modular library of composable responsible-AI checks and policies.

summary: Continuous Threat Exposure Management (CTEM) is an early-stage, open-source project that stakes out an ambitious design: a Python-based architecture driven by an Agentic AI workflow for continuously managing security exposures. As the README states in its single descriptive line, it is "an open-source python-based architecture that uses Agentic AI workflow" — the seed of a platform intended to operationalise the CTEM discipline with autonomous AI agents rather than one-off, point-in-time scans.

The problem it targets is one every security leader recognises: attack surfaces change faster than quarterly pentests or annual audits can track, and traditional vulnerability management buries teams in CVE lists with no business context. CTEM reframes the work as a continuous loop — scope what matters, discover exposures, prioritise by real risk, validate exploitability, and mobilise remediation — and this project's declared intent is to automate that loop with agentic AI.

An honest note for readers of this portfolio: the repository is at the initial-commit / scaffolding stage. It ships a README, an MIT LICENSE, and a .gitignore — the vision and licensing are established, but the Python engine, the AI agents, and the detections framed below are the intended design, not yet-committed code. This page documents the project's declared architecture and direction so it can be evaluated on its concept, with implementation to follow.

features.title: Agentic AI workflow (declared core)
features.description: The README commits the project to an Agentic AI workflow as its central engine — autonomous AI agents intended to drive the exposure-management loop rather than static, rule-only scanning.

features.title: Continuous exposure discovery (designed)
features.description: Per the tagline, the platform is designed to continuously discover security exposures across the attack surface instead of relying on periodic point-in-time assessments.

features.title: Risk-based prioritisation (designed)
features.description: Intended to rank exposures by real-world risk and business context so remediation effort targets what attackers would actually reach, not raw CVE volume.

features.title: Exposure validation (designed)
features.description: Aims to validate whether prioritised exposures are genuinely exploitable, filtering theoretical findings from those that warrant urgent action.

features.title: Python-based, extensible architecture (declared)
features.description: The README fixes Python as the implementation language, positioning the platform for an open, scriptable, and community-extensible codebase.

features.title: Open-source under MIT (in place)
features.description: Ships with a permissive MIT license already committed, inviting reuse, contribution, and integration without commercial friction.

Note: these layers reflect the project's stated design intent (README + tagline + CTEM methodology); no engine code is committed to the repository yet.

architecture_layers.name: Mobilisation / output layer (designed)
architecture_layers.description: Turns validated, prioritised exposures into remediation-ready output to close the continuous CTEM loop.

summary: Risk Operation Center (ROC) is the executive-facing capstone of a broad open-source security portfolio. Unlike the scanners, detection engines, and posture-management tools that surround it, ROC is not designed to produce its own findings — its charter is to consume the output of all those upstream tools and translate it into business risk that a CXO can actually act on. It sits in the 'risk' category as an aggregation and simulation layer rather than a point tool.

The problem it targets is a familiar one for security leaders: individual tools emit torrents of technical findings — CVEs, misconfigurations, detections, exposures — none of which map cleanly to a board-level decision. ROC's stated mission is to ingest that data, combine it with real business data and outcomes, and run simulations so leadership sees risk in decision-ready terms instead of raw severity scores. The goal is risk-informed prioritization: which exposures actually threaten the business, and where investment moves the needle.

Important context for this page: the repository is currently at a foundational, charter-defining stage. It contains a README stating the platform's mission, an MIT license, and project scaffolding — no application code has been committed yet. The architecture, capabilities, and controls described below reflect the platform's documented charter and intended design, not shipped functionality. They are presented so the portfolio accurately conveys where this initiative is headed while remaining honest about its current maturity.

features.title: Cross-Tool Finding Aggregation
features.description: Designed to consolidate output from the author's portfolio of open-source security tools into a single, unified risk picture.

features.title: Business-Context Risk Modeling
features.description: Charter calls for fusing technical findings with real business data and outcomes so risk is expressed in terms leadership understands.

features.title: Risk Simulation Engine
features.description: Intended to run simulations over aggregated data to model likely outcomes and 'what-if' scenarios that drive prioritization.

features.title: Executive Decision Support
features.description: Built to help CXOs make risk-informed decisions rather than parse CVSS scores and per-tool dashboards.

features.title: Outcome-Driven Prioritization
features.description: Aims to tie findings to real-world business outcomes instead of raw technical severity, focusing effort where it matters.

features.title: Portfolio-Wide Coverage Hub
features.description: Positioned as the central aggregation point spanning the many security domains covered by the upstream open-source tools.

features.title: Open-Source Foundation
features.description: MIT-licensed, making it a freely reusable starting point for organizations building their own risk operations center.

The layers below describe this intended data flow — the repository presently holds the mission statement and scaffolding rather than an implemented pipeline.

summary: Third-Party Risk Management is an early-stage, open-source initiative to build an agentic-AI platform for automated vendor risk assessments, security-questionnaire analysis, and continuous third-party monitoring. The repository's stated direction is captured in its own words: 'an open-source python based architecture with Agentic AI workflow.' It targets GRC and security teams who today drown in spreadsheet-based vendor questionnaires and point-in-time reviews.

Important context for readers: as of this writing the repository is at the scaffolding stage. It contains only project bootstrap files — a README declaring intent, an MIT LICENSE, and a boilerplate .gitignore — under a single 'Initial commit'. There is no application code, test suite, or CI pipeline in the repo yet. The capabilities described on this page reflect the project's declared scope and roadmap, not shipped functionality.

The problem it aims to solve is real and well understood: third-party risk programs are bottlenecked by manual questionnaire review, inconsistent scoring, and assessments that go stale the moment they are signed off. The intended answer here is an agentic AI workflow that ingests vendor evidence, reasons over questionnaire responses, and keeps a living risk posture per vendor. This detail page documents that vision and the current repository state transparently, so a security leader can gauge both the ambition and the maturity at a glance.

summary: Barracuda WAF Security Testing is a read-only Python auditor for Barracuda Web Application Firewall appliances. It authenticates to the WAF's REST API (v3.2, port 8443) and runs 169 configuration checks across 16 categories — WAF policies, SSL/TLS, access control, authentication, services, network, DDoS, bot protection, API security, logging, firmware, content rules, adaptive profiling, backup, licensing, and known-CVE matching — then produces a weighted posture score and an interactive report. Every API call is a GET (plus a login POST and logout DELETE), so the scanner never changes a single line of WAF configuration.

The problem it solves is that a WAF is only as strong as its policy configuration, and Barracuda's admin console spreads that configuration across dozens of screens: attack-action modes, cipher suites, cookie and parameter protection, rate limits, security headers, learning-mode profiles, syslog/SIEM forwarding, and firmware patch level. Auditing all of that by hand is slow and inconsistent. This tool codifies the vendor-hardening and CIS-style baseline into repeatable, versioned checks that a security engineer can run in minutes and diff over time.

It is aimed at WAF administrators, application-security teams, and auditors who need evidence-grade assurance that a Barracuda appliance is configured to block, not just monitor. Each finding carries a severity, the observed vs. expected value, a Barracuda-specific remediation step (often a ready-to-run REST API command), and a mapping to CIS, OWASP Top 10 2021, PCI DSS v4.0, and NIST CSF 2.0 controls — turning a raw config dump into a prioritized, framework-aligned action list.

Notable engineering touches: a resilient API client that tolerates the several token/auth-header formats different Barracuda firmware revisions return, a self-contained dark-theme HTML report with a posture ring gauge and severity/category filters, YAML scan profiles for targeted assessments, and CI-friendly exit codes (0 clean / 1 HIGH / 2 CRITICAL). The whole thing runs offline against mock responses in its 48-test suite, so contributors can extend checks without a live appliance.

features.title: 169 config checks, 16 categories
features.description: Audits WAF policies, SSL/TLS, access control, auth, services, network, DDoS, bots, API security, logging, firmware, content rules, adaptive profiling, backup, licensing, and CVEs in one pass.

features.description: Uses only GET requests (plus login POST and logout DELETE) so it can safely run against production appliances without touching configuration.

features.title: Weighted posture scoring with letter grades

features.title: Four-framework compliance mapping
features.description: Maps each finding to CIS Barracuda WAF Benchmark 1.0, OWASP Top 10 2021, PCI DSS v4.0, and NIST CSF 2.0 controls out of the box.

features.title: Known-CVE firmware matching

features.title: Interactive self-contained HTML report
