barracuda-waf-security-testing.architecture_summary: A single CLI entry point orchestrates a registry of 16 independent checker classes, each of which pulls configuration through one read-only REST API client, emits structured findings, and feeds them into a shared severity-scoring and compliance-mapping layer before rendering HTML/JSON reports. Data flows linearly: authenticate to the WAF, discover services/policies/certs, run enabled checkers, score, map to frameworks, report, and exit with a severity-based code.

architecture_layers.name: Scanner CLI (barracuda_waf_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: argparse-based entry point that loads scan profiles, resolves enabled categories from ALL_CHECKERS, orchestrates the run, prints a colored console summary, and sets the CI exit code (0/1/2).

architecture_layers.name: REST API client (utils/api_client.py — BarracudaWafClient)
architecture_layers.description: Read-only client for restapi/v3.2 handling token/Basic auth across firmware variants, multi-format token extraction, retries, connectivity verification, and SSL-verify toggling.

architecture_layers.name: Check modules (checks/ — 16 Checker classes)
architecture_layers.description: Each class (WafPoliciesChecker, SslTlsChecker, CveChecker, etc.) implements run_all() over ~134 private _check_ methods, appending findings with id, severity, actual/expected, and remediation.

architecture_layers.name: Config normalization (utils/config_helper.py)
architecture_layers.description: Helpers like safe_int, deep_get, and extract_config that flatten the inconsistent nested shapes the Barracuda API returns so checks stay robust.

architecture_layers.name: Severity & scoring (utils/severity.py)
architecture_layers.description: Defines severity weights and order, computes the 0-100 posture score, converts it to an A-F grade, and tallies per-severity counts.

architecture_layers.name: Compliance mapping (config/compliance_maps.yaml)
architecture_layers.description: 130 finding-ID mappings tying each check to CIS, OWASP, PCI DSS, and NIST CSF controls, extensible for additional frameworks.

architecture_layers.name: Report generator (utils/report_generator.py)
architecture_layers.description: Produces the self-contained dark-theme HTML report (posture ring, severity filters, category chart) and the structured JSON report.

project_structure.description: Main CLI entry point — argument parsing, profile loading, checker orchestration, console summary, and exit codes.
16 category checker modules (waf_policies, ssl_tls, access_control, authentication, cve_checks, ...) registered in __init__.py as ALL_CHECKERS.
Largest module — 25 WAF policy checks covering attack actions, request limits, cookie/parameter protection, URL normalization, and JSON/XML rules.
Firmware CVE matcher with a curated KNOWN_CVES database (12 entries) including CVSS scores and upgrade recommendations.
BarracudaWafClient — the read-only REST API client with multi-format token auth, retries, and SSL toggle.
HTML and JSON report generation, including the posture ring gauge and severity/category visualizations.
Severity weights, posture-score computation, A-F grading, and per-severity counting.
safe_int / deep_get / extract_config helpers that normalize the WAF API's nested response shapes.
Finding-to-framework mappings for CIS, OWASP Top 10 2021, PCI DSS v4.0, and NIST CSF 2.0.
Default YAML scan profile enabling all check categories; template for targeted profiles.
48 unit tests exercising all 16 check modules against mock API responses (tests/test_data/mock_responses.json).
Runtime dependencies: requests, pyyaml, jinja2, urllib3.

security_controls.detail: Verifies attack action is set to deny-and-log, request/response limits, cookie security (encryption, HttpOnly, tamper-proofing), parameter protection, URL normalization, data-theft prevention, and JSON/XML payload rules.

Flags weak protocols (SSLv2/3, TLS 1.0/1.1) and cipher suites (RC4, DES, 3DES, EXPORT, MD5), and checks TLS 1.3, perfect forward secrecy, HSTS, OCSP stapling, client-cert config, plus certificate expiry, key size, and signature algorithm.
Audits IP ACLs, geo-blocking, rate-limiting/rate-control policies, brute-force prevention, IP reputation, and URL access rules.
Checks admin MFA, password policy strength, session timeout, LDAP/SAML security, role-based access, and account lockout thresholds.

Matches appliance firmware against curated Barracuda and embedded-component CVEs — including the actively-exploited CVE-2023-2868, Spreadsheet::ParseExcel RCEs, mod_proxy SSRF, and Log4Shell exposure — with CVSS scores and fixed-version guidance.
Evaluates slow-client/Slowloris defenses, connection limits, SYN flood, HTTP flood, and per-client request rate caps.
Assesses bot detection, CAPTCHA, client fingerprinting, JavaScript challenge, signature blocking, and web-scraping protection.

security_controls.area: Security Response Headers & Content Rules (11 checks)
security_controls.detail: Checks CSP, X-Frame-Options, HSTS, Referrer-Policy, open-redirect exposure, and URL rewriting in content rules.
Confirms syslog forwarding, SIEM integration via CEF/LEEF, WAF and audit logging, and alerting are configured for detection and forensics.

Covers scheduled/encrypted/offsite backups and HA config sync, management-access restriction, SNMPv3, Telnet/SSH exposure and VLAN segmentation, plus positive-security learning-mode and URL/parameter profiling.

summary: LogOcean is a self-hosted, agentless SIEM that ingests network, endpoint, cloud, identity, and OT/ICS logs from 29 vendors, auto-detects each format, normalizes every record to one common schema, and retains everything in PostgreSQL for three years or more. It takes logs through three front doors — a web upload page, an HTTP ingest API with API-key auth, and a syslog receiver (UDP/TCP/TLS) fronted by a bounded async queue — so a burst of live traffic never blocks the receiver. There are no agents to deploy: you point collectors, forwarders, and other tools at it.

Ingested events are matched inline against a Sigma-compatible detection engine (90 detection rules) while a background scheduler runs 10 correlation/threshold rules over the event store, raising alerts you triage at /alerts and group into cases. On top of the detection core sit the pieces a SOC actually needs: threat-intel IOC enrichment, webhook/email notifications, agentless response playbooks with time-boxed auto-revert, UEBA entity-risk scoring, kill-chain reconstruction that stitches related alerts into multi-tactic attack stories, and a detection-engineering workbench that maps coverage and flags noisy or dead rules.

What sets LogOcean apart is that its detection posture is measured, not asserted: a /coverage scoreboard computes MITRE ATT&CK (Enterprise + ICS) and MITRE ATLAS coverage directly from the rules, exports ATT&CK Navigator layers, and is enforced by a CI rule-linter. A community SigmaHQ importer translates the open rule corpus into the engine, and an optional Claude-powered SOC copilot explains alerts, summarizes cases, and drafts Sigma rules from plain English. Passive OT/ICS monitoring ingests Zeek + ICSNPP telemetry (Modbus/DNP3/S7comm/CIP) with an ATT&CK-for-ICS rule pack and IEC 62443 / NERC CIP mapping — without ever touching a device.

It is built for security engineers and small-to-mid SOC teams who want a real, auditable SIEM they fully control — no per-GB licensing, no cloud lock-in, no JavaScript build step. The whole stack is Python + FastAPI + PostgreSQL 16, server-rendered, and tested unit + integration against a real database in CI across Python 3.11–3.13.

features.title: 29 auto-detected vendor parsers
features.description: Ingests network, endpoint, cloud/identity, private-cloud (Nutanix), OT/ICS, and generic CEF/LEEF/syslog/JSON logs, auto-detecting the format on upload and normalizing every one to a single schema.

features.title: Three agentless ingest paths
features.description: Web upload, an HTTP ingest API with API-key auth and gzip/bomb-guarded bodies, and a UDP/TCP/TLS syslog receiver fronted by a bounded async queue so a burst never blocks intake.

features.title: Sigma-based detection & correlation engine
features.description: Ships 90 per-event detection rules plus 10 threshold/cardinality correlation rules, supporting the common Sigma modifiers (contains/cidr/base64offset/windash/fieldref) so many community rules load unmodified.

features.title: MITRE ATT&CK + ATLAS coverage scoreboard
features.description: A /coverage page computes Enterprise + ICS + ATLAS coverage from the rules themselves, rolled up by tactic, fidelity, and data source, with ATT&CK Navigator layer export and a CI rule-linter.

features.title: Community SigmaHQ importer
features.description: Translates thousands of open ATT&CK-tagged SigmaHQ rules into the engine by mapping Sigma logsource to a gate over normalized fields, honestly skipping anything it cannot faithfully run.

features.title: Triage, cases & saved searches
features.description: Acknowledge/assign/annotate alerts, build suppression/allowlist rules to cut noise, group related alerts into cases with severity roll-up, and save per-user queries as one-click chips.

features.title: Notifications & agentless response
features.description: Delivers new alerts to webhook/email channels and runs response playbooks that POST containment intents to your SOAR/firewall/IAM, with time-boxed auto-revert that fires the inverse action on expiry.

features.title: UEBA entity risk & kill-chain reconstruction
features.description: Pure-PostgreSQL behavioural analytics baseline every user/host/IP for new-entity/new-association anomalies and stitch related alerts across ATT&CK tactics into narrated attack stories.

features.title: AI SOC copilot (Claude)
features.description: Optional, off-by-default assistant that explains alerts, summarizes cases in ATT&CK order, and drafts Sigma-subset rules from natural language, all RBAC-gated and audited.

features.title: Passive OT/ICS monitoring
features.description: Enriches Zeek + ICSNPP telemetry (Modbus/DNP3/S7comm/CIP) into a control-plane action + ot.* fields, with an ATT&CK-for-ICS rule pack, controller asset inventory, and master to controller baselining.

features.title: Partitioned PostgreSQL storage with 3-year retention
features.description: Events are RANGE-partitioned by month with GIN full-text + jsonb indexes; purge is an instant partition drop and re-ingesting the same file is idempotent via a dedup hash.

features.title: Auth, RBAC & audit trail
features.description: Built-in login with admin/analyst/viewer roles, pbkdf2 passwords, server-side sessions, security headers + CSRF, a last-admin lockout guard, and an audit log of every security-relevant action.

logocean-siem.architecture_summary: Three ingest inputs share one source-agnostic core pipeline: detect format, parse with a vendor parser, normalize to a common NormalizedEvent schema, and store in month-partitioned PostgreSQL. Live sources buffer in a bounded async queue drained by batching writer workers. Every event is evaluated inline against per-event detection + threat-intel rules and updates UEBA baselines as it is stored, while a background scheduler runs correlation SQL over the event store; both raise rows in the alerts table that fan out to notifications and agentless response.

architecture_layers.name: Ingest front doors
architecture_layers.description: Web upload, HTTP ingest API (app/api.py, API-key auth, gzip-aware), and the UDP/TCP/TLS syslog receiver (app/receivers/syslog.py) feeding a bounded async queue (app/streaming.py).

architecture_layers.name: Parse & normalize core
architecture_layers.description: app/detect.py auto-detects format, app/parsers/ (29 parsers) parse to NormalizedEvent, and app/pipeline.py runs the source-agnostic parse to normalize to insert path with dedup + full-text blob (app/normalize.py).

architecture_layers.name: Storage layer
architecture_layers.description: app/db.py + schema.sql manage the RANGE-partitioned events table (monthly partitions, GIN full-text + jsonb, btree indexes) with idempotent ON CONFLICT ingest and instant partition-drop retention.

architecture_layers.name: Detection & correlation engines
architecture_layers.description: app/detection/engine.py is the per-event Sigma-subset evaluator; correlation.py runs threshold/cardinality rules over SQL on a scheduler; runtime.py loads and syncs the rule registry from rules/*.yml.

architecture_layers.name: Alerting & response
architecture_layers.description: app/alert_actions.py fans newly-raised alerts to app/notify/ (webhook/email) and app/response/ (agentless playbooks + stateful auto-revert), all on background threads.

architecture_layers.name: Analytics & coverage
architecture_layers.description: Pure, DB-light modules: risk.py (UEBA), killchain.py (attack stories), workbench.py (rule tuning), coverage.py (ATT&CK/ATLAS scoreboard), navigator.py (layer export), ot.py (ICS analytics).

architecture_layers.name: Enrichment & collectors
architecture_layers.description: app/threatintel/ (IOC matcher + feed loader) and app/collectors/ (agentless pull connectors for Okta/GitHub/GitLab/AWS/Entra/M365/GCP with SigV4, OAuth, and signed-JWT auth).

architecture_layers.name: Web UI & API
architecture_layers.description: app/main.py serves FastAPI routes and server-rendered Jinja2 pages (dashboard, search, alerts, cases, killchain, risk, workbench, coverage, compliance, ot, admin) with no JS build step.

project_structure.description: FastAPI routes, server-rendered Jinja2 UI, and application lifespan wiring.
Source-agnostic parse to normalize to insert core, with inline detection, threat-intel, and UEBA baselining.
29 vendor/format parsers plus the zeek_ics OT/ICS enrichment helper.
Sigma-subset per-event engine, correlation/threshold engine, and the rule-registry runtime.
Pure ATT&CK (Enterprise + ICS) + ATLAS detection-coverage scoreboard computed from the rules.
Agentless pull connectors (Okta/GitHub/GitLab/AWS/Entra/M365/GCP) plus the polling scheduler.
90 detection + 10 correlation Sigma-subset YAML rules, plus imported/ for generated SigmaHQ imports.
Agentless response playbooks (match + action YAML) including block-brute-force and log-high-severity.
Partitioned events table, FTS/jsonb/btree indexes, and all supporting tables (alerts, cases, iocs, users...).
CLI that translates a SigmaHQ clone into rules/imported/*.yml for the engine.
Unit (DB-free) and integration (real Postgres) tiers — 303 test functions across 32 files.
Living, phased detection-coverage programme plan.

security_controls.detail: 90 per-event rules with full modifier support (contains/startswith/endswith/re, cidr, numeric lt/gt, exists, fieldref, base64/base64offset/windash) and the and/or/not + N-of condition grammar, each exception-isolated so one bad rule can't sink the rest.

10 threshold rules over the event store — brute-force/connection-flood bursts plus distinct-count behaviours: password spray (one source to many accounts), distributed brute force, port scan, host sweep, and OT-protocol enumeration.

LSASS memory access & credential-dumper tools, NTDS/SAM extraction, remote-thread injection, BYOVD driver load, UAC hijack, LSA/AppInit persistence, WDigest, Defender/AMSI/ETW tamper, PsExec/msiexec/BITS, and Cobalt Strike named pipes — each fidelity-tagged and IOC-verified.

AWS (CloudTrail logging/GuardDuty/Config disabled, admin-policy attach, public S3, access-key/root login), GCP (privileged IAM grant, SA key, logging-sink delete), Azure (RBAC assignment, diagnostic-settings/Key Vault tamper), Okta/M365/GitHub/GitLab identity abuse — API operation names adversarially verified.

Request-line detections for SQLi, path traversal/LFI, OS command injection, XSS, web-shell access, and scanner UAs firing across Apache/Nginx + Zeek + Suricata HTTP; Suricata IDS passthrough; and auditd rules for reverse shells, setuid backdoors, sudoers/SSH/cron persistence, and /etc/shadow access.

Modbus write/diagnostic, S7comm program-download/PLC-stop, DNP3 restart/disable-unsolicited, CIP set-attribute write, an IT-to-OT conduit-violation rule enforcing the Purdue/IEC 62443 zone model via cidr selections, and an OT-enumeration correlation — all tagged with T0NNN techniques.

Matches every event's src/dst IPs (exact + CIDR), domains, hashes, and URLs — pulled from normalized fields, raw, and free-text message — against in-memory IOC feeds (file or http, line/CSV/JSON), raising a Threat Intelligence Match alert at the highest matched severity.

A /coverage scoreboard measures ATT&CK Enterprise (~82 techniques), ICS (11), and ATLAS coverage by tactic/fidelity/data-source, with Navigator export and a CI rule-linter enforcing unique ids, valid level/fidelity, and an attack.*/atlas.* tag per rule.

Size-capped streamed uploads/API bodies, a version-stable JSON deep-nesting depth guard (rejected before json.loads), a decompression-bomb cap on gzip, CSV formula-injection neutralization, and fully parameterized whitelisted-column search/correlation SQL.

Built-in login with admin/analyst/viewer roles, pbkdf2 passwords, revocable server-side sessions, always-on security headers (X-Frame-Options DENY, nosniff, CSP frame-ancestors none), CSRF same-origin checks, a last-admin lockout guard, and an audit log of every sensitive action.

summary: IT-GRC Portal is a self-hosted, open-source Governance, Risk & Compliance platform built specifically to run an ISO/IEC 27001:2022 ISMS end to end. Instead of a spreadsheet graveyard of controls and evidence, it ships all 93 Annex A controls and all 30 mandatory management-system clauses (4-10) pre-seeded on first boot, then gives every module a real workflow: owners, review dates, conformity status, tasks, approvals, and audit-tracked sign-off. Clone it, `docker compose up`, wire it to your corporate IdP over SAML/OIDC, and you have a working ISMS the same afternoon.

What sets it apart from a single-standard tool is the Unified Control Framework. Five catalogs live side by side - ISO 27001:2022 (93), ISO/IEC 27019:2024 energy-sector ENR controls (12), NIST CSF 2.0 (22 categories), SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria (13), and ISA/IEC 62443-2-1:2024 OT program elements (8) - joined by a control-to-control crosswalk (96 seeded mappings) and a cross-framework coverage matrix. That includes a rarely-seen IT-ISMS to OT-security bridge (66 ISO/27019 to IEC 62443 mappings), making this one of the few open GRC tools that speaks both the IT and the industrial/energy compliance dialects: test once, comply many.

The portal covers the full ISMS lifecycle: a 5x5 risk register with inherent-vs-residual scoring and a heat map, Statement of Applicability, CMMI-style maturity self-assessments and vendor questionnaires, the Clause 7.5 documented-information register (17 mandatory records), interested parties (4.2), IS objectives and KPI/KRI/KCI metrics with auto-derived RAG status (6.2/9.1), a supplier/third-party register (5.19-5.23), incident management (5.24-5.28), awareness & training (7.2/7.3), evidence, audits and findings, and policies with attestation. A daily posture snapshot feeds compliance, conformity, document-readiness and training-completion trend charts with no scheduler required.

It is aimed at CISOs, GRC managers, internal auditors, and control/risk owners who want to prepare for and maintain certification without paying for a proprietary SaaS GRC suite - while keeping their ISMS data inside their own perimeter. The stack is production-shaped (FastAPI async backend, React 18 SPA, PostgreSQL 16, Alembic migrations, RBAC with six roles) and CI-verified, but it is honest about its scope: it organizes and evidences your ISMS, it does not replace an accredited external audit.

itgrc-portal-iso27001.architecture_summary: A three-tier full-stack web app: a React 18 + TypeScript SPA talks over a versioned REST API (/api/v1) to an async FastAPI backend backed by PostgreSQL 16. On startup the app runs Alembic migrations and idempotently seeds all control catalogs, clauses, documents, sample data, and RBAC roles, so a fresh database becomes a working ISMS with no manual steps. Derived posture numbers flow through single-source-of-truth helpers and a daily snapshot table that grows the trend charts without a scheduler.

architecture_layers.name: React SPA (frontend/src)
architecture_layers.description: 34 route pages (module lists + detail pages) plus Layout/StatusBadge components, an axios API client with a JWT interceptor, an auth context hook, and Recharts dashboards; built with Vite and Tailwind.

architecture_layers.name: FastAPI route layer (backend/app/api)
architecture_layers.description: 24 route modules, one per domain (controls, clauses, risks, soa, assessments, tasks, analytics, suppliers, incidents, training, reports, reminders, ...), all mounted under /api/v1 with Swagger/ReDoc docs.

architecture_layers.name: Domain & schema layer (models + schemas)
architecture_layers.description: 22 SQLAlchemy 2.0 async ORM models with UUID PKs and timezone-aware timestamps, paired with Pydantic v2 Create/Update/Read schemas; derived logic like compute_rag, task_is_overdue, and aggregate_score lives on the models.

architecture_layers.name: Seed engine (seed/iso27001.py)
architecture_layers.description: A ~1,300-line, SQLAlchemy-import-free data module holding all five framework catalogs, the crosswalk, ISMS clauses, mandatory documents, and sample records, seeded via per-framework idempotent gates.

architecture_layers.name: Analytics & posture engine (api/analytics.py)
architecture_layers.description: compute_headline() is the single source of truth for headline posture scores and record_posture_snapshot() upserts one row per day (triggered by the dashboard call) to build the posture-trend time series.

architecture_layers.name: Data & migration layer
architecture_layers.description: PostgreSQL 16 accessed via asyncpg, schema managed by Alembic with a baseline migration built from Base.metadata and autogenerate for changes; runs alembic upgrade head automatically on boot.

architecture_layers.name: Auth & RBAC layer
architecture_layers.description: JWT (python-jose) with bcrypt-hashed passwords (passlib), SAML/OIDC-ready IdP hooks, and six default roles enforced through shared FastAPI dependencies (get_current_user, require_superuser).

summary: Most autonomous red-team agents put one LLM in a tool-use loop and hope it stays on task across a multi-day engagement. RedChain takes the opposite stance: it runs an offensive security engagement as a deterministic state machine of Kill Chain phases, where each phase is driven by a purpose-built specialist agent, produces a schema-validated artifact, and cannot advance until a hard-coded gate confirms that artifact is complete. The orchestrator — not the model — owns every phase transition.

Built on the Anthropic SDK, RedChain trades the freewheeling autonomy of loop-based agents for reproducibility, auditability, and structured deliverables. Every engagement lives in a self-contained directory: a manifest, a SQLite state store recording phase status and gate decisions, one markdown artifact per phase, and a full JSONL prompt/response transcript for every agent run. Engagements checkpoint to disk and resume exactly where a gate paused them, which is what makes multi-day, multi-analyst campaigns tractable.

The comparison the README draws is precise — autonomous loops track phases but never enforce them, decide their own next step, and leave you a conversation log; RedChain enforces phases in code, keeps transitions deterministic, and leaves you an artifact-per-phase plus a per-gate transcript audit trail. It is the right tool when a red team needs signed-off scope, defensible evidence, and a report they can hand to a SOC — not when they want a model to freelance against a live target.

This is an honest Alpha (v0.1.0): three of the nine Kill Chain phases — scope, recon, and report — are wired end-to-end behind the webapp preset, with the remaining six (weaponize, delivery, exploit, installation, C2, objectives) scaffolded as registry stubs. Critically, the MVP plans and validates rather than weaponizes: agents synthesize scope briefs and recon dossiers and explicitly refuse to actively scan or exploit, making it safe to demonstrate while the offensive phases mature.

features.title: Gated phase state machine
features.description: Each phase must pass a code-enforced gate before the orchestrator advances, so a scope brief with no objectives or a recon dossier with no entrypoints hard-stops the pipeline instead of silently proceeding.

features.title: Specialist agent per phase
features.description: Distinct system-prompted agents (Planner for scope/report, Network Analyst for recon) each own a narrow deliverable, rather than one general agent improvising every step.

features.title: Validated artifact per phase
features.description: Every phase emits a Pydantic-modeled, Jinja2-rendered markdown artifact where the structured model is the source of truth and the markdown is the human-readable view.

features.title: Checkpoint and resume
features.description: Engagement state persists to SQLite between phases so `redchain resume` picks up at the next unsatisfied phase — built for multi-day campaigns that span sessions.

features.title: Full transcript audit trail
features.description: Every agent invocation writes a JSONL prompt/response transcript with token-usage metadata, giving a defensible, replayable record of exactly what each agent saw and produced.

features.title: Dry-run mode with fixtures
features.description: `--dry-run` swaps live API calls for canned fixture responses, letting the entire orchestrator run end-to-end in CI or offline without an API key.

features.title: Prompt caching built in
features.description: System prompts are sent with ephemeral cache_control so repeated agent calls across a long engagement reuse cached context and cut token cost.

features.title: Preset-driven engagements
features.description: YAML presets declare the phase order and default model for an engagement class (e.g. webapp), making engagement types reproducible and versionable.

features.title: Plan-not-weaponize safety posture
features.description: Recon and scope agents are instructed to synthesize passive findings and never actively scan or exploit, keeping the MVP safe to demonstrate against real targets.

features.title: Queryable vulnerability reference
features.description: A CWE-tagged pattern library (SQLi, XSS, IDOR, SSRF, open redirect) is loadable by tag to seed findings with validation and remediation guidance.

features.title: Tool integration wrappers
features.description: A subprocess integration layer (nmap -sV with XML parsing) degrades gracefully to an explanatory stub when the binary is absent, rather than crashing the phase.

features.title: Rich CLI with status inspection
features.description: `engage`, `resume`, `status`, and `list-presets` commands render phase state, gate reasons, and artifacts as color tables or machine-readable JSON.

redchain-orchestrator.architecture_summary: RedChain is a Python package (src/redchain) layered as a runtime that owns control flow, a set of Kill Chain phases each paired with a gate, specialist agents that wrap the Anthropic SDK, and supporting skills, integrations, templates, presets, and a vulnerability reference. The CLI loads a YAML preset, provisions an engagement directory with a SQLite state store and manifest, then the Orchestrator iterates the preset's phases: for each it runs the phase's agent(s), renders a Pydantic-validated artifact from a Jinja2 template, and calls the gate; a failed gate marks the phase failed_gate and pauses the run for resume, while a pass records completion and advances.

architecture_layers.name: runtime/ (Orchestrator + stores)
architecture_layers.description: Orchestrator drives phase transitions; EngagementState is the SQLite store (phases, gate_decisions, transcripts tables); ArtifactStore renders/persists Jinja2 artifacts; AgentSession is the Anthropic wrapper with dry-run and transcript logging.

architecture_layers.name: phases/ (Kill Chain stages)
architecture_layers.description: One module per phase (scope, recon, report wired; six stubbed) defining the artifact model, agent dispatch, and prompt; PHASE_REGISTRY maps preset names to (Phase, Gate) pairs.

architecture_layers.name: gates/ (artifact validators)
architecture_layers.description: Per-phase validators (ScopeGate, ReconGate, ReportGate) that inspect the rendered artifact for required sections and non-empty content and return a pass/fail GateDecision that blocks advance.

architecture_layers.name: agents/ (specialist LLM roles)
architecture_layers.description: System-prompted Agent subclasses — Planner (scope + report) and NetworkAnalyst (recon) — registered in AGENT_REGISTRY, each producing JSON-only responses parsed into artifact models.

architecture_layers.name: skills/ + integrations/ (reusable helpers + tools)
architecture_layers.description: Skills are reusable Python helpers (recon_osint stub); integrations are subprocess tool wrappers (nmap) with timeout handling and graceful degradation when the tool is missing.

architecture_layers.name: templates/ + presets/ + vulnref/ (content)
architecture_layers.description: Jinja2 templates render artifacts (scope brief, recon dossier, executive report); YAML presets declare phase order and default model; vulnref is a CWE-tagged, tag-queryable vulnerability pattern library.

project_structure.description: Orchestrator, SQLite state store, artifact store, and the Anthropic AgentSession wrapper with dry-run support.
One module per Kill Chain phase (scope, recon, report implemented) plus base Phase class and PHASE_REGISTRY.
Artifact-completeness validators (scope_gate, recon_gate, report_gate) that block phase advancement.
Specialist agent classes (Planner, NetworkAnalyst) with system prompts and the AGENT_REGISTRY.
Jinja2 templates for the three artifacts: scope_brief, recon_dossier, executive_report.
YAML engagement templates; webapp.yaml defines the MVP scope->recon->report flow and default model.

Reusable Python helpers invokable from any phase; recon_osint is a stub for future passive OSINT providers.

security_controls.detail: Structures engagements as a nine-stage Kill Chain (scope, recon, weaponize, delivery, exploit, installation, C2, objectives, report); v0.1.0 enforces scope, recon, and report end-to-end.
Blocks advance unless the scope brief contains In Scope, Objectives, and Authorization sections and the In Scope and Objectives sections are non-empty (placeholder-aware).
Requires the recon dossier to declare at least one entrypoint or service (via list/table row counting) so downstream weaponization has something to act on.
Requires Executive Summary, Findings, and Next Steps sections and a substantive (>=60 char) executive summary before the engagement can complete.
Agents assume authorized engagements, mark proposed defaults explicitly in the scope brief, and capture authorization_notes and rules_of_engagement as first-class artifact fields.
The Network Analyst synthesizes recon from scope and public knowledge, separates confirmed facts from hypotheses (leads), and is instructed not to actively scan the target in the MVP.
Ships five web vulnerability patterns — SQLi (CWE-89), Reflected XSS (CWE-79), IDOR (CWE-639), SSRF (CWE-918), Open Redirect (CWE-601) — each with validation and remediation notes.
The report artifact model constrains findings to a fixed severity scale (critical/high/medium/low/info) with title, description, evidence, and recommendation.
Convention mandates that agent transcripts never store secrets; integrations must accept a timeout and never inject unsanitized strings into subprocess calls.
SQLite records every gate decision with reason and timestamp, and each agent run persists a full JSONL transcript with token usage for post-engagement review.

summary: Exploit PoC & Detection Lab is a purple-team repository that reproduces five publicly disclosed, patched vulnerabilities and techniques so blue teams can prove their detections actually fire. Every pack is self-contained and follows one hard rule set: benign by default (a PoC only ever writes a proof file or prints the run-as identity), impactful actions gated behind clearly-labeled off-by-default switches, and every exploit shipped alongside the detection content needed to catch it.

The catalog spans four CVEs and one ATT&CK technique across a deliberate spread of attack surfaces: a Windows AD DS / Network Configuration Operators local privilege escalation to SYSTEM (CVE-2025-21293), two unauthenticated RCEs that were mass-exploited in the wild and added to CISA KEV — WSUS .NET deserialization (CVE-2025-59287) and SAP NetWeaver Visual Composer file-upload (CVE-2025-31324) — an LSASS credential-dump technique via the signed LOLBin comsvcs.dll (T1003.001), and a safe, non-destructive assessment kit for a wormable HTTP.sys kernel RCE (CVE-2026-47291).

What makes it notable is the discipline. The HTTP.sys pack deliberately ships no weaponized exploit — because firing a kernel heap overflow BSODs the host and no public root-cause exists — and instead gives a definitive patched/vulnerable verdict from the MSRC-documented MaxRequestBytes condition. The SAP pack's benign JSP probe proves arbitrary-Java RCE without dropping a usable command shell, and even ships a YARA rule for its own probe so defenders can tell an authorized test from a real attacker. It's a reference for how to publish offensive research responsibly.

The audience is detection engineers, SOC teams, red/purple teamers, and researchers who want to validate coverage in an isolated, snapshotted lab. Each pack documents the vuln (CVSS, affected/patched versions, root cause), the exact run and cleanup steps, remediation guidance, and Sigma/YARA/Suricata/KQL/Sysmon detections mapped to MITRE ATT&CK.

features.title: Five curated exploit/technique packs
features.description: Four disclosed CVEs plus one ATT&CK technique, each a self-contained, isolated directory that is runnable and cleanable on its own with no cross-pack dependencies.

features.title: Benign-by-default payloads
features.description: Default payloads only prove execution (write a proof file or print the run-as identity); genuinely impactful actions like spawning a SYSTEM shell or adding an admin are compile-time/runtime switches that ship OFF.

features.title: Detection paired with every exploit
features.description: No pack is complete without Sigma, and most add YARA, Suricata, KQL hunting queries, and Sysmon config so defenders can validate the full telemetry-to-alert pipeline.

features.title: Safe assessment over weaponization
features.description: For the wormable HTTP.sys kernel RCE, the repo ships a non-destructive checker (patch state, MaxRequestBytes config, exposure) instead of a BSOD-inducing overflow, giving a definitive verdict at zero risk to the target.

features.title: Gated, self-cleaning red-team drivers
features.description: The SAP and WSUS drivers default to dry-run/probe, gate the actual upload behind --confirm, track dropped artifacts in state files, and provide reliable --cleanup and self-destruct so tests leave no residue.

features.title: Detection-first credential-access lab
features.description: The LSASS/comsvcs pack exists to generate telemetry: a dry-run-by-default trigger whose robust detection keys on any process opening lsass.exe with a dump access mask (Sysmon EID 10), not a brittle string match.

features.title: Reproducible lab provisioning
features.description: Packs include setup/cleanup scripts (create the Network Configuration Operators lab user, install the WSUS role) to stand up a vulnerable target and tear it down safely.

features.title: Before/after patch demonstration
features.description: Each README documents the exact fix (KB / SAP Note / registry ACL change) and how to verify it, so a pack doubles as a patch-efficacy and remediation-validation exercise.

features.title: MITRE ATT&CK-mapped Sigma
features.description: All ten Sigma rules carry attack.* tags spanning eight techniques (T1546, T1068, T1190, T1505.003, T1059.001/003, T1003.001, T1218.011) for coverage tracking.

features.title: Authoritative references per pack
features.description: Every README cites NVD, MSRC/vendor advisories, CISA KEV, and original research so findings are auditable and grounded, not hand-waved.

exploit-poc.architecture_summary: The repo is not a single application but a collection of five uniform PoC packs, each following the canonical template defined in CLAUDE.md: a README, a driver, optional payload source, lab provisioning scripts, and a detection/ folder. A pack's driver walks a linear pipeline — setup/register, trigger in the privileged context, check for proof, then clean up — while detection content is authored in parallel so the exploit and its signatures ship together.

architecture_layers.name: Driver / entrypoint
architecture_layers.description: A Python exploit.py or PowerShell trigger (or the CVE-2026-47291 safe checker) that orchestrates setup -> trigger -> check -> cleanup with argparse flags and safety gates like --confirm and dry-run defaults.

architecture_layers.name: Payload layer
architecture_layers.description: Minimal benign proof artifacts kept separate from the driver: the x64 perfctr_payload.c performance-counter DLL with off-by-default SPAWN_SHELL/ADD_ADMIN defines, and the identity-fingerprint poc_probe.jsp that self-destructs.

architecture_layers.name: Lab provisioning
architecture_layers.description: setup_lab / cleanup_lab / install_wsus scripts that build a vulnerable, isolated target (lab user in Network Configuration Operators, WSUS role) and reliably tear it down.

architecture_layers.name: Detection layer
architecture_layers.description: Per-pack detection/ folder holding Sigma rules, Sysmon EID configs, YARA signatures, Suricata IDS rules, and KQL hunting queries that fire on the telemetry the driver generates.

architecture_layers.name: Remediation & docs
architecture_layers.description: Each README ties the pack together with CVSS scoring, affected/patched matrices, exact run/cleanup steps, remediation (patch KB / SAP Note / hardening), and authoritative references.

Repo conventions and hard safety guardrails: authorized-lab-only, benign-by-default, pair-with-detection, never-weaponize.

SAP NetWeaver Visual Composer unauth file-upload RCE (CISA KEV); benign JSP probe, gated upload/cleanup driver, and Sigma/YARA/KQL detection.
Detection-first LSASS credential dump via comsvcs.dll; dry-run PowerShell trigger with Sysmon/Sigma/KQL and RunAsPPL/Credential Guard hardening.
HTTP.sys kernel RCE safe-assessment pack: non-destructive checker (no overflow) plus Sigma/Suricata/KQL crash-and-oversized-request detection.
The detection folder in each pack: Sigma rules, Sysmon XML, YARA, Suricata rules, and KQL hunting queries for blue-team validation.
Open-source license for the repository.
Ignore rules (README notes it needs extending to exclude native build output for compiled payloads).

security_controls.detail: Registers a perf-counter DLL under Dnscache/NetBT service keys and forces WmiPrvSE.exe (SYSTEM) to load it via a WMI query; detected with Sysmon EID 12/13/7 Sigma rules for perf-DLL registration outside System32 and unusual WmiPrvSE image loads.

AES-128-CBC-encrypted AuthorizationCookie deserialization gadget over SOAP to Client/Reporting web services; detected via w3wp.exe/wsusservice.exe spawning a shell (near-zero-FP process-chain rule) and suspicious large POSTs to .asmx endpoints.

Missing-authorization Metadata Uploader POST drops a JSP that runs as <sid>adm; detected by uploader POST + webshell GET Sigma, a YARA rule for JSP command webshells, and SAP-process-spawns-shell hunting.

LSASS memory dump via signed comsvcs.dll MiniDump; high-fidelity detection keys on lsass.exe access masks (0x1010/0x1410/0x1438/0x143a/0x1fffff) in Sysmon EID 10 plus the rundll32+comsvcs+MiniDump command line.

Non-destructive checker reads OS build, hotfixes, HTTP service state, and MaxRequestBytes/MaxFieldLength to verdict patched vs. the MSRC-stated vulnerable config (>=65534), plus benign remote http.sys fingerprinting — never sends an oversized request.

Impactful behavior is compiled/flagged OFF (perfctr_payload.c SPAWN_SHELL/ADD_ADMIN defines default 0; JSP probe has no command exec; no bundled ?cmd= webshell or payload generator), keeping public artifacts non-weaponized.

Packs prescribe the fix and prevention: patch KBs / SAP Notes 3594142+3604119, RunAsPPL, Credential Guard, the Defender ASR LSASS rule, SeDebugPrivilege restriction, and MaxRequestBytes/firewall mitigations.
10 Sigma rules (attack.* tagged across 8 ATT&CK techniques), 3 YARA rules, 3 Suricata IDS SIDs, 2 Sysmon configs, and 4 KQL hunting query sets for Defender XDR / Sentinel.

summary: Large language models opened a new attack surface that traditional WAFs and DLP tools never anticipated: the prompt itself. Prompt injection, jailbreaks, encoded instruction smuggling, and models that leak secrets or their own system prompt now sit between your application and any hosted LLM. AI Runtime Guardrail is a provider-agnostic inspection proxy that drops into that gap. Point your app's LLM base URL at the guardrail and it inspects every prompt and every completion in real time, enforcing block / redact / allow / alert policy on the way in and on the way out.

The design philosophy is cheapest-first layering. A microsecond YAML signature layer runs before an entropy/obfuscation heuristic layer, which runs before an optional Claude-based LLM judge that only fires as a backstop when the cheap layers find nothing. That keeps inline latency low and cost bounded while still catching novel attacks. Detection is data, not code: injection and DLP rules live in YAML packs you extend by appending a name/regex/severity, and enforcement thresholds live in a policy file with per-category overrides.

It is built to be safe by default. Monitor mode (offline log replay) never blocks, inline enforcement is opt-in, and the LLM judge is off unless explicitly enabled and fails open so a broken detector or API outage never takes down live traffic. Every verdict is written to an immutable JSONL audit chain, enforcement events forward to a SIEM via file or webhook, and each detection is tagged to OWASP LLM Top 10, NIST AI RMF, and MITRE ATLAS controls for instant GRC mapping.

It is positioned as the defensive runtime counterpart to offensive LLM red-team tooling: the attacks those tools generate become this firewall's regression corpus, validated by an adversarial benchmark that doubles as a CI gate (fail the build if recall drops or false positives climb). It suits AI platform teams, application security engineers, and GRC owners who need enforceable, auditable guardrails in front of Claude, OpenAI-compatible, or self-hosted models.

features.title: Inline reverse proxy
features.description: A drop-in FastAPI proxy: repoint your app's LLM base URL at the guardrail and it inspects, enforces, and forwards to the real upstream transparently.

features.title: Bidirectional inspection
features.description: Enforces policy on inputs (injection, jailbreak, PII/secret leakage) and outputs (data exfiltration, system-prompt leakage) in a single pipeline.

features.title: Cheapest-first layered detection
features.description: Runs signature regex, then obfuscation heuristics, then an optional LLM judge, ordered by latency budget so fast checks gate the expensive ones.

features.title: Input and output redaction
features.description: Masks secret spans in the prompt before it ever reaches the upstream and in the completion before it returns to the caller.

features.title: Provider-agnostic adapters
features.description: Anthropic Messages and OpenAI-compatible Chat Completions wire formats are handled via a pluggable provider registry.

features.title: LLM-judge backstop
features.description: A gated Claude Haiku 4.5 classifier (escalating to Sonnet 4.6) that fires only when cheap layers find nothing, using structured JSON output and failing open.

features.title: YAML-driven rules and policy
features.description: Add a detection or change enforcement by editing YAML packs and a policy file, with no code change and per-category action overrides.

features.title: Immutable audit chain plus SIEM alerting
features.description: Every decision is appended to a JSONL audit log; non-allow enforcement events forward to a JSONL file and/or webhook sink.

features.title: Adversarial benchmark as CI gate
features.description: A labeled attack/benign corpus produces recall, FPR, precision, F1, block rate, and latency percentiles, with min-recall/max-fpr thresholds that exit non-zero on regression.

features.title: GRC reporting
features.description: Generates HTML, JSON, and CSV reports from the audit log with an executive summary and OWASP/NIST/ATLAS coverage table.

features.title: Safe-by-default enforcement
features.description: Monitor mode never blocks, inline enforcement is opt-in, and the LLM judge stays off unless enabled with an API key.

features.title: Resilient pipeline
features.description: A failing detector is caught and logged rather than allowed to break the proxy, so inspection degrades gracefully instead of dropping traffic.

ai-runtime-guardrail.architecture_summary: Traffic flows through a FastAPI reverse proxy that wraps a shared inspection Pipeline. The pipeline sorts detectors by latency budget (cheapest-first), runs each applicable detector for the request stage, aggregates their findings through a policy-driven decision engine into a single Verdict, then audits and optionally alerts on it. Provider adapters normalize per-LLM wire formats so the same detectors and redaction engine work across Anthropic and OpenAI-compatible upstreams.

architecture_layers.name: Inline proxy (core/proxy.py)
architecture_layers.description: FastAPI reverse proxy: inspects and redacts each input segment before the upstream call, enforces block/redact on non-streaming output, and passes streaming through with a tail audit.

architecture_layers.name: Inspection pipeline (core/pipeline.py)
architecture_layers.description: Orders detectors cheapest-first by LATENCY_BUDGET_MS, gates each via applies_to/should_run, wraps them in exception handling, and emits an audit record plus SIEM alert per verdict.

architecture_layers.name: Detector layers (detectors/)
architecture_layers.description: Layer 1 signature regex injection, Layer 2 obfuscation heuristics (zero-width/base64/entropy), regex DLP for PII and secrets on both stages, an output system-prompt-leak detector, and a Layer 3 Claude LLM judge.

architecture_layers.name: Decision and redaction (core/decision.py, core/redaction.py)
architecture_layers.description: Resolves the most-severe action (BLOCK > REDACT > ALERT > ALLOW) with per-category policy overrides, and applies span masking shared by the CLI and proxy.

architecture_layers.name: Provider adapters (core/providers/)
architecture_layers.description: BaseProvider ABC with anthropic and openai adapters selected through a registry, handling segment extraction, output text, refusals, and stream parsing.

architecture_layers.name: Governance and telemetry (core/compliance.py, reporter.py, alerting.py, logger.py)
architecture_layers.description: Maps categories to OWASP/NIST/ATLAS, renders HTML/JSON/CSV reports, forwards enforcement events to SIEM, and appends immutable JSONL audit records.

architecture_layers.name: Validation harness (core/benchmark.py, corpus/)
architecture_layers.description: Loads a labeled adversarial corpus and computes recall/FPR/precision/F1/latency with per-technique breakdown, usable as a CI regression gate.

Pipeline, policy, decision, redaction, proxy, config, benchmark, compliance, reporter, alerting, logger, and provider adapters.

Layer 1 prompt-injection/jailbreak signature pack (13 regex patterns with severities).
PII/secret DLP pack (11 patterns) applied on both input and output stages.
Severity-to-action thresholds and per-category enforcement overrides.
Labeled adversarial validation set: attacks.jsonl and benign.jsonl (15 each).
BaseProvider ABC, registry, and anthropic/openai wire-format adapters.
Jinja2 dark-theme report template for the GRC/executive report.
Proxy settings template: upstream provider, judge toggle, alerting, and audit paths.
Per-phase pytest suites (36 tests) covering pipeline, proxy, detectors, benchmark, and reporting.
CI: pytest on Python 3.10/3.11/3.12 plus the benchmark regression gate.

security_controls.detail: 13 case-insensitive regex patterns for known injection/jailbreak phrasing: ignore-previous-instructions, disregard-system-prompt, reveal-system-prompt, developer/DAN mode, unrestricted roleplay, safety-filter bypass, fake system prefix, and more.
Detects zero-width/invisible Unicode smuggling, base64 blobs that decode to injection phrasing or printable payloads, and Shannon-entropy checks that catch encoded attacks signatures miss.
11 DLP patterns run on both input and output: private keys, AWS access/secret keys, GCP/Slack/GitHub tokens, JWTs, bearer tokens, US SSNs, credit cards, and emails, redacted rather than hard-blocked.
Conservative output-side detector defending OWASP LLM07 with four phrasing signatures for completions that disclose the assistant's own instructions.
A gated Claude classifier that only runs when cheaper layers find nothing, uses a strict JSON schema verdict, and fails open so it can never break live traffic.
Resolves the most severe action (BLOCK > REDACT > ALERT > ALLOW) from severity thresholds, with per-category overrides winning (data_leak redact, prompt_injection block, system_prompt_leak alert).

Input BLOCK returns a provider-shaped refusal before the upstream is ever called (protecting even streaming), input REDACT masks secrets before forwarding, and non-streaming output is block/redact enforced.
Every verdict is written to an append-only JSONL audit chain that never logs matched secret values, and non-allow events forward to a JSONL file and/or SIEM webhook.

A labeled corpus spanning nine attack techniques (direct override, DAN roleplay, developer mode, encoded/zero-width smuggling, safety bypass, system-prompt exfil, fake system) enforces recall and false-positive-rate thresholds in CI.

summary: SBOM Security turns a project's lockfiles into a security-grade software bill of materials and then does the work an inventory alone can't: it correlates every component with known vulnerabilities, tracks how dependencies drift between scans, and enforces license and supply-chain policy as a CI gate. It is CycloneDX-first — the OWASP standard with native vulnerability/VEX support — and also emits SPDX 2.3, so the same scan feeds both security tooling and procurement/GRC workflows.

The design is deliberately opinionated. It reads resolved, exact versions from lockfiles rather than manifest ranges, so an SBOM records what is actually installed. Every component is assigned a spec-correct package URL (purl) — `pkg:pypi/requests@2.31.0` — which becomes the spine for OSV vulnerability lookup, cross-ecosystem drift comparison, and dedup. Parsing is pure-stdlib (no `requests`/`toml`), keeping the dependency surface of a supply-chain tool honest and its footprint minimal on Python 3.10+.

It covers four ecosystems out of the box — Python, Node, Java/Maven, and Go — each behind a pluggable `BaseParser`. Beyond generation, it queries OSV.dev by purl to attach CVEs with CVSS score, severity, and fixed-in version; snapshots a baseline so `drift` reports added/removed/upgraded/downgraded components and `audit --baseline` surfaces only newly-introduced vulnerabilities; and evaluates a YAML policy for denied/allowed SPDX licenses, banned packages, and a maximum vulnerability severity.

For security leaders it is a portable, auditable supply-chain check that drops into pre-commit and GitHub Actions, produces a combined JSON/CSV/HTML report, and rolls results up to NTIA minimum elements, CISA SBOM, OWASP SCVS, and NIST SSDF controls. Network failures fail safe — components are left un-enriched rather than reported as clean — so the gate never manufactures a false all-clear.

features.title: Dual-standard SBOM output
features.description: Generates CycloneDX 1.5 or SPDX 2.3 JSON with document metadata, per-component purl, licenses, and properties (ecosystem, direct/transitive, scope, source file).

features.title: Four-ecosystem lockfile parsing
features.description: Resolves exact versions from Python (requirements/poetry.lock/Pipfile.lock), Node (package-lock v1/v2/v3, yarn.lock), Maven (pom.xml + gradle.lockfile), and Go (go.mod/go.sum).

features.title: OSV.dev vulnerability correlation
features.description: The audit command batch-queries OSV by purl and attaches each CVE/advisory with CVSS score, severity bucket, and fixed-in version to its component.

features.title: CI severity gate
features.description: --fail-on <severity> exits non-zero when any finding meets or exceeds a threshold, turning the audit into a hard build gate.

features.title: Dependency drift & baseline
features.description: Snapshot a project with baseline, then drift reports added/removed/upgraded/downgraded components against it, with --fail-on-drift to gate CI.

features.title: New-vulnerability-only auditing
features.description: audit --baseline suppresses vulnerabilities already recorded in the baseline, so accepted risk stays quiet and only newly-introduced CVEs surface.

features.title: License & package policy engine
features.description: A YAML policy enforces allowed/denied SPDX licenses, an allow/warn/deny unknown-license mode, banned packages by name/ecosystem/version, and a max vulnerability severity — any error-level violation fails the build.

features.title: Combined GRC report
features.description: The report command writes a self-contained JSON/CSV/HTML report bundling SBOM, vulnerabilities, policy violations, and a compliance roll-up.

features.title: SPDX license normalization
features.description: Maps inconsistent license spellings (Apache License, Version 2.0 to Apache-2.0) onto SPDX IDs so policy and SPDX export are reliable; unknown values pass through untouched.

features.title: Fail-safe networking
features.description: OSV queries use a hard timeout with an injectable HTTP layer; any network error leaves components un-enriched and returns a warning rather than a false clean bill of health.

features.title: Smart project walk & dedup
features.description: Skips vendored directories (node_modules, .venv, dist, target) and dedups by (ecosystem, name, version), with a _merge step letting authoritative files enrich sparser ones.

features.title: Pre-commit & GitHub Actions ready
features.description: Ships .pre-commit-hooks.yaml (sbom-audit, sbom-policy) and a CI workflow with a 3.10-3.12 test matrix, SBOM artifact, and vulnerability gate.

sbom-security.architecture_summary: A Click CLI drives a linear pipeline: SbomGenerator walks the project tree (skipping vendored dirs), dispatches each file to a matching ecosystem BaseParser, builds spec-correct purls, and dedups/merges into an Sbom model. Downstream engines consume that model — CycloneDX/SPDX serializers, an OSV client for vulnerability correlation, version/drift/baseline for change tracking, a policy engine for gating, and a reporter/compliance layer for GRC output. purl is the shared key that ties correlation, drift, and dedup together.

architecture_layers.name: CLI (main.py)
architecture_layers.description: Click command group exposing generate, audit, check, baseline, drift, report, and list-components, with Rich tables and a startup banner.

architecture_layers.name: Parser layer (parsers/)
architecture_layers.description: BaseParser ABC with one subclass per ecosystem (python, node, maven, go); matches files by name and returns resolved Component lists from lockfiles.

architecture_layers.name: Generation engine (core/engine.py, models.py, purl.py)
architecture_layers.description: SbomGenerator walks the tree, dispatches to parsers, auto-builds purls in Component.__post_init__, and dedups/merges into a sorted Sbom.

architecture_layers.name: Serializers (core/cyclonedx.py, spdx.py, licenses.py)
architecture_layers.description: CycloneDX 1.5 and SPDX 2.3 JSON writers with metadata, per-component purl/properties, and SPDX-ID license normalization.

architecture_layers.name: Vulnerability engine (core/osv.py, cvss.py)
architecture_layers.description: OSVClient batch-queries OSV.dev by purl, fetches each unique advisory once, and parses CVSS v3.x vectors into severity-bucketed Vulnerability objects attached to components.

architecture_layers.name: Change tracking (core/version.py, drift.py, baseline.py)
architecture_layers.description: Cross-ecosystem version comparison, diff() into added/removed/upgraded/downgraded Changes, and Baseline snapshots of components plus known vuln ids.

architecture_layers.name: Policy & GRC (core/policy.py, reporter.py, compliance.py)
architecture_layers.description: YAML-driven license/package/vuln policy producing error/warn Violations, plus JSON/CSV/HTML reporting and NTIA/CISA/SCVS/SSDF compliance mapping.

security_controls.detail: Batch-queries OSV.dev by purl, fetches each unique advisory once, and attaches CVE/OSV id, CVSS v3.x base score, severity bucket, and fixed-in version to affected components.
core/cvss.py parses CVSS v3.x vectors to a base score and buckets none/low/medium/high/critical, with a text-severity fallback from OSV database_specific fields.
audit --fail-on <severity> and the policy max_severity gate exit 1 when a finding meets/exceeds the threshold, blocking merges on newly-introduced risk.
drift diffs current components against a baseline into added/removed/upgraded/downgraded changes, catching silent supply-chain shifts; --fail-on-drift gates CI.
Denied/allowed SPDX license lists plus an allow/warn/deny mode for components with no license metadata, evaluated after SPDX-ID normalization.
Policy rules match by name, optional ecosystem, and optional version to flag known-malicious or disallowed packages (e.g. event-stream on npm) as error violations.
Baselines record known vuln ids; audit --baseline suppresses previously-accepted findings (matched by id/osv_id/aliases) and reports only new ones.
OSV network/timeout errors leave components un-enriched and return a warning instead of a false clean result, preventing gates from passing on incomplete data.
Spec-correct package URLs (PEP 503 name normalization for pypi, scoped npm, group/artifact maven, slash-preserving golang) key vuln lookup, drift, and (ecosystem,name,version) dedup.
Scores NTIA minimum elements coverage and maps live status to CISA SBOM, OWASP SCVS, and NIST SSDF controls as audit evidence in the report.

summary: Secrets Scanner is a defensive appsec tool that hunts hardcoded credentials wherever they hide: across source files, config, and — critically — the full git history, where a secret introduced in an old commit still lives and remains recoverable even after a later commit "removed" it. It is positioned as the defensive mirror of the offensive T1552.001 "Credentials in Files" technique, mapping to the AccuKnox SECURING SECRETS capability: one side finds the gap, this side closes it.

Its defining principle is that secrets never leak out of the scanner. Every finding carries only a redacted preview (for example `AKIA…8N (20 chars)`) and a stable SHA-256 fingerprint — the raw value is never written to a finding, log, baseline, or report. Detection is entirely read-only and never transmits anything. Rules are data, not code: all 28 signatures live in a single YAML pack that anyone can extend or tune without touching Python, with per-rule Shannon-entropy gating to keep generic patterns from firing on values like `password123`.

It is built to live in a pipeline. A single `--fail-on <severity>` flag turns any scan into a CI build gate; a baseline snapshot suppresses a repo's existing findings so the gate breaks only on a genuinely new leak; inline pragmas and an on-by-default allowlist keep noise down; and a shipped pre-commit hook plus a GitHub Actions workflow make adoption a two-line change. Findings roll up to CWE, OWASP Top 10, PCI DSS v4.0, and NIST 800-53 controls, so a scan doubles as GRC audit evidence.

The standout differentiator is safe, opt-in live verification. On demand, the `verify` command makes a single read-only request per unique secret to that secret's own provider and reports whether the credential is actually live — turning a wall of maybe-stale findings into a ranked "rotate this one now" list. It is engineered for safety: opt-in with confirmation, provider-scoped, no redirects (so the auth header can't leak), rate-limited and timed out, failing safe to UNVERIFIED on any network error, and never logging or storing the raw value.

features.title: 28 signature rules as tunable YAML
features.description: A single `rules/secret_patterns.yaml` pack detects cloud, VCS/CI, AI/LLM, SaaS, registry, and generic credential formats — extend or tune it without writing any Python.

features.title: Redact-by-construction findings
features.description: Every finding stores only a masked preview and a SHA-256 fingerprint, so the raw secret never reaches a log, report, baseline, or terminal.

features.title: Full git-history scanning
features.description: The `history` command walks every commit on every ref and scans added lines, catching a secret at the commit that introduced it even if a later commit deleted it — with author, email, date, and message for rotation.

features.title: Safe opt-in live verification
features.description: The `verify` command makes one read-only call per unique secret to its own provider and labels it VALID, INVALID, UNVERIFIED, or SKIPPED, so teams rotate confirmed-live keys first.

features.title: Per-rule entropy gating
features.description: Noisy or generic rules only fire above a configurable Shannon-entropy floor, cutting false positives on low-entropy placeholders like `password123`.

features.title: Opt-in entropy detector
features.description: The `--entropy` mode flags high-entropy, mixed-alphabet strings that no signature matches, catching unknown or novel key formats.

features.title: Baseline triage with incremental merge
features.description: Snapshot today's findings, then surface only new secrets; `baseline --update` folds triaged findings back in (with an optional reason) so review is incremental and fingerprints stay stable across line moves.

features.title: Three-layer noise control
features.description: An on-by-default allowlist drops doc samples and template placeholders, inline `# pragma: allowlist secret` comments suppress single lines, and `--gitignore` skips git-ignored files using git's own check-ignore engine.

features.title: CI fail-gate
features.description: `--fail-on <severity>` exits non-zero on any finding at or above a threshold, and `--fail-on-valid` breaks the build when a credential is confirmed live.

features.title: Multi-format reporting
features.description: `--report` writes JSON, CSV, or a self-contained dark-themed HTML report — with an executive summary, compliance roll-up, and findings table — inferring format from the file extension and with no Jinja2 dependency.

features.title: Compliance roll-up for GRC
features.description: Each finding is mapped to CWE-798/321/522, OWASP Top 10 (2021), PCI DSS v4.0, and NIST 800-53 controls, turning a scan into audit evidence.

features.title: Turnkey pre-commit and Actions integration
features.description: A shipped `.pre-commit-hooks.yaml` (id: secrets-scan) blocks commits introducing HIGH+ secrets, and the repo's own CI runs a self-scan gate across Python 3.10–3.12.

secrets-scanner.architecture_summary: A small stdlib-first Python package built around a data pipeline: walk_files streams candidate files, a list of pluggable detectors emit redacted Findings, results are deduped by fingerprint and filtered through an optional baseline, then rendered as table/JSON or written as a report — with an optional fail-on gate. The history command swaps the file walk for a git-log diff walk that reconstructs each commit's added lines, and the verify command re-recovers raw secrets in-memory to make read-only provider calls. Detection rules and allowlist live entirely in YAML, so breadth grows without code changes.

architecture_layers.name: Walker (core/walker.py)
architecture_layers.description: Streams scannable files from a tree, skipping binaries (null-byte sniff + extension list), files over 5 MB, and noise dirs like .git, node_modules, venv, dist, and .terraform.

architecture_layers.name: Detectors (detectors/)
architecture_layers.description: A BaseDetector interface with two implementations: SignatureDetector (compiled YAML regex rules with capture groups, entropy gates, and verifier hints) and the opt-in EntropyDetector for novel high-entropy strings.

architecture_layers.name: Scan engine (core/engine.py)
architecture_layers.description: SecretScanner runs the detector set over each file's full content (so multi-line PEM keys match), applies inline-pragma and gitignore filtering, dedups by fingerprint, and drops baseline-suppressed findings into a ScanResult.

architecture_layers.name: Git-history engine (core/git_history.py)
architecture_layers.description: GitHistoryScanner replaces the file walk with a git log --all -p diff walk, reconstructing added lines per commit with a line map for accurate line numbers, and attributes each finding to the introducing commit.

architecture_layers.name: Live verification (core/verify.py)
architecture_layers.description: Verifier/Runner classes recover the raw secret in-memory from a finding's line and regex, then make a single no-redirect, timed, rate-limited read-only request per unique secret to its own provider (GitHub, GitLab, OpenAI, Anthropic, Stripe, SendGrid).

architecture_layers.name: Models & safety core (core/models.py)
architecture_layers.description: Severity/Verification enums, the Finding and ScanResult dataclasses, redact() masking, and the SHA-256 fingerprint() that powers dedup and baseline suppression without storing raw secrets.

architecture_layers.name: Triage & suppression (core/baseline, allowlist, pragma, gitignore)
architecture_layers.description: Baseline load/write/suppress with incremental --update, value/path allowlist from the rule pack, inline pragma line suppression, and git check-ignore-based filtering.

architecture_layers.name: Reporting & compliance (core/reporter.py, core/compliance.py)
architecture_layers.description: Dependency-free JSON/CSV/HTML report writers with redacted previews, plus a mapper that ties each rule to CWE/OWASP/PCI-DSS/NIST controls and rolls them up per scan.

project_structure.description: Click CLI exposing the scan, history, verify, and baseline commands (also the secrets-scan console script).
SecretScanner and default_detectors() — the filesystem scan pipeline.
GitHistoryScanner — scans added lines across every commit on every ref.
Safe opt-in live verification: no-redirect HTTP, per-provider verifiers, in-memory raw-secret recovery.
Severity/Verification enums, Finding/ScanResult dataclasses, redact() and fingerprint() helpers.
JSON/CSV/self-contained HTML report writers (no Jinja2), previews stay redacted.
Maps each rule to CWE-798/321/522, OWASP 2021, PCI DSS v4.0, and NIST 800-53 controls.
The triage/suppression layer: baseline merge, allowlist, inline pragmas, and gitignore-aware filtering.
SignatureDetector — loads and compiles the YAML rule pack, applies capture/entropy/allowlist logic.
EntropyDetector plus shannon_entropy() — opt-in detection of novel high-entropy strings.
The 28 signature rules plus the allowlist section — the single source of detection truth.
51 pytest tests across scanner, git_history, verify, reporter, and compliance; git/network isolated.

security_controls.detail: AWS Access Key ID (critical) and Secret Access Key, Azure Storage account key, and GCP API key, service-account JSON marker, and OAuth client secret.
GitHub classic PAT, fine-grained PAT, and OAuth/app/refresh/server tokens, plus GitLab PAT — all critical severity.
OpenAI API key (entropy-gated at 3.5) and Anthropic API key, both critical and both live-verifiable.
Stripe secret key, Slack token + incoming webhook, SendGrid, Twilio, Mailgun, Telegram bot, Shopify, and DigitalOcean tokens.
npm access tokens and PyPI upload tokens, both critical.
PEM private-key blocks (RSA/EC/OPENSSH/DSA/PGP), JSON Web Tokens, private-key variable assignments, and database connection URIs with inline credentials.
Generic api_key/secret/token/password assignments gated by a 2.5 Shannon-entropy floor to suppress low-entropy placeholders.
Opt-in detector flags high-entropy (>=4.0), mixed-alphabet strings of 24+ chars that no signature matches, catching unknown formats.
Read-only provider checks for GitHub, GitLab, OpenAI, Anthropic, Stripe, and SendGrid classify a secret as VALID/INVALID/UNVERIFIED/SKIPPED, failing safe on any network error.
History scanning surfaces secrets that were committed then deleted but remain recoverable in git, attributed to the introducing commit and author for rotation.

summary: Active Directory Security Assessment is a read-only, Python-based auditor for Microsoft AD environments. It connects over LDAP/LDAPS (and optionally WinRM) and runs 86 defensive checks across 15 categories - Kerberos attack surface, privilege escalation, AD CS certificate misconfigurations, password policy, trust relationships, authentication weaknesses, and more - then produces an interactive HTML report and machine-readable JSON with a 0-100 posture score and letter grade.

The design philosophy is deliberately non-invasive: every AD query is an LDAP search operation and every remote command is a PowerShell Get-* cmdlet, so the scanner never modifies a single object. That makes it safe to run against production domain controllers by a Domain Users account (Domain Admins unlocks full coverage), and safe to wire into CI/CD - the process exits 0 when clean, 1 on HIGH findings, and 2 on CRITICAL.

Coverage is split across two data sources. Roughly 70% of checks are answered directly from LDAP/LDAPS (Kerberoasting and AS-REP roasting candidates, delegation abuse, krbtgt age, AD CS ESC templates, privileged-group membership, trusts), while about 30% require an optional WinRM session for host-side registry and audit-policy settings (WDigest, LM hash storage, SMB signing, Credential Guard, LAPS, NTLM restriction). WinRM-dependent checks degrade gracefully to INFO notifications when the session is unavailable, so an LDAP-only run still returns actionable results.

It is aimed at blue teams, AD administrators, and internal auditors who need a repeatable, evidence-backed snapshot of domain security posture. Every finding ships with a severity, the observed-versus-expected state, a plain-English recommendation, and a copy-paste PowerShell remediation command (display only, never executed), turning a scan into a prioritized, hardening to-do list.

features.title: 86 read-only AD security checks
features.description: Audits 86 distinct misconfigurations across 15 attack-relevant categories using only LDAP search and PowerShell Get-* commands, never mutating the directory.

features.title: Kerberos attack-surface analysis
features.description: Flags Kerberoastable and AS-REP-roastable accounts, unconstrained/constrained/RBCD delegation, DES-only encryption, stale krbtgt passwords, and an empty Protected Users group.

features.title: AD CS (ESC) certificate abuse detection
features.description: Inspects certificate templates and enrollment services for ESC1 (SAN + client auth), ESC2 (Any Purpose EKU), ESC6 (EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2), and ESC8 (HTTP enrollment/NTLM relay).

features.title: Privilege-escalation and DCSync mapping
features.description: Enumerates Domain/Enterprise/Schema Admin membership, stale and orphaned adminCount accounts, dangerous ACLs, AdminSDHolder tampering, and non-standard principals holding DCSync replication rights.

features.title: Posture scoring with letter grades
features.description: Distills all findings into a single 0-100 score via severity-weighted penalties (CRIT x15, HIGH x5, MED x2, LOW x0.5) mapped to an A-F grade for at-a-glance reporting.

features.title: Self-contained interactive HTML report
features.description: Emits a single dark-theme HTML file with an animated score ring, severity cards, and live client-side search plus severity/category filters - no external assets or internet required.

features.title: Dual LDAP + WinRM data collection
features.description: Combines directory-level LDAP/LDAPS evidence with optional host-side WinRM registry and audit-policy checks, gracefully skipping WinRM checks with INFO notices when unavailable.

features.title: PowerShell remediation for every finding
features.description: Each finding carries a ready-to-run (display-only) remediation command such as Set-ADAccountControl or Reset-ADServiceAccountPassword, so operators know exactly how to fix it.

features.title: CI/CD-friendly exit codes
features.description: Returns exit 0 (clean), 1 (HIGH findings), or 2 (CRITICAL findings) so scans can gate pipelines or trigger alerts automatically.

features.title: Selective scans and YAML profiles
features.description: Run all 15 categories or a comma-separated subset via --checks, or drive enablement from a reusable default_profile.yaml scan profile.

features.title: Flexible authentication and transport
features.description: Supports simple or NTLM binds, plaintext LDAP (389) or encrypted LDAPS (636), optional SSL verification skip, and configurable ports/timeouts.

features.title: Offline-testable check modules
features.description: Each checker is independently unit-tested against mock LDAP responses, so 28 tests validate detection logic with no live domain controller.

active-directory-security-assessment.architecture_summary: A single CLI entry point orchestrates a linear connect -> discover -> assess -> score -> report pipeline. The scanner authenticates a read-only LDAP/LDAPS client and an optional WinRM client, then iterates a registry of 15 pluggable checker classes (ALL_CHECKERS) that each return a list of finding dicts; findings are aggregated, scored by a severity-weighted posture model, and rendered to self-contained HTML and JSON.

architecture_layers.name: CLI orchestrator (ad_security_scanner.py)
architecture_layers.description: Argparse-driven entry point that loads the profile, resolves enabled categories, wires up clients, runs each checker, prints the console summary, and sets severity-based exit codes.

architecture_layers.name: LDAP client (utils/ldap_client.py)
architecture_layers.description: Read-only ldap3 wrapper that auto-discovers the base DN from RootDSE and exposes typed getters (get_users, get_groups, get_trusts, get_certificate_templates, get_fine_grained_password_policies, etc.).

architecture_layers.name: WinRM client (utils/winrm_client.py)
architecture_layers.description: Optional pywinrm client running only Get-* PowerShell for host-side settings (audit policy, SMB config, WDigest, LM hash, Credential Guard, LAPS, NTLM) with graceful connect-failure fallback.

architecture_layers.name: Check modules (checks/*.py)
architecture_layers.description: 15 independent checker classes registered in ALL_CHECKERS; each run_all() invokes private _check_ methods that emit structured findings with id, severity, actual/expected, recommendation, and remediation_cmd.

architecture_layers.name: Severity & scoring engine (utils/severity.py)
architecture_layers.description: Defines severity ordering and weights, computes the clamped 0-100 posture score, converts it to an A-F grade, and tallies severity counts.

architecture_layers.name: Report generator (utils/report_generator.py)
architecture_layers.description: Renders findings into a self-contained dark-theme HTML report (score ring, severity cards, live search/filter JS) and a structured JSON report with metadata and severity summary.

project_structure.description: Main CLI entry point: argument parsing, client setup, checker orchestration, console summary, and exit codes.
15 check modules (domain_config, kerberos_security, password_policy, privileged_accounts, certificate_services, ...) plus __init__.py exposing the ALL_CHECKERS registry.
Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, delegation (unconstrained/constrained/RBCD), DES accounts, krbtgt age, Protected Users checks.
AD CS auditing for ESC1/ESC2/ESC6/ESC8 template and CA misconfigurations.
Read-only LDAP/LDAPS connection handler with RootDSE base-DN discovery and object getters.
Optional WinRM client issuing only Get-* PowerShell for registry/audit-policy checks.
Self-contained HTML and JSON report generation with posture score, filters, and remediation.
Severity weights, posture-score computation, and A-F grade mapping.
Default scan profile enabling all 15 check categories with connection settings.
28 unit tests exercising checker logic against mock LDAP responses (no live AD required).
Mock LDAP data fixtures backing the offline unit tests.
Runtime dependencies: ldap3, pywinrm, pyyaml, jinja2.

security_controls.detail: Detects Kerberoastable users and admins (SPN-bearing accounts), AS-REP roasting (DONT_REQUIRE_PREAUTH), unconstrained/constrained/RBCD delegation, DES-only encryption, krbtgt passwords older than 180 days, and empty Protected Users group.

Audits certificate templates and enrollment services for ESC1 (enrollee-supplied SAN + client auth), ESC2 (Any Purpose EKU), ESC6 (EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2 on the CA), and ESC8 (HTTP/NTLM-relay enrollment).
Reviews Domain/Enterprise/Schema Admin and built-in Administrator membership, nested and dangerous groups, stale admins, orphaned adminCount, and Kerberoastable privileged accounts.

Eleven checks covering minimum length, complexity, lockout threshold, history, max age, reversible encryption, fine-grained password policies, and password-never-expires/stale-password accounts (min-length advice aligned to CIS 14+).
Flags NTLMv1/LM hash storage, WDigest credential caching, missing SMB signing, SMBv1 on DCs, and unrestricted/unaudited NTLM - primarily via WinRM registry inspection.

Identifies non-standard principals with DCSync rights, DFSR/FRS SYSVOL replication and health issues, Pre-Windows 2000 Compatible Access membership, dangerous domain-object ACLs, and AdminSDHolder tampering.
Checks LDAP signing, channel binding, anonymous bind and LDAPS enforcement, plus DNS zone transfers, insecure dynamic updates, scavenging, and WPAD block-list hardening.
Evaluates SID filtering, selective authentication and bidirectional trusts, alongside domain functional level, AD Recycle Bin, machine account quota, DC OS versions, and stale DCs.
Covers audit policy, PowerShell/command-line logging, Credential Guard, LAPS deployment, RDP/Restricted Admin and Remote Credential Guard restrictions, event-log sizing, and unlinked GPOs.

All directory access uses LDAP search operations and all remote execution uses PowerShell Get-* cmdlets only - no Set-/New-/Remove-, so scans never alter AD; remediation commands are surfaced for the operator but never run.
