summary: Alpha-AI is an agentic offensive-security framework that turns an LLM into a hands-on operator. It exposes a curated set of Kali penetration-testing binaries to a Claude/GPT/Copilot agent over two parallel surfaces — a FastMCP server (stdio transport, for Claude Desktop/Code) and a FastAPI REST API — so the same tool wrapper is written once and reachable from either. The agent can then chain reconnaissance → vulnerability discovery → exploitation → reporting against explicitly authorized targets, with every invocation normalized into structured Finding objects.

The design principle that makes it interesting to a security leader is that it is authorization-first. A YAML whitelist (literal hosts, CIDR ranges, and glob patterns) is enforced by a central dispatcher before any remote tool touches a host; a target that isn't listed raises UnauthorizedTargetError, which the REST layer maps to HTTP 403. Only local-only lookups such as searchsploit opt out. A single tool registry is the source of truth for both surfaces, a disk cache keyed on sha256(tool, target, args) avoids re-running expensive scans, and structlog records tool name, target, argv, return code, and duration for every run.

This is deliberately an early-stage MVP (v0.1.0). It ships 9 tool wrappers across five categories — nmap, nuclei, gobuster, ffuf, sqlmap, enum4linux, crackmapexec, hydra, and searchsploit — each backed by a pure, unit-tested parser that converts raw tool output into severity-rated findings. It is built for red-teamers and AI-security researchers who want to experiment with autonomous, tool-using pentest agents inside a controlled Kali container, with a documented pattern for adding tool #10 in five files. Roadmap phases target broader tool breadth, an LLM autopilot loop, Markdown/HTML reporting, and MITRE ATT&CK mapping.

features.title: Dual MCP + REST exposure
features.description: The same tool set is reachable both as MCP tools (FastMCP, stdio) for Claude Desktop/Code and as JSON REST endpoints (FastAPI on :8000), written once and shared.

features.title: Authorization-first execution
features.description: A YAML target whitelist supporting literals, CIDRs, and globs blocks any tool from running against a host that hasn't been explicitly authorized.

features.title: Single tool registry
features.description: Every wrapper self-registers with one ToolRegistry, so MCP shims and REST routes build their surfaces from the same source of truth with no duplicated logic.

features.title: Central dispatcher
features.description: One Dispatcher enforces authorization, cache lookup, tool invocation, and cache write for both surfaces, so security policy lives in exactly one place.

features.title: Result caching
features.description: A disk-backed JSON cache keyed on sha256(tool, target, args) skips re-running expensive scans, with a use_cache=False override to force a fresh run.

features.title: Async subprocess runner
features.description: CommandRunner executes Kali binaries asynchronously with per-tool timeouts, PATH validation via which(), timeout-kill handling, and structured exec/done logging.

features.title: Pure, testable parsers
features.description: Each tool has an I/O-free parse_* function that turns raw XML/JSON/text into normalized Finding objects with severities, evidence, references, and CVEs.

features.title: Severity-rated findings
features.description: Parsers apply per-tool severity policy — sqlmap/hydra findings are always CRITICAL, crackmapexec Pwn3d! is CRITICAL, gobuster sensitive paths MEDIUM — for consistent triage.

features.title: Structured audit logging
features.description: structlog records tool name, target, argv, return code, and duration for every invocation, giving a machine-readable trail of agent activity.

features.title: Kali container runtime
features.description: A Dockerfile on the kalilinux/kali-rolling base preinstalls the tool binaries and ships alpha-mcp / alpha-api entrypoints for reproducible deployment.

features.title: Documented extension pattern
features.description: CLAUDE.md specifies the exact five-file recipe (parser, wrapper, registry import, MCP shim, REST route, plus a parser test) to add a new tool.

features.title: Threat-intel data models
features.description: An included threat_intel module defines IP/domain/file-reputation and MITRE ATT&CK mapping models (currently mock-backed) as scaffolding for future enrichment.

alpha-ai.architecture_summary: Alpha-AI is a layered Python framework. An LLM agent calls in over one of two surfaces — FastMCP (stdio) or FastAPI (HTTP) — both of which delegate to a shared Dispatcher. The dispatcher consults the tool registry, enforces the target-authorization whitelist, checks the result cache, then invokes the tool wrapper, which builds an argv, runs it through the async CommandRunner, and hands stdout to a pure parser that emits normalized, severity-rated Finding objects packaged in a ToolResult.

architecture_layers.name: Exposure surfaces (servers/)
architecture_layers.description: mcp_server.py (FastMCP @mcp.tool shims) and rest_api.py (FastAPI POST /tools/<name> routes) present identical capabilities to LLM agents.

architecture_layers.name: Dispatcher
architecture_layers.description: servers/dispatcher.py centralizes the request flow: registry lookup → authorization gate → cache get → tool fn → cache put → ToolResult.

architecture_layers.name: Tool registry
architecture_layers.description: core/registry.py holds ToolSpec metadata (name, category, description, parameters, requires_authorization) and load_builtin_tools() imports the 9 wrappers so they self-register.

architecture_layers.name: Core services
architecture_layers.description: core/auth.py (TargetAuthorizer whitelist), core/cache.py (sha256 JSON cache), core/runner.py (async subprocess + timeout), and core/logging.py (structlog) provide cross-cutting infrastructure.

architecture_layers.name: Tool wrappers (tools/)
architecture_layers.description: Thin per-tool modules under recon/web/network/cred/exploit that build argv, call the runner, and delegate parsing — no business logic.

architecture_layers.name: Parsers (parsers/)
architecture_layers.description: Pure functions (parse_nmap_xml, parse_nuclei_jsonl, parse_sqlmap, …) that convert raw tool output into list[Finding] with severity, evidence, references, and CVEs.

architecture_layers.name: Models (core/models.py)
architecture_layers.description: Pydantic v2 types — Severity, ToolCategory, Target, Finding, CommandResult, ToolResult — that normalize output across every tool.

architecture_layers.name: Threat intel (threat_intel/)
architecture_layers.description: Standalone reputation and MITRE ATT&CK mapping models with mock lookup sources; scaffolding not yet wired into the MCP/REST surfaces.

project_structure.description: Framework core: registry, dispatcher-consumed auth, cache, async runner, pydantic models, structlog setup.
Tool wrappers grouped by category: recon/, web/, network/, cred/, exploit/ — one file per binary.
Nine pure output parsers (nmap, nuclei, gobuster, ffuf, sqlmap, enum4linux, crackmapexec, hydra, searchsploit) returning normalized findings.
The two exposure surfaces (mcp_server.py, rest_api.py) plus the shared dispatcher.py.
Threat-intel models and mock IP/domain/hash/MITRE lookup sources (present, not yet exposed).
Template authorization whitelist — copy to targets.yaml and add literals, CIDRs, or globs.
Pytest suite across auth, dispatcher, registry, runner, and three parser test files.
kalilinux/kali-rolling image that installs the tool binaries and pip-installs the package with alpha-mcp/alpha-api entrypoints.
Package metadata, dependency pins (fastmcp/fastapi/uvicorn/pydantic/structlog/pyyaml), console scripts, pytest and ruff config.
Engineering guide: architecture, request flow, tool inventory with severity policy, the add-a-tool recipe, and roadmap.
Top-level overview with architecture diagram, component table, runtime instructions, and tool status matrix.

security_controls.detail: TargetAuthorizer loads a YAML whitelist and matches literals, CIDR networks, and fnmatch globs; require() raises UnauthorizedTargetError (a PermissionError subclass) that the REST API maps to HTTP 403. Enforced in the dispatcher before any remote tool runs.
Port scanning and service detection (-Pn -T<timing> -p <ports> -sV -oX -); XML parsed to open-port findings, one INFO finding per open port.
Template-based vulnerability scanning; JSONL parsed with nuclei severity mapped 1:1 to Alpha-AI Severity and classification.cve-id captured onto findings.
Directory and content brute-forcing; discovered paths rated by policy — sensitive paths (admin, backup, .git, .env, config, wp-admin, phpmyadmin) flagged MEDIUM, other 200s LOW.
Automated SQL-injection detection in batch mode with level/risk/technique controls; any confirmed injection point is emitted as CRITICAL.
SMB/Samba enumeration of shares, users, and OS banner; disk shares MEDIUM, users LOW, OS INFO.
Multi-protocol (smb/…) network and AD checks; a Pwn3d! success line is CRITICAL, other [+] successes HIGH.
Login brute-forcing across SSH/FTP/SMB/RDP/HTTP/MySQL/Postgres with stop-on-first support; any valid credential found is CRITICAL.
Local Exploit-DB lookup (requires_authorization=False, the only opt-out); severity by exploit type — remote/webapps HIGH, local/dos MEDIUM — with exploit-db.com references.

Async CommandRunner validates binaries with which() (raising ToolNotFoundError), enforces per-tool timeouts with kill-on-timeout, never raises on non-zero exit, and logs argv/return code/duration via structlog for every run.

usage: docker build -t alpha-ai .   # build the Kali container with all tool binaries
docker run -it --rm alpha-ai alpha-mcp   # run the MCP server over stdio for Claude Desktop/Code
docker run -it --rm -p 8000:8000 alpha-ai alpha-api   # run the REST API on :8000
curl -X POST http://localhost:8000/tools/nmap -H 'content-type: application/json' -d '{"target":"127.0.0.1","ports":"22,80,443","timing":4}'
curl http://localhost:8000/tools   # list all registered tools (also GET /health)
python -m pytest tests/test_parsers.py tests/test_auth.py tests/test_registry.py tests/test_dispatcher.py -q   # run the parser/auth/registry tests on a non-Linux host
